title: Deprecation cleanup: remove pickle.loads() from pydantic/deprecated/parse.py
body:
## Summary

`pydantic/deprecated/parse.py` contains a `pickle.loads()` call that fires when `allow_pickle=True` is passed to the deprecated `load_str_bytes()` / `load_file()` functions. The functions are correctly marked `@deprecated`, but the unsafe pickle path is still reachable and presents an unnecessary security risk for any caller that sets `allow_pickle=True` on untrusted input.

## Details

**File:** `pydantic/deprecated/parse.py:55`

```python
elif proto == Protocol.pickle:
    if not allow_pickle:
        raise RuntimeError('Trying to decode with pickle with allow_pickle=False')
    bb = b if isinstance(b, bytes) else b.encode()
    return pickle.loads(bb)  # ← unsafe on untrusted input
```

`load_file()` (line 76) auto-selects `Protocol.pickle` when the file extension is `.pkl`, regardless of `allow_pickle`. Any caller that passes `allow_pickle=True` and reads from an attacker-influenced file path achieves arbitrary code execution via `pickle.loads()`.

This is especially relevant in AI data pipeline contexts (LangChain, Pydantic AI, FastAPI) where pydantic is used as a data-model layer and `.pkl` files are common for cached artifacts.

## Suggested fix

Remove the `pickle.loads()` branch entirely from `deprecated/parse.py`. There is no valid migration path from v1 that _requires_ pydantic to deserialize pickle — callers who need pickle can call `pickle.loads()` directly, which at least makes the risk explicit and searchable.

If complete removal is not possible before the next major version, add a hard `DeprecationError` (not just a warning) before the `pickle.loads()` call so it is impossible to call silently:

```python
elif proto == Protocol.pickle:
    raise PydanticDeprecatedSince20(
        "pickle deserialization via pydantic is removed. Use pickle.loads() directly."
    )
```

## Impact

- **CWE-502** — Deserialization of Untrusted Data
- Callers using `allow_pickle=True` with untrusted input (e.g., files from a shared filesystem or network path)

title: `__pydantic_init_subclass__` cannot receive custom class keyword arguments without also defining `__init_subclass__`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

**Description**

The docs for `__pydantic_init_subclass__` state:

> "This will receive the same `kwargs` that would be passed to the standard `__init_subclass__`"

Which I understood to mean I could use `__pydantic_init_subclass__` as a drop-in replacement for `__init_subclass__`.

But in practice, custom kwargs passed at class definition time (e.g. `class Foo(Bar, my_kwarg='value')`) cause a `TypeError`.

**Root cause**

`ModelMetaclass.__new__` passes the full `kwargs` to `super().__new__(mcs, cls_name, bases, namespace, **kwargs)`. This causes Python to invoke `__init_subclass__` on the parent chain with those kwargs before Pydantic calls `__pydantic_init_subclass__`. Since nothing in the MRO between `Event` and `object` consumes `event_type`, `object.__init_subclass__` raises.

**Workaround**

Define a no-op `__init_subclass__` alongside `__pydantic_init_subclass__` to absorb the kwargs:

```python
@classmethod
def __init_subclass__(cls, my_kwarg: str | None = None, **kwargs: object) -> None:
    super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)
```
but that becomes it's own problem if you're trying to satisfy a strict type-checker with the type of kwargs (no Any).

**Expected behavior**

Ideally, overriding `__pydantic_init_subclass__` with custom kwargs should work without also requiring an `__init_subclass__` override.  Or it's my reading comprehension... Maybe the doc should warn that the hook is not meant to be a drop-in replacement and that custom kwargs will require a `__init_subclass__` swallowing those args.


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import override

class Event(BaseModel):
    e_type: str

    # @classmethod
    # def __init_subclass__(cls, event_type: str | None = None, **kwargs: object) -> None:
    #     super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)

    @override
    @classmethod
    def __pydantic_init_subclass__(cls, event_type: str | None = N

title: `@validate_call` on list and dict parameters causing `id` changes
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, validate_call

class TestModel(BaseModel):
    a: int
    b: str

def without_validate(dic: dict, lst: list, obj: TestModel):
    print(f'without_validate: {id(dic)=} {id(lst)=} {id(obj)=}')

    dic.pop('a')
    lst.pop(0)
    obj.a = 2

@validate_call
def with_validate(dic: dict, lst: list, obj: TestModel):
    print(f'with_validate: {id(dic)=} {id(lst)=} {id(obj)=}')
    without_validate(dic, lst, obj)

def test(func):
    dic = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
    lst = [1, 2]
    obj = TestModel(a=1, b='2')

    print(f'initial: {id(dic)=} {id(lst)=} {id(obj)=}')

    func(dic, lst, obj)

    print(f'after: {dic=} {lst=} {obj=}')

test(without_validate)
test(with_validate)
```

Output:

```
initial:          id(dic)=1674935373504 id(lst)=1674935060608 id(obj)=1674925117504
without_validate: id(dic)=1674935373504 id(lst)=1674935060608 id(obj)=1674925117504
after:            dic={'b': 2}          lst=[2]               obj=TestModel(a=2, b='2')

initial:          id(dic)=1674948086464 id(lst)=1674935060608 id(obj)=1674925117504
with_validate:    id(dic)=1674936360448 id(lst)=1674928693824 id(obj)=1674925117504
without_validate: id(dic)=1674936360448 id(lst)=1674928693824 id(obj)=1674925117504
after:            dic={'a': 1, 'b': 2}  lst=[1, 2]            obj=TestModel(a=2, b='2')  # elements not removed!
```

When the function is decorated with `@validate_call`, the `id` of list and dict is different between inside and outside, causing functions that need modifying them not working correctly.

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
             pydantic version: 2.13.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.46.4

title: `experimental.pipeline`: nested `|` and `&` operators raise `AttributeError` during schema generation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When composing `pydantic.experimental.pipeline` validators using nested `|` and `&` operators, model construction fails during schema generation with:
```
AttributeError: '_Pipeline' object has no attribute '__dict__'
```
I would expect nested pipeline compositions to either:
* generate a valid schema and validate successfully, or
* raise a user-facing error indicating that the composition is unsupported.

Instead, schema generation crashes with an internal AttributeError.

I would be interested in contributing a fix if maintainers agree this is a bug.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.experimental.pipeline import validate_as


class TmpBaseModel(BaseModel):
    some_int: Annotated[int, (validate_as(int) | validate_as(int)) & validate_as(int)]


data = {"some_int": 42}
val = TmpBaseModel.model_validate(data)
assert val.some_int == 42
```

Environment Info:

- Pydantic version: 2.14.0a1
- Python:  3.11.13

title: Segfault stack overflow when a PEP 695 type alias resolves to a self-referential model with no Union/Optional base case.
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

A pydantic BaseModel with unbounded recursion on a `type` alias causes a segmentation fault/stack overflow that aborts the interpreter.

The error occurs across various different Python and Pydantic versions. All of the below are affected:

|Python|Pydantic|Pydantic Core|
|----------|-------------|--------------------|
|3.14.5|2.13.4|2.46.4|
|3.13.5|2.13.4|2.46.4|
|3.13.5|2.12.5|2.41.5|

On Python 3.13, the below script just fails without any output. The exit code is 139.
On Python 3.14, an informative error message is produced. The exit code is 134.

```
Fatal Python error: _Py_CheckRecursiveCall: Unrecoverable stack overflow (used 8169 kB) while calling a Python object
Python runtime state: initialized

Current thread 0x00007fbfe553ebc0 [python] (most recent call first):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.14/site-packages/pydantic/plugin/_schema_validator.py", line 53 in create_schema_validator
  File "/usr/lib/python3.14/site-packages/pydantic/_internal/_model_construction.py", line 690 in complete_model_class

[1]    66457 abort (core dumped)  python mre.py
```

I agree that the user code in the MRE is itself ill-formed and does not represent anything anyone should use in production. However, I firmly believe that regardless of any input, a segmentation fault should not be produced.

title: 'fraction_validator' raises unhandled 'ZeroDivisionError' for zero-denominator string input instead of 'ValidationError'
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

**Bug description**

The `fraction_validator` in `pydantic/_internal/_validators.py` only catches `ValueError` when constructing a `fractions.Fraction` from user input. However, Python's standard `fractions.Fraction` raises `ZeroDivisionError` when the denominator is zero (e.g., the string `"6/0"`). Because this exception is not caught, it bubbles up as a raw Python exception instead of being converted into a `ValidationError`, violating Pydantic's contract that all invalid user input should result in a `ValidationError`.

This was discovered during fuzzing of `TypeAdapter.validate_strings()` with the Atheris fuzzer.

**Expected behavior**

A `pydantic.ValidationError` should be raised with a message indicating that the input is not a valid fraction.

**Actual behavior**

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../pydantic/_internal/_validators.py", line 253, in fraction_validator
    return Fraction(input_value)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../fractions.py", line 281, in __new__
    raise ZeroDivisionError('Fraction(%s, 0)' % numerator)
ZeroDivisionError: Fraction(6, 0)
```

**Root cause**

In `pydantic/_internal/_validators.py`, line 247-254:

```python
def fraction_validator(input_value: Any, /) -> Fraction:
    if isinstance(input_value, Fraction):
        return input_value

    try:
        return Fraction(input_value)
    except ValueError:
        raise PydanticCustomError('fraction_parsing', 'Input is not a valid fraction')
```

The `except` clause only catches `ValueError`, but `Fraction("6/0")` raises `ZeroDivisionError`.

**Proposed fix**

Add `ZeroDivisionError` to the exception tuple:

```diff
--- a/pydantic/_internal/_validators.py
+++ b/pydantic/_internal/_validators.py
@@ -250,7 +250,7 @@ def fraction_validator(input_value: Any, /) -> Fraction:
 
     try:
         return Fraction(input_value)
-    except ValueError:
+    except (ValueError, Z

@dataclass(slots=True)
class DerivedDC(BaseDC):

title: StrEnum instances no longer normalised to str instances when used as dict keys in dynamic model creation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In 2.12.5, if using a `StrEnum` instance as the key of a dict being passed to `create_model`, that instance would be normalised to a `str` instance before being set as the field name on the dynamically created model. In 2.13.4, it remains as a `StrEnum` instance. 

For example: 
- Create a `main.py`:
```python
import pydantic, enum

class MyStrEnum(enum.StrEnum):
    sender = 'sender'

inner = pydantic.create_model('Inner', x=(str, pydantic.Field(default=None)))
Model = pydantic.create_model('M', **{MyStrEnum.sender: (inner, pydantic.Field(default=None))})

try:
    Model.model_validate({'sender': {'x': 123}})
except pydantic.ValidationError as e:
    loc0 = e.errors()[0]['loc'][0]
    print(f'loc[0]={loc0!r}  type={type(loc0).__name__}  is str={type(loc0) is str}')
```
- Install pydantic 2.12.5 and run the file
```bash
uv add pydantic==2.12.5
uv run main.py
```
- Observe the following output
```
loc[0]='sender'  type=str  is str=True
```
- Now install 2.13.4 and run the file
```bash
uv add pydantic==2.13.4
uv run main.py
```
- Observe the following output
```
loc[0]=<MyStrEnum.sender: 'sender'>  type=MyStrEnum  is str=False
```

```Python

```

title: BaseModel-like API for top-level discriminated unions
body:
### Description

This is related to #10409, especially the later comments asking whether `TypeAdapter` can behave more like a `BaseModel`.

Using the docs example:

```py
from typing import Annotated, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, RootModel, TypeAdapter


class Cat(BaseModel):
    pet_type: Literal["cat"]
    meows: int


class Dog(BaseModel):
    pet_type: Literal["dog"]
    barks: float


class Lizard(BaseModel):
    pet_type: Literal["reptile", "lizard"]
    scales: bool


type Pet = Annotated[
    Cat | Dog | Lizard,
    Field(discriminator="pet_type"),
]
```

For a nested field, the documented pattern works well:

```py
class Model(BaseModel):
    pet: Pet
```

But for a flat top-level object, the available options all have tradeoffs:

```py
pet = TypeAdapter(Pet).validate_python({"pet_type": "cat", "meows": 3})
pet.pet_type  # works
pet.model_dump()  # works
```

But `TypeAdapter` itself is not model-like:

```py
TypeAdapter(Pet).model_validate(...)  # not available
```

`RootModel` gives a model-like API, but wraps the actual object under `.root`:

```py
class PetModel(RootModel[Pet]):
    pass

pet = PetModel.model_validate({"pet_type": "cat", "meows": 3})
pet.root.pet_type  # works
pet.pet_type       # does not
```

Is there, or should there be, a Pydantic-native way to define a top-level discriminated union that:

- preserves the flat input/output shape
- selects the correct concrete model using the discriminator
- is constructible/validatable like a `BaseModel`
- exposes the selected model's fields directly, without `.root`

In other words, something with the ergonomics of `TypeAdapter(Pet).validate_python(...)`, but with a first-class model-style API.

title: __slots__ is not propagated to generic parameterizations, silently re-adding __weakref__
body:
## Description

`BaseModel.__class_getitem__` synthesizes a new class for each parameterization (`Model[X]`) via `pydantic/_internal/_generics.py::create_generic_submodel`. That function builds the class with a namespace containing only `__module__` (plus what `prepare_class` adds) and never forwards `__slots__` from `origin`. Because the new class body has no `__slots__`, Python's class machinery adds the default `__dict__` and `__weakref__` descriptors. The parent's `__slots__ = ()` is silently discarded for every parameterization.

## Reproducer

```python
import weakref
import pydantic

class Envelope[T](pydantic.BaseModel):
    __slots__ = ()
    content: None | T = None

base = Envelope()
print("base __weakref__:", "__weakref__" in type(base).__dict__)       # False - good
weakref.ref(base)                                                       # TypeError - good

param = Envelope[int]()
print("param __weakref__:", "__weakref__" in type(param).__dict__)     # True  - unexpected
weakref.ref(param)                                                      # succeeds - unexpected

# Even an explicit subclass with __slots__ = () cannot recover, because
# the auto-synthesized intermediate already carries __weakref__:
class Sub(Envelope[int]):
    __slots__ = ()
weakref.ref(Sub())                                                      # succeeds
```

## Expected

`Envelope[int]` (and any user subclass of it) should honor the `__slots__ = ()` declared on the origin and reject `weakref.ref(...)`, matching the un-parameterized `Envelope`.

## Actual

The synthesized `Envelope[int]` class has neither `__slots__` in its own dict nor any inherited declaration that suppresses default storage, so `__weakref__` (and `__dict__`, but `BaseModel` itself forces that one) reappears on every parameterized subclass and on anything inheriting from it.

## Root cause

`pydantic/_internal/_generics.py::create_generic_submodel` constructs the class with:

```python
namespace: dict[str, Any] =

title: `model_copy(update=...)` inconsistencies
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

#### 1

When doing a `model_copy(update={"_private_member":"new_val"})` on a model with a PrivateAttr, the new value for private attribute doesn't properly replace the old one (it gets added to `__dict__` instead).

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, PrivateAttr

class M(BaseModel):
    _private: str = PrivateAttr(default="old")

m2 = M.model_validate({}).model_copy(update={"_private":"new"})
print(f"{m2.__dict__=}")
print(f"{m2.__private_attributes__=}")
```

prints

```text
m2.__dict__={'_private': 'new'}
m2.__private_attributes__={'_private': ModelPrivateAttr(default='old')}
```

#### 2

When doing a `model_copy(update={"extra_member": "some_val"})` on a model where extras are forbidden, the extra member is allowed:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel,

class M(BaseModel, extra="forbid"):
    pass

m2 = M.model_validate({}).model_copy(update={"extra":"new"})
print(f"{m2.extra=}")
print(f"{m2.model_config.get('extra')=}")
```

prints

```text
m2.extra='new'
m2.model_config.get('extra')='forbid'
```

title: Cannot use `None` as type argument
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic's BaseModel.__class_getitem__ is annotated as `typevar_values: type[Any] | tuple[type[Any], ...]`, which causes type checkers to reject `None` as a type argument even though `None` is a valid PEP 484 type
annotation meaning `type(None)` / `NoneType`.

Error in PyCharm: `Expected type 'type | tuple[type, ...]', got 'type[None]'`

Root cause: pydantic/main.py BaseModel.__class_getitem__ signature should accept
`None` (and other special forms) as valid type arguments, not just `type[Any]`.

We could use `NoneType` to get rid of the problem, however, I suggest we update annotation on pydantic side.

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic as p


class Box[T](p.BaseModel):
    pass


# Triggers the warning because Pydantic's __class_getitem__ is typed as
# typevar_values: type[Any] | tuple[type[Any], ...]
# and None is not type[Any]:
x = Box[None]()
```

title: Use enum name instead of value
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/2980
Originally posted by **al-dpopowich** July 13, 2021 I have not been able to find this in a Discussion or Issue... If I have a python Enum: ```python import enum class StateAbbrev(enum.Enum): """State Abbreviations""" NY = "New York" MA = "Massachusetts" # etc ``` I can use this with SQLAlchemy... ```python from sqlalchemy import Table, Column, Enum # etc User = Table( 'myuser', ... Column('state', Enum(StateAbbrev)) ... ) ``` On saving, sqlalchemy uses *name* **NOT** the *value*. That is, sqlalchemy will store "NY", not "New York". Similarly, via my REST API, I want to accept the "codes" not the (possibly) long strings. This is a disconnect from pydantic which validates by value. I have seen answers to similar questions on stackoverflow suggest using the same value as name, e.g: ```python class Foo(enum.Enum): A = "A" B = "B" ``` But this is not practical when you want to offer your Enum as a CODE=>HUMAN_READABLE mapping. In short, what I want is a Config option, e.g., `validate_enum_by_name` (for backward compatibility, default to `False`): ```python # in validators.py def enum_member_validator(v: Any, field: 'ModelField', config: 'BaseConfig') -> Enum: enum = field.type_ try: enum_v = enum[v] if config.validate_enum_by_name else enum(v) except (KeyError, ValueError): # enum should be an enum, so will be iterable raise errors.EnumMemberError(enum_values=list(enum)) return enum_v.value if config.use_enum_values else enum_v ```

title: Add a global option to serialise/deserialise enums by name not value
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

There's an existing [discussion](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/2980) about this which started off as a feature request but was changed to a discussion. I think that discussion has run its course so I'd like to make a feature request which can either be progressed, or closed if the project does not want to go that way.

This feature request is for a single, global flag added to the configuration:

- Add a boolean configuration option: "serialise_enum_by_name" or similar, default False.
- If it is False: no change to serialisation / schemas.
- If it is True: serialisation / de-serialisation uses the enum name, and generated schemas contain a list of enum names.

I think this needs to be part of the core library as there have been a few suggested workarounds over time in the linked discussion, but they all rely on specific internal behaviour at the time and eventually break with newer releases.

There is some discussion in the linked issue of a non-global requirement that could be applied to specific enum instances. I'd like to explicitly exclude those from this feature request: I think that is a sufficiently different use case that if people want that it should go in its own request.





### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

title: Any plans to add skill.md within the repo?
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Hi team,
Big fan of pydantic, however a lot of times my AI generated code contains outdated pydantic v1 logic like 
`from typing import List` 
or something like that. FastAPI recently added an official [SKILL.md in their docs](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/.agents/skills/fastapi/SKILL.md).
There are some [unofficial skills available](https://www.skills.sh/bobmatnyc/claude-mpm-skills/pydantic), but that may/may not update their doc every time consistently with version updates.

Do you have a plan to support that? I would have to pick up the implementation and contribute to it

title: Add support for Python 3.15
body:
- [x] Update to a yet-to-be-released PyO3 version adding 3.15 support (https://github.com/PyO3/pyo3/pull/6014): https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/13301
- [ ] Update `MISSING` sentinel to reflect [the latest implementation](https://docs.python.org/3.15/whatsnew/3.15.html#whatsnew315-sentinel). Should we wait for mypy to [support it](https://github.com/python/mypy/pull/21454) before making it non-experimental?
- [ ] Add support for [lazy imports](https://docs.python.org/3.15/whatsnew/3.15.html#whatsnew315-lazy-imports) (will require work to catch errors when accessing `sys.modules`).
- [ ] Add support for [`frozendict` type](https://docs.python.org/3.15/whatsnew/3.15.html#whatsnew315-frozendict).
- [ ] Investigate if we need to do anything related to [frame pointers](https://docs.python.org/3.15/whatsnew/3.15.html#whatsnew315-frame-pointers).
- [ ] Use [`TypeForm`](https://docs.python.org/3.15/whatsnew/3.15.html#whatsnew315-typeform) whenever possible (in `TypeAdapter`, but also in several other places), once [mypy](https://github.com/python/mypy/pull/21262#issuecomment-4364872582) and [pyright](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/pull/11412) supports it.
- [ ] Investigate https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12778 and related changes to the `base64` module.

title: UrlConstraints can be bypassed by passing an existing AnyUrl instance to constrained URL fields
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2
- [x] I searched the existing tests and code paths relevant to `UrlConstraints`
- [x] I reproduced this locally with a focused regression test

### Description

`UrlConstraints` appear to be bypassable when the input is an already-constructed `AnyUrl` instance instead of a raw string.

The cleanest example is:

```python
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import AnyUrl, TypeAdapter, UrlConstraints

HttpOnlyUrl = Annotated[AnyUrl, UrlConstraints(allowed_schemes=["http", "https"])]
ta = TypeAdapter(HttpOnlyUrl)

ta.validate_python("file:///etc/passwd")      # correctly rejected
ta.validate_python(AnyUrl("file:///etc/passwd"))  # unexpectedly accepted
```

I traced this to the `_BaseUrl` wrapper path in `pydantic/networks.py`. `UrlConstraints.__get_pydantic_core_schema__` mutates the inner URL schema, but `_BaseUrl.__get_pydantic_core_schema__` returns early for `isinstance(v, source)`, which seems to let an existing `AnyUrl` instance skip the inner constrained schema path.

This looks like a wrapper-layer issue in `pydantic`, not a generic `pydantic-core` URL limitation. `pydantic-core` already has tests showing that constrained `url_schema(...)` validators can reject existing core `Url` objects with non-allowed schemes.

### Example Code

```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import AnyUrl, BaseModel, TypeAdapter, UrlConstraints

HttpOnlyUrl = Annotated[AnyUrl, UrlConstraints(allowed_schemes=["http", "https"])]

ta = TypeAdapter(HttpOnlyUrl)

assert ta.validate_python("https://example.com")

existing = AnyUrl("file:///etc/passwd")
accepted = ta.validate_python(existing)
assert accepted.scheme == "file"


class Model(BaseModel):
    v: HttpOnlyUrl


assert Model.model_validate({"v": AnyUrl("file:///etc/passwd")}).v.scheme == "file"
```

### Reproduction

I added two focused tests in `tests/test_networks.py`:

- `test_custom_constraints_reject_existing_url_instance`
- `test_model_val

title: support alternate date serializations in `pydantic_core.to_json`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Migrating to pydantic from older frameworks inevitably involves accommodating clients who expect serialization in different formats.  When using `pydantic_core.to_json` there is no way I have found to intercept date[time] types to format them in anything other than iso8601. I opened [this PR](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/13155) with an opinionated implementation of how we might support rfc2822 dates for serialization in `pydantic_core.to_json`.

However, I'm thinking a more general approach might be to implement something similar to orjson's [OPT_PASSTHROUGH_DATETIME](https://github.com/ijl/orjson#opt_passthrough_datetime).  This would allow an implementer to sub in any serialization function desired in python via the existing `fallback` function without having to accept the opinions I have included in the above PR for rfc2822.

Thoughts?

I have a model that contains a dict of instances of BaseModel as described below:

title: Usage of validation_alias with AliasChoices with Mypy gives explicit-any error
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I've recently tried to bump pydantic version in a project from `2.10.6` to `2.13.4` and it introduced mypy errors related to the usage of an `AliasChoices` as a validation_alias in a  field of a`BaseModel`.

### current mypy config:
```ini
[mypy]
plugins = pydantic.mypy
disallow_any_explicit = true
strict = true

[pydantic-mypy]
init_forbid_extra = true
init_typed = true
warn_required_dynamic_aliases = false
```

### Tests
I've then tested different minor versions to check which one introduced this change, and apparently the next version, `2.11.0a1`, introduced this behavior.

Testing in the example code, we get the following error:
```
test.py:6: error: Explicit "Any" is not allowed  [explicit-any]
```
This seems to indicate that it affects the type checking of the class, and not the typing of the `AliasChoices` itself.

### Questions:
1. Is this is a missing configuration on my part?
1. Is there another way to use validation_alias with multiple options that are properly typed for mypy?

## Related documentation
I've read the documentation about [warn_required_dynamic_aliases](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/integrations/dev-tools/mypy/#warn_required_dynamic_aliases) as well which seemed to indicate that the pydantic mypy plugin was properly configured.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import AliasChoices, BaseModel, Field

validation_alias_choices = AliasChoices("choice", "other_choice")


class ModelWithMypyError(BaseModel):
    test_field: str = Field(validation_alias=validation_alias_choices)


class WorkingModel(BaseModel):
    test_field: str = Field(validation_alias="choice")
```

title: No schema for built-in `string.templatelib.Template`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I wanted to use a Python 3.14 template string literal (`t"..."`) in a pydantic model, but when I made a `string.templatelib.Template` field, I got the following error:

```
pydantic.errors.PydanticSchemaGenerationError: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for <class 'string.templatelib.Template'>. Set `arbitrary_types_allowed=True` in the model_config to ignore this error or implement `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on your type to fully support it.

If you got this error by calling handler(<some type>) within `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` then you likely need to call `handler.generate_schema(<some type>)` since we do not call `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on `<some type>` otherwise to avoid infinite recursion.

For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.13/u/schema-for-unknown-type
```

[The docs](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/concepts/types/) say  "built-in and standard types can be used as-is", so I'm reporting this as a bug and not as a feature request, but feel free to relabel this if I'm wrong.

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic
from string import templatelib


class Model(pydantic.BaseModel):
    field: templatelib.Template
```

title: Nit about validator documentation code sample
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

This is strictly a documentation related issue.


The code sample here: https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/2.3/usage/validators#model-validators has a function `check_passwords_match` that tests if the fields are `None`.  These tests are unnecessary in this context as the fields are not `Optional`.  Including these tests makes the code sample gives the impression that the password fields to be `None` when in fact they cannot due to the design of pydantic.

# Reproduce the error

Include the following additional code snippet at the bottom of the example code:
```
bad = UserModel(username="bad", password1=None, password2=None)
```
and execute the script.  You'll see that you get validation errors on the password fields /before the custom validator is run/.


# Proposed fix

Remove the (uneccessary) None checks from the validator function:

```
 @model_validator(mode="after")                                                                                      
 def check_passwords_match(self) -> "UserModel":                                                                     
        pw1 = self.password1                                                                                            
        pw2 = self.password2                                                                                            
        if pw1 != pw2:                                                                                                  
            raise ValueError("passwords do not match")                                                                  
        return self e validator function to be

```
Optional:  include a comment that they cannot be None due to the pydantic intrinsic type validation that is run prior to the execution of the custom validator.

### Example Code

```Python
See main description.
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.13.3

title: Is `warn_untyped_fields` no longer recommended for the Mypy plugin?
body:
Seems like we should document `warn_untyped_fields`, and possibly validate the config: ### Discussed in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/13130
Originally posted by **johnthagen** April 30, 2026 I used to for all of my projects using Pydantic enable: ```toml [tool.pydantic-mypy] init_forbid_extra = true init_typed = true warn_required_dynamic_aliases = true warn_untyped_fields = true ``` But I checked the docs at - https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/integrations/dev-tools/mypy/#configuring-the-plugin > A configuration file with all plugin strictness flags enabled ```toml [tool.pydantic-mypy] init_forbid_extra = true init_typed = true warn_required_dynamic_aliases = true ``` This flag is not recommended there even thought it says "all plugin strictness flags". Was this flag removed at some point (I was unable to find a reference to this searching the issues and PRs). Does `warn_untyped_fields` still do anything? It looks like extra keys are ignore: ```toml [tool.pydantic-mypy] init_forbid_extra = true init_typed = true warn_required_dynamic_aliases = true aaa = true ``` Should the Mypy plugin validate that the user is only using expected keys?

title: Ability to replace empty strings with default value
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

This is mostly coming from using pydantic-settings with environment variables, but I think it could be useful in general to have an easy way to treat empty strings as unset/missing and use a default value instead. This could be something like a `empty_default: bool` argument to `StringConstraints`. If using `strip_whitespace=True`, whitespace-only strings would be processed this way as well.

I know `SettingsConfigDict` has `env_ignore_empty`, but this would allow per-field customization (and also acts differently with `strip_whitespace`).

title: pydantic-core macOS wheels break Homebrew install on macOS 15 (-headerpad_max_install_names)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

macOS 15 CI runners use Apple's new linker (`ld_prime`), which reserves significantly less Mach-O header padding than the old `ld`. Homebrew's post-install fixup (`install_name_tool`) needs that padding to rewrite dylib paths after installation. When it's absent, `brew install`/`brew upgrade` fails for any formula that vendors `pydantic-core`.

**Error:**
```
Error: Failed changing dylib ID of
  .../pydantic_core/_pydantic_core.cpython-311-darwin.so
Updated load commands do not fit in the header of
  .../_pydantic_core.cpython-311-darwin.so
...needs to be relinked, possibly with -headerpad or -headerpad_max_install_names
```

**Fix:** three lines in `pydantic-core/build.rs`, gated to macOS:

```rust
if std::env::var("CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS").as_deref() == Ok("macos") {
    println!("cargo:rustc-link-arg=-Wl,-headerpad_max_install_names");
}
```

`cargo:rustc-link-arg` passes the flag directly to the final `cdylib` link step, covering published wheels, source builds, and downstream Homebrew formulas.

The same root cause and fix is being addressed in `pyca/cryptography`: https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/pull/14777

A branch with the fix is ready at `washingtoneg/pydantic:fix/macos-headerpad-max-install-names`.

### Example Code

```Python

```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic-core 2.46.3, macOS 15
```

title: RootModel JSON schema drops examples from root field FieldInfo in 2.13
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In Pydantic 2.13, `Field(examples=...)` on a `RootModel` root type is no longer surfaced in the generated JSON schema. This worked in 2.12.

The `FieldInfo` correctly stores the examples value, and `TypeAdapter` with the same type produces the correct schema — only `RootModel.model_json_schema()` drops it.

### Example Code

```Python
import json
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import Field, RootModel, TypeAdapter

MyType = Annotated[str, Field(examples=["hello", "world"])]

class MyModel(RootModel[MyType]):
    pass

# examples MISSING
print(json.dumps(MyModel.model_json_schema(), indent=2))
# {"title": "MyModel", "type": "string"}

# examples PRESENT with TypeAdapter
print(json.dumps(TypeAdapter(MyType).json_schema(), indent=2))
# {"examples": ["hello", "world"], "type": "string"}

# FieldInfo has the data — it's just not surfaced in the schema
print(MyModel.model_fields['root'].examples)
# ['hello', 'world']
```

## Expected output from `model_json_schema()`:

```json
json{
  "examples": ["hello", "world"],
  "title": "MyModel",
  "type": "string"
}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
Python 3.14.4
Pydantic 2.13.0
Works in Pydantic 2.12.5
```

title: Mypy plugin: create_model fails to resolve inheritance when __base__=cls is used in a @classmethod
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Following #12879, to support dynamic models in the Mypy plugin, there is still a gap when using `create_model` within a factory `@classmethod`.

When `__base__=cls` is passed (to `create_model`), the plugin fails to recognize that the generated model is a subclass of the current class. This makes it impossible to use` type[Self]` as a return type without casting or adding `# type: ignore[return-value]`

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, create_model
from typing import Self

class CompositeModel(BaseModel):
    """Base class that generates dynamic variants of itself."""

    @classmethod
    def composite(cls) -> type[Self]:
        fields = {"plugin_id": (int, ...)}
        
        # do stuff...

        m = create_model(
            cls.__name__,
            __base__=cls,
            **fields,
        )
        return m
```

title: built-in error message localization via model_config (inheritable)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Pydantic validation error messages are always in English. For any non-English
API this forces users to either:

1. Catch `ValidationError` globally and rewrite `msg` fields (FastAPI
   exception handler pattern), or
2. Use third-party packages like
   [`pydantic-i18n`](https://github.com/boardpack/pydantic-i18n) or
   [`pydantic-translations`](https://github.com/orsinium-labs/pydantic-translations), or
3. Replace every built-in constraint with a hand-written `@field_validator`
   that raises `PydanticCustomError` with a translated string.

All three approaches share the same problems:
- Boilerplate repeated in every project.
- Translations live far away from the model definitions.
- The built-in constraints are lost from JSON Schema (option 3) or the
  translation layer has to parse English strings and reverse-engineer
  `ctx` (options 1 & 2).

Since Pydantic already emits **stable error `type` codes** (documented at
[Validation Errors](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/errors/validation_errors/))
and already carries `ctx` with all the template variables, a first-class
localization hook is a very small step — and it would eliminate an entire
category of third-party glue code.

### Current workaround

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator
from pydantic_core import PydanticCustomError

class FindAddressRequest(BaseModel):
    model_config = {"extra": "forbid"}

    query: str
    count: int = Field(10, gt=1, le=20)

    @field_validator("query")
    @classmethod
    def query_min_length(cls, v: str) -> str:
        if len(v) < 5:
            raise PydanticCustomError(
                "value_error.length",
                "Строка должна содержать не менее 5 символов",
            )
        return v
```

One field,

title: Support `AliasPath` with an integer first segment to index top-level list inputs
body:
### Initial Checks
- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

`AliasPath` already supports integer indices after a string dict-key (e.g. `AliasPath("data", 0)` picks `input["data"][0]`), but `AliasPath(N)` — int as the first/only path segment — fails at class-definition time with an opaque schema-build error from `pydantic-core`:

```
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.SchemaError: Error building "model" validator:
  SchemaError: Error building "model-fields" validator:
  TypeError: 'int' object cannot be converted to 'PyList'
```

#### What we'd like

Treat `AliasPath(N)` as "index `N` of the root input when it's a list/tuple", paralleling the documented behaviour for nested lists.

**Input:**
```python
[42, "alice", "a@example.com"]
```

**Working code:**
```python
from pydantic import AliasPath, BaseModel, Field

class Row(BaseModel):
    id: int = Field(validation_alias=AliasPath(0))
    name: str = Field(validation_alias=AliasPath(1))
    email: str = Field(validation_alias=AliasPath(2))

Row.model_validate([42, "alice", "a@example.com"])
# → Row(id=42, name='alice', email='a@example.com')
```

Should also compose with non-contiguous indices for sparse records where some positions are intentionally skipped:

```python
# Input is a list where only positions 0 and 7 carry fields we care about.
class Record(BaseModel):
    id: int = Field(validation_alias=AliasPath(0))
    status: str = Field(validation_alias=AliasPath(7))
```

#### Fallback

If full support is out of scope: raise a clear `TypeError` from `AliasPath.__init__` when the first argument isn't a `str`, so users hit the limitation at construction time rather than the `pydantic-core` traceback above.

### Affected Components
- [X] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [X] [Field Types](https:

title: not-exactly-a-feature request: Update pinned libc to 0.2.185 (which allows cygwin builds)
body:
Hi!  Currently, pydantic-core's Cargo.lock pins the libc crate at 0.2.155 (from about 2 years ago), but cygwin builds require newer versions.  I was able to get pydantic to install by building locally with a patched Cargo.lock targeting libc 0.2.185 instead.  I was then able to run tests (without pytest-examples, as `ruff` has some build issues) and got no errors.

So, it looks like this change might allow cygwin-python users to use pydantic, at least for the pydantic-core 2.46.3 version I tried this with.  (Targeting cygwin's python 3.12 and its rust 1.91.)

Happy to roll a PR for this if it'd be helpful.

title: # SIGSEGV at shutdown (`Py_FinalizeEx` / GC): Django + pydantic-core (Python 3.12)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Maybe Related: [pydantic#12867](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12867).

## Summary

When running Django’s test runner inside a Docker CI image with **`pydantic`** /
**`pydantic-core`** pins in the **2.11+** line, the **test suite reports success**, then
the **Python process segfaults during teardown** while the interpreter is shutting down
(**garbage collection** → **`Py_FinalizeEx`**).

Under **`gdb`**, the crashing thread shows **three frames inside**
**`pydantic_core/_pydantic_core.cpython-312-…so`** immediately above **`datetime_dealloc`**
in **`_datetime.cpython-312-…so`**. That matches the **shutdown / datetime /
pydantic-core** pattern discussed in **#12867** (the issue’s Python 3.11 trace includes
**`TzInfo`** / **`_Py_Dealloc`**).

We do **not** have a minimal public repro without our application code; this report
documents **versions**, **symptoms**, **`gdb`** capture, and **how we run tests** so
maintainers can compare with **#12867** and decide whether this is the same root cause on
**Python 3.12**.

---

## Environment

| Item | Value |
| --- | --- |
| OS / image | Rocky Linux 9–style container (`test-django` in our repo) |
| Python | **3.12** |
| Packages (repro) | **`pydantic==2.13.2`**, **`pydantic-core==2.46.2`** (`pip -U` in image) |
| Runner | Django **`manage.py test`** with Postgres + Redis (Compose) |
| Baseline (stable in our runs) | **`pydantic==2.10.6`**, **`pydantic-core==2.27.2`** |

---

## Expected vs actual

**Expected:** After tests finish, the process exits with code **0** and the interpreter
shuts down cleanly.

**Actual:**

- Tests print **`OK`** and normal Django teardown (including **`Flushing redis…`**).
- Then either:
  - **`Fatal Python error: Segmentation fault`** (stderr), often **`Garbage-collecting`**
    with no Python frame; exit **139**, or
  - Under **`gdb`**: **`SIGSEGV`** with the stack below.

The failure happens **after** t

title: Model field name shadowing model class name breaks Optional nested model parsing
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a BaseModel field name is identical to the referenced model class name, Pydantic fails to correctly parse the nested model if it is optional.

### Example Code
The following example does not work as expected:

```python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Address(BaseModel):
    street: str

class School(BaseModel):
    name: str
    Address: Optional[Address] = None

if __name__ == '__main__':
    entry = {"name": "Example name", "Address": {"street": "Example street"}}
    s = School(**entry)
    print(f"{s = }")
```


It only works when slightly modifying the Address class name like the following:
```python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel

class _Address(BaseModel):
    street: str

class School(BaseModel):
    name: str
    Address: Optional[_Address] = None

if __name__ == '__main__':
    entry = {"name": "Example name", "Address": {"street": "Example street"}}
    s = School(**entry)
    print(f"{s = }")
```

title: Inherited string annotations break when class has method shadowing a builtin
body:
## Description

After #13070 (`b36eaeb4`, "Drop support for Python 3.9"), `safe_get_annotations` was changed from:

```python
if isinstance(obj, type):
    return obj.__dict__.get('__annotations__', {})
else:
    return getattr(obj, '__annotations__', {})
```

to:

```python
return getattr(obj, '__annotations__', {})
```

The old code used `obj.__dict__` for classes, which returns only the class's **own** annotations. The new code uses `getattr`, which walks the MRO and includes **inherited** annotations — including string annotations from parent classes that use `from __future__ import annotations`.

These inherited string annotations are then `eval`'d with `vars(typ)` as `localns` (via `NsResolver.types_namespace`). If the class has a method that shadows a builtin name (e.g. a `dict` method), the method is found instead of the builtin type, causing a `TypeError`.

## Minimal reproduction

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    metadata: dict[str, Any] | None = None

    def dict(self, **kwargs: Any) -> dict:
        return {}
```

```
TypeError: 'function' object is not subscriptable
Unable to evaluate type annotation 'dict[str, Any] | None'.
```

Works on v2.13.2, fails on `main`.

## Real-world impact

This breaks `langchain-core`, where `BaseLanguageModel` (defined with `from __future__ import annotations`) has a `metadata: dict[str, Any] | None` field, and `BaseLLM` inherits it while also defining a `dict()` method override. Importing `langchain_openai` fails at class definition time.

Caught by the [logfire weekly CI](https://github.com/pydantic/logfire/actions/runs/24666273628/job/72124642387) which tests against pydantic `main`.

## Root cause

The `TODO` comment in the old code said "just do `getattr` when dropping support for Python 3.9" — but for `__annotations__`, `__dict__` access vs `getattr` has different inheritance semantics. The simplification changed behavi

title: Ability to pass context to __get_pydantic_core_schema__ and __get_pydantic_json_schema__
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I maintain the following library: https://github.com/psalvaggio/dynapydantic https://psalvaggio.github.io/dynapydantic/latest/

The general idea of the library is to offer polymorphic validation to mirror the polymorphic serialization that has been added to pydantic. Essentially, classes keep track of their children via `__pydantic_init_subclass__`, register them, the create unions to serve as field annotations.

Initially, I made this work with a custom annotation around the base class, but I have come up with a way to make the following syntax work:

```python
import dynapydantic
import pydantic

class Base(
    dynapydantic.SubclassTrackingModel,
    union_mode="smart",
    implicit_polymorphic=True,
):
    pass

class A(Base, extra="forbid"):
    a: int

class B(Base, extra="forbid"):
    other: Base

B(other={"other": {"other": {"a": 2}}}) # B(other=B(other=B(other=A(a=2))))
```

This requires overriding `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` and `__get_pydantic_json_schema__`, which works fine in the typical usage.

However, I'm running into issues with inheritance. Consider the following simple tree:

```python
Base <- Mid <- Leaf
```
If I put `Base` (let's pretend it is abstract) into my model, I'd want the field to accept `Mid` and `Leaf`, which are concrete. If I put `Mid` in there however, I'd want it to accept `Mid` or `Leaf`. Same thing if `Base` was not abstract and would be acceptable. This leads to two paths's to `__get_pydantic_core_schema__`:

1. If I use the type directly as a field annotation. Here I want to make the validator that validates the union.
2. If I am making a TypeAdapter from a base class's schema. Here I want the schema that would have been produced had I not overridden `__get_pydantic_core_schema__`.

However,

title: Regression on Python 3.12: from_attributes=True now raises get_attribute_error for optional nested fields when attribute access raises an AttributeError subclass
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

 I found a behavior change between Pydantic 2.12 and 2.13 when validating objects with from_attributes=True.

 For an optional nested field, if attribute access raises a subclass of AttributeError, Pydantic 2.12 treats that as a missing attribute and sets the field to None, while Pydantic 2.13
 raises a ValidationError with type='get_attribute_error'.

 Interestingly, I can reproduce this on Python 3.12, but not on Python 3.13.

 This matters in Django because reverse one-to-one missing relations raise RelatedObjectDoesNotExist, which is a subclass of AttributeError.

### Example Code


#### Repro on Python 3.12 with Pydantic 2.12

 ```bash
   uvx --python 3.12 --with "pydantic~=2.12.0" python - <<'PY'
   import pydantic
   from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

   print("pydantic", pydantic.__version__)


   class MissingRelation(AttributeError):
       pass


   class Child(BaseModel):
       x: int


   class Parent(BaseModel):
       child: Child | None = None
       model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)


   class Obj:
       @property
       def child(self):
           raise MissingRelation("missing child")


   print(Parent.model_validate(Obj()))
   PY
```
 

 Output:

 ```
   pydantic 2.12.x
   child=None
 ```

 #### Repro on Python 3.12 with Pydantic 2.13

 ```bash
   uvx --python 3.12 --with "pydantic>=2.13" python - <<'PY'
   import pydantic
   from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

   print("pydantic", pydantic.__version__)


   class MissingRelation(AttributeError):
       pass


   class Child(BaseModel):
       x: int


   class Parent(BaseModel):
       child: Child | None = None
       model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)


   class Obj:
       @property
       def child(self):
           raise MissingRelation("missing child")


   print(Parent.model_validate(Obj()))
   PY
 ```

 Output:

   pydantic 2.13.x
   pydantic_core._pydantic_

title: Decimal JSON schema pattern rejects pydantic's own scientific notation serialization output (v2.12 regression)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

**`model_json_schema(mode="serialization")` pattern for `Decimal` breaks on scientific notation**

The serialization schema for `Decimal` fields generates a regex that rejects values that `model_dump_json()` itself produces.

Specifically: `pydantic-core`'s serializer calls `str()` on `Decimal` objects, which outputs scientific notation for small/large values. E.g. `Decimal('0.0000001')` becomes `"1E-7"`. But the pattern added in #11987 (`^(?!^[-+.]*$)[+-]?0*\d*\.?\d*$`) has no `[eE]` component, so it'll never match those strings.

As a result, any downstream consumer validating `model_dump_json()` output against `model_json_schema()`, such as OpenAPI validators and codegen + validation tools like Orval + Zod, will intermittently reject perfectly valid serialized values. Intermittent because only certain `Decimal` values trigger scientific notation.

**v2.11**:
```json
{"anyOf": [{"type": "string"}, {"type": "null"}]}
```

**v2.12**:
```json
{"anyOf": [{"pattern": "^(?!^[-+.]*$)[+-]?0*\\d*\\.?\\d*$", "type": "string"}, {"type": "null"}]}
```

Two possible fixes:
- Append `(?:[eE][+-]?\d+)?` to the pattern in serialization mode
- Normalize `Decimal` to fixed-point during serialization before it ever hits the schema

### Example Code

```Python
import json
import re
from decimal import Decimal
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    value: Decimal | None

schema = MyModel.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")
pattern = next(
    v for v in schema["properties"]["value"]["anyOf"] if v.get("type") == "string"
)["pattern"]
regex = re.compile(pattern)

# pydantic serializes this as "1E-7" which fails its own schema's pattern
m = MyModel(value=Decimal("0.0000001"))
serialized = json.loads(m.model_dump_json())["value"]

print(f"Pattern: {pattern}")
print(f"Serialized: {serialized!r}")
print(f"Matches: {bool(regex.match(serialized))}")
# Pattern: ^(?!^[-+.]*$)[+-]?0*\d*\.?

title: Incomplete generic recursive models in Pydantic 2.13
body:
From https://github.com/ome-zarr-models/ome-zarr-models-py/issues/417:

```python
# module2.py
from typing import Generic, TypeVar, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel

TBaseItem = Union["GroupSpec", "ArraySpec"]
TItem = TypeVar('TItem', bound=TBaseItem)


class ArraySpec(BaseModel):
    pass


class GroupSpec(BaseModel, Generic[TItem]):
    members: TItem
```

```python
# module1.py
from module2 import GroupSpec, TBaseItem


class BaseGroupv04(GroupSpec[TBaseItem]):
    pass
```

Pydantic 2.12.5:

```python
from module1 import BaseGroupv04

BaseGroupv04.__pydantic_complete__
#> True (2.12.5)
#> False (2.13.0)

BaseGroupv04.model_fields['members']
#> FieldInfo(annotation=Union[GroupSpec, ArraySpec], required=True) (2.12.0)
#> FieldInfo(annotation=Union[PydanticRecursiveRef, ArraySpec], required=True) (2.13.0)
```

Bisected to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12463. It also seems that it already manifested on Python 3.14 even before Pydantic 2.13.

title: `model_copy(deep=True, update=...)` unnecessarily deepcopies fields that will be replaced by `update`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

When calling `model_copy(deep=True, update={...})`, the current implementation deepcopies the **entire** model first, then applies the `update` dict afterwards:

```python
# pydantic/main.py
def model_copy(self, *, update=None, deep=False):
    copied = self.__deepcopy__() if deep else self.__copy__()  # full deepcopy first
    if update:
        ...
        copied.__dict__.update(update)  # then overwrite
    return copied
```

This means fields specified in `update` are deepcopied and then immediately discarded. This causes two problems:

1. **Wasteful performance**: fields that will be replaced are deepcopied for nothing.
2. **Fails on non-copyable fields**: if a field contains an object that cannot be deepcopied (e.g., native C extensions, file handles, GPU tensors), `model_copy` raises an error — even though the caller explicitly intends to replace that field via `update`.

### Example Code

```python
import mujoco
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class PhysicsEngine:
    """Wraps a native MuJoCo simulation (not deepcopy-safe)."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_string("<mujoco/>")
        self.data = mujoco.MjData(self.model)


class AppConfig(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)

    name: str = "default"
    engine: PhysicsEngine = None


config = AppConfig(engine=PhysicsEngine())

# This FAILS even though we are replacing `engine`:
copy = config.model_copy(
    deep=True,
    update={"engine": None},  # explicitly replacing the non-copyable field
)
# ValueError: negative dimensions are not allowed
# (from deepcopy trying to copy the MuJoCo native structs)
```

### Proposed Solution

**Option A (minimal):** Add a parameter (e.g., `update_before_co

title: Support Json Pointers (RFC 6901) in Serialization
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

[RFC 6901](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6901) specifies a syntax for the use of json pointers.

For efficiency and interoperability of object graph serialization, it would be desirable for pydantic to support the json pointer usage as an optional part of pydantic serialization.  Pydantic consumers using object graphs/data structures with many references could see a very significant amount of runtime bandwidth savings.  The use of RFC 6901 enables cross-language+cross-library interoperability.

We have implemented the 'local' usage of RFC 6901(no remote pointers) in [python](https://github.com/OpenMCPTools/jref_python), [java](https://github.com/OpenMCPTools/jref_java), and [typescript](https://github.com/OpenMCPTools/jref_typescript) and would like to consider contributing the python implementation (and/or design) to this project.


### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)

title: Missing ValidationInfo data using model_validate_json with v2.13.0
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

After updating from 2.12.5 to 2.13.0, when using `model_validate_json` along with a `field_validator` that includes `ValidationInfo`, the data is missing. The error does not occur when using `model_validate`. A reproducible example is provided below.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationInfo, field_validator


class Foo(BaseModel):
    field1: str
    field2: str

    @field_validator("field2")
    @classmethod
    def _validate_field2(cls, v: str, info: ValidationInfo) -> str:
        if info.data["field1"] == v:
            raise ValueError
        return v


# this works
f1 = Foo.model_validate({"field1": "a", "field2": "b"})

# this fails with TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable
f2 = Foo.model_validate_json('{"field1": "a", "field2": "b"}')
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi! 👋 

I'm packaging this project for Arch Linux (together with @christian-heusel).
With the release of pydantic 2.13.0 we noticed, that the [pydantic-core](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core) repository is now archived and that its sources have been moved to this repository instead.
The pydantic release 2.13.0 appears to require a pydantic_core 2.46.0:
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/46bf4fa648af3a1fbf4603a37f210e9d9c618357/pyproject.toml#L50
However, there don't seem to be any specific tags for pydantic_core 2.46.0 to tie a download to (however, a version on PyPI exists, but it is unclear from which version of the code it has been created).
The pydantic_core package seems to have been upgraded to 2.46.0 in the same commit that is used to upgrade pydantic to 2.13.0: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/commit/1b359edab09c623464d23c6fd2503ae5ff276d43

This unfortunately does not allow us to upgrade pydantic currently, because we cannot identify pydantic_core 2.46.0 reliably in this repository.
Can you please introduce dedicated tags for the pydantic_core component in this repository, so that we can use it as specific build input? 🙏 

FTR: On Arch Linux, we are building from upstream source (https://rfc.archlinux.page/0020-sources-for-python-packaging/), not from PyPI releases, because those are often broken or incomplete and harder to patch.

title: Overriding `extra` causes `BaseModel` equality to fail
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Take this simple reproducer
```python
class A(BaseModel, extra="allow"):
    a: int

A(a=1) == A.model_validate({"a": 1})   # True
A(a=1) == A.model_validate({"a": 1}, extra="forbid")   # False
```
So, just the fact that we forbid extra fields in the second example cause the equality to fail, even though the field values are identical and we didn't actually pass in any extra fields.

This appears to be because `A(a=1).model_extra == {}` and `A.model_validate({"a": 1}, extra="forbid").model_extra is None`. This check needs to be extended so that model extra being `{}` vs `None` still counts as being equal

### Example Code

```Python
class A(BaseModel, extra="allow"):
    a: int

assert A(a=1) == A.model_validate({"a": 1})                   # Passes
assert A(a=1) == A.model_validate({"a": 1}, extra="forbid")   # Fails
```

title: ConfigDict accepts unknown keys without warning — typos like stric=True silently ignored
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`ConfigDict` accepts any key without validation. A typo like `stric=True` (missing the `t`) is silently accepted - `strict` is never set, and no warning is emitted. The developer gets no signal that their configuration has no effect.

Pydantic validates everything else - field types, annotations, aliases. `ConfigDict` is the one place where an invalid key passes through without any check. A `logging.warning` on unrecognized keys would catch typos and removed/renamed settings early.

I found this by systematically scoring enforcement levels across pydantic's config propagation pipeline. The invariant analysis methodology is documented at [github.com/savarin/lean-agent](https://github.com/savarin/lean-agent). I have a working fix (`pydantic/_internal/_config.py`, ~11 lines) and can open a PR immediately if this is something the team wants.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(stric=True)  # typo: should be 'strict'
    name: str

m = MyModel(name="alice")
print(f"model_config: {MyModel.model_config}")
print(f"strict actually set: {MyModel.model_config.get('strict', 'NOT SET')}")
print(f"stric (typo) present: {'stric' in MyModel.model_config}")
# model_config: {'stric': True}
# strict actually set: NOT SET
# stric (typo) present: True
# — zero warnings emitted
```

title: AliasPath in serialization_alias — opaque error
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Passing `AliasPath` or `AliasChoices` as `serialization_alias` in `Field()` crashes with an opaque error from the Rust layer. The error doesn't explain what went wrong or suggest a fix.

This is adjacent to the alias consistency discussions in #7251, #8379, and #8551 - `serialization_alias` only accepts `str`, but the type hints don't prevent passing `AliasPath`, and the error that results is confusing.

Current error:

```
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.SchemaError: Error building `model` serializer:
  SchemaError: Error building `model-fields` serializer:
  TypeError: 'AliasPath' object is not an instance of 'str'
```

Expected: a `PydanticUserError` raised at `Field()` construction time with a message like: "`serialization_alias` must be a `str`, not `AliasPath` or `AliasChoices`. Use `validation_alias` for complex alias paths."

Catching this at `FieldInfo.__init__` rather than at schema build time gives the developer a clear, actionable message. Happy to open a PR if this is something the team wants.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.fields import AliasPath, Field

try:
    class User(BaseModel):
        name: str = Field(serialization_alias=AliasPath("user", "name"))
except Exception as e:
    print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")
    # pydantic_core._pydantic_core.SchemaError: Error building `model` serializer:
    #   SchemaError: Error building `model-fields` serializer:
    #   TypeError: 'AliasPath' object is not an instance of 'str'
```

body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

On musl-based Python installed with uv, if Pydantic is called from a thread other than the main thread, the entire process is killed by SIGSEGV.

A minimal reproducible example is available at <https://github.com/satgo1546/pydantic-segfault-mwe>.

I have confirmed this behavior on:

- Alpine Linux 3.23 x86_64
- Alpine Linux 3.23 arm64
- Ubuntu 24.04 x86_64 with musl-tools installed
- Ubuntu 24.04 arm64 with musl-tools installed

Note that uv uses distributions from the Astral [python-build-standalone](https://github.com/astral-sh/python-build-standalone) project instead of the official python.org binaries. The problem is gone with official python.org binaries.



### Example Code

```Python

# A minimal reproducible example is available at <https://github.com/satgo1546/pydantic-segfault-mwe>.
import threading
from pydantic import BaseModel
class MyModel(BaseModel):

title: Decimal JSON schema pattern uses lookahead regex unsupported by pydantic-core
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

  Pydantic v2 emits a JSON schema for Decimal fields that includes a regex pattern with a negative lookahead `((?!...))`. This pattern cannot be compiled by `pydantic-core`'s Rust regex crate, which does not support look-around assertions.

  This means the JSON schema pydantic produces for Decimal cannot be round-tripped back through pydantic's own validation  e.g. constructing a `TypeAdapter` with `StringConstraints(pattern=...)` using the emitted pattern fails with a `SchemaError`.

  This is a practical problem for any tooling that reconstructs pydantic types from emitted JSON schemas (e.g. MCP structured content parsing via FastMCP).


### Example Code

```Python
"""Minimal reproduction: Decimal JSON schema pattern uses lookahead unsupported by pydantic-core.

Pydantic generates a JSON schema for Decimal fields with a regex pattern that uses
a negative lookahead (?!...). When that schema is round-tripped back through pydantic
(e.g. via TypeAdapter with StringConstraints), pydantic-core's Rust regex engine
rejects it because it does not support look-around assertions.
"""

from decimal import Decimal
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, StringConstraints, TypeAdapter


class MyModel(BaseModel):
    amount: Decimal


def test_decimal_json_schema_pattern_roundtrips_through_pydantic_core():
    schema = MyModel.model_json_schema()

    # Decimal emits an anyOf with a string variant carrying the pattern
    string_variant = next(v for v in schema['properties']['amount']['anyOf'] if v.get('type') == 'string')
    pattern = string_variant['pattern']

    # The emitted pattern contains a negative lookahead:
    assert '(?!' in pattern

    # Feeding it back into StringConstraints fails with:
    #   SchemaError: regex parse error: look-around ... is not supported
    ConstrainedStr = Annotated[str, StringConstraints(pattern=pattern)]
    TypeAdapter(ConstrainedStr)

title: `.model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` excludes fields with default values that have been modified
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I use models that have list fields with a default list factory so I can append to them after a new model has been instantiated. When I try to dump the models with `exclude_unset` set to True, those fields that have had data appended aren't included. I can see the argument that they weren't technically "set" but that seems to go against the spirit of excluding unset fields to me. Examples below.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class TestDump(BaseModel):
    default_list: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)


a = TestDump()
a.default_list.append("asdf")
print(a)
# output: default_list=['asdf']
print(a.model_dump(mode="json", exclude_unset=True))
# output: {}

b = TestDump()
b.default_list = ["asdf"]
print(b)
# output: default_list=['asdf']
print(b.model_dump(mode="json", exclude_unset=True))
# output: {'default_list': ['asdf']}
```

As of 27th March release, I found that latest pydantic-core was failing to install due to lack of wheels and wanting to build at install time.

Thing is this is the default python version being shipped to Windows users right now. Following the simple install instructions from MS or Python.org and an average user will end up with 3.14. I have dealt with this by pinning back the version in my project's `pyproject.toml`, however this isn't ideal.

Given that cpython 3.14 is now default Windows version, some pre-compiled wheels in your release would be appreciated.

- CPython 3.14

- result: attempts source build → fails without Rust
- expected: wheel exists (as in 2.41.5)

title: Support for x-enum-varnames (Enum member names) in JSON Schema Generator
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Hi! Thanks for the great library!

I need support for the `x-enum-varnames` OpenAPI 3 extension field in the JSON Schema Generator and was curious to see whether there is interests in adding support directly to pydantic, rather than simply creating a custom generator.
I have implemented a [PR](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/13004), and would love to commit it if there is interest.

### Context

`x-enum-varnames` maintains the key(name) of any enum value that defines it in the form `name = 'value'`.
  The default behavior is to use the value as both the key and value.
  This is a frequently supported extension field in OpenAPI 3 and is used by many code generators to allow generating bot Python and Typescript enums in the following form:

```python
class Fruit(Enum):
    apple = 'Apple'
    grape = 'Grape'
``` 
```typescript
export enum Fruit {
    apple = "Apple",
    grape = "Grape"
}
``` 

Without this extension we lose this extra information, and a 1:1 recreation of the original code is impossible:
```python
class Fruit(Enum):
    Apple = 'Apple'
    Grape = 'Grape'
``` 

### Proof Of Concept
In my PR I have added a new option 

**`use_x_enum_varnames`**: `bool`
Default: `False`
```
Whether the generated json-schema/OpenAPI 3 enums should have the `x-enum-varnames` extension populated.
This maintains the key of any enum value that defines it in the form `key = 'value'`.
The default behavior is to use the value as both the key and value.
This is a frequently supported extension field in OpenAPI 3.
```

### Example

```python
from enum import Enum

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

class SomeEnum(Enum):
    FOO = 'foo'
    BAR = 'bar'
    BAZ = 'baz'

class DefaultModel(BaseModel):
    value: SomeEnum

DefaultModel.

title: Subclass Serialization Includes Child Fields with MISSING Sentinel
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

# Pydantic Bug Report: Subclass Serialization Includes Child Fields with MISSING Sentinel

## Summary

When a base class contains fields with `| MISSING` type annotation AND a container field uses `| MISSING` in its union type, Pydantic fails to properly serialize subclasses according to their declared parent type. Instead, it serializes the full runtime (child) type, including fields that should be excluded.

## Versions

- **pydantic**: 2.12.5
- **pydantic_core**: 2.41.5

## Minimal Reproducer

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, TypeAdapter
from pydantic.experimental.missing_sentinel import MISSING

class Base(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra='forbid')
    base_field: str | MISSING = MISSING  # Key ingredient 1

class Parent(Base):
    parent_field: str

class Child(Parent):
    child_field: str  # Should NOT appear when serializing as Parent

class Container(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra='forbid')
    item: Parent | MISSING = MISSING  # Key ingredient 2

# Create child and assign to parent-typed field
child = Child(parent_field='p', child_field='c')
container = Container(item=child)

# Serialize
result = TypeAdapter(Container).dump_python(container, mode='json')

# Expected: {'item': {'parent_field': 'p'}}
# Actual:   {'item': {'parent_field': 'p', 'child_field': 'c'}}
print(f"Has child_field (BUG): {'child_field' in result.get('item', {})}")  # True (incorrect)
```

## Control Tests

The issue **only** occurs when **both** conditions are met:

### Without MISSING in base class - Works correctly

title: Multiple sets of serializer wanings emitted when `WrapSerializer` used
body:
> I think these are already "propagated" by the exception as you demonstrated above
> 
> This is when they appear as exceptions, which is only the case in the two bullet points I mentioned. In the general case, I feel like the warnings should be propagated to the outer caller, because right now it acts weirdly:
> 
> ```python
> from typing import Annotated
> 
> from pydantic import BaseModel, WrapSerializer
> 
> 
> class Model(BaseModel):
>     a: Annotated[str, WrapSerializer(lambda v, h: h(v))]
>     b: str
>     c: str
> 
> m = Model(a="t", b="t", c="t")
> m.a = 1
> m.b = 2
> m.c = 3
> 
> m.model_dump()
> # Two separate warnings:
> """
> UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
>   PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='a', input_value=1, input_type=int])
> UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
>   PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='b', input_value=2, input_type=int])
>   PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `str` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='c', input_value=3, input_type=int])
> """
> ```
> 
> Maybe we could special case the `final_check()` call within the handler to raise an error if the warnings mode is set to `'error'`, and otherwise just propagate the collected warnings? 

 _Originally posted by @Viicos in [#12800](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12800#issuecomment-4132943512)_

title: Add `zizmor` for GitHub Actions security linting, pin GitHub actions
body:
The recent Trivy and LiteLLM incidents motivated us to look more into the supply chain security of the software we rely on every day.

In particular, we found that Pydantic currently does not pin the third-party GitHub actions it uses. This is dangerous because GitHub Actions are referenced via git tags, which are mutable. Hijacking a repository referenced in Pydantic's workflows could allow an attacker to infect release artifacts produced in GitHub Actions with malware.

To follow best practices,

1. actions should be pinned to a hash (i.e., `uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@37802adc94f370d6bfd71619e3f0bf239e1f3b78 # v7.6.0` instead of `uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v7`)
2. Dependabot should be configured to autoupdate actions with a cooldown of, for example, 7 days (see also [this blog post](https://blog.yossarian.net/2025/11/21/We-should-all-be-using-dependency-cooldowns) about this topic)

To add CI validation that enforces pinning of all actions, I propose introducing [zizmor](https://github.com/zizmorcore/zizmor) as a pre-commit hook in this repository. Zizmor bundles various [audit rules](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits), among which are [`dependabot-cooldown`](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#dependabot-cooldown) and [`unpinned-uses`](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#unpinned-uses). Those address exactly the concerns I bring forward. Another notable example is [`template-injection`](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#template-injection).

We already use `zizmor` within our organizations and are very happy with it.

I will open a PR and reference it here to help you better understand what needs to be done to bring this forward.

*Edit: The PR is https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12981*

Cc @pavelzw

title: mypy `[arg-type]`-error when passing in type that can be converted to `RootModel`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The below example code (named `foo.py`) passes when running `python foo.py` as the input `"1.0"`-string gets converted to `float`. However, running `mypy foo.py` (with or without `--strict`) gives the following error:
```bash
foo.py:11: error: Argument 1 to "RootModel" has incompatible type "str"; expected "float"  [arg-type]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```
I would expect the `BaseModel`-fields and the `RootModel`-field to behave the same in this case. That is, if the value can be converted – either via a custom made validator or through the conversion table – then `mypy` should not raise an error. 

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic


class Foo(pydantic.BaseModel):
    f: float


RFoo = pydantic.RootModel[float]

f = Foo(f="1.0")
rf = RFoo("1.0")

assert isinstance(f.f, float)
assert isinstance(rf.root, float)
assert f.f == rf.root
```
