title: Add an equivalent allow_inf_nan config setting for decimal.Decimal that defaults to false
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

This has been discussed in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1885 and I just got bitten by this today while consuming a .NET REST API that returned "Infinity". Our code runs in ARM and we use decimal.Decimal everywhere to preseve precision as data flows through out system from other systems that could be X86_X64 or ARM.

This code replicates the problem.

```python
import dataclasses
import decimal

import pydantic


@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
class TestDataClass:
    positive_infinity: decimal.Decimal
    negative_infinity: decimal.Decimal


expected = TestDataClass(
    positive_infinity=decimal.Decimal("Infinity"),
    negative_infinity=decimal.Decimal("-Infinity"),
)
adapter = pydantic.TypeAdapter(TestDataClass)
actual = adapter.validate_python({"positive_infinity": "Infinity", "negative_infinity": "-Infinity"})

print(bool(expected == actual))
```

My workaround for now is to add a custom "type hint" that's really a class with a __get_pydantic_core_schema__ to do the conversion.

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [x] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [x] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https:/

title: Unexpected behaviour for `AliasChoices`
body:
Alias choices is reported with the follow signature
```
class AliasChoices(choices: list[str | AliasPath])
```
as it is created as a dataclass at
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/1a37a3d479dea9b21c7379e882bf5ec56fb94fc2/pydantic/aliases.py#L57-L58
but this conflicts with the "real" signature at https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/1a37a3d479dea9b21c7379e882bf5ec56fb94fc2/pydantic/aliases.py#L70-L71

When trying to create a simple model like this as a test
```py
def test_reproduce():
    class Model(BaseModel):

        toDate: Annotated[
            str,
            Field(
                validation_alias=AliasChoices(["to", "to_date", "toDate"]),
            ),
        ]

```
the following exception is raised:
```py
E           pydantic_core._pydantic_core.SchemaError: Error building "model" validator:
E             SchemaError: Error building "model-fields" validator:
E             TypeError: The first item in an alias path should be a string
```

This exception is not really helpful at this point.

A possible solution would probably be to explicitly allow the choices keyword with a test being
```py


def test_reproduce_minmal():
    assert AliasChoices(choices=["to", "to_date", "toDate"]) == AliasChoices(
        "to", "to_date", "toDate"
    )
```

i.e.
```py

    @overload
    def __init__(self, *, choices: list[str | AliasPath]) -> None: ...

    @overload
    def __init__(
        self,
        first_choice: str | AliasPath,
        *other_choices: str | AliasPath,
    ) -> None: ...

    def __init__(
        self,
        first_choice: str | AliasPath = None,
        *other_choices: str | AliasPath,
        choices: list[str | AliasPath] | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        match (first_choice, choices):
            case (None, None):
                raise ValueError("No aliases specified")
            case (None, _choices):
                self.choices = _choices
            case (_first_choice, None):
                self.choices = [_first_

title: `BaseModel.model_zip()` - construct a list of models from column-oriented data
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

A common pattern when working with Pydantic models is constructing a list of instances from parallel sequences - essentially a column-to-row transpose:

```python
orders = [
    Order(customer=c, product=p, quantity=q)
    for c, p, q in zip(customers, products, quantities, strict=True)
]
```

This comes up frequently in data processing pipelines, ETL code, and API layers where intermediate results are naturally columnar (one list per field) but the final output is a list of validated models.

**Proposed API**

A classmethod on `BaseModel`:

```python
orders = Order.model_zip(customer=customers, product=products, quantity=quantities)
```

Equivalent to the comprehension above, but:

- Validates that all sequences have the same length (raises on mismatch)
- Each row is validated through the normal model constructor

Of course, the name doesn't have to be `model_zip` :)

**Why this belongs in Pydantic**

`model_zip` would be a natural batched counterpart to normal model construction. Pydantic already provides field-aware tooling around model instantiation, including mypy plugin support and editor support in  e.g., VS Code. A built-in `BaseModel.model_zip(...)` would extend that experience to the common case where data is organized by field rather than by row.

Pydantic is also the right layer for this because it already owns the model’s field definitions and runtime validation semantics. While users can validate `list[MyModel]`, they still need to manually transpose columnar inputs first. `model_zip` would fill that gap with a small, explicit API for a common pattern.

**Alternatives considered**

- **Keep the comprehension**: Works, is type-safe (the IDE sees the constructor call), but verbose and repetitive when the pattern recurs across

title: `model_validate` and `model_validate_json` works differently with `Enum._missing_` method.
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`Enum._missing_ ` is a [enums function](https://docs.python.org/3/library/enum.html#enum.Enum._missing_) which called on creating enum instance from missing member.

`model_validate` works with this function properly - `value` param of option contains real "missing" value.
However, `model_validate_json` passes `None` to this field.

### Example Code

```Python
import enum
from typing import Any, Literal, Self
import pydantic

class E(enum.Enum):
    A = "A"
    B = "B"

    _FALLBACK = "FALLBACK"

    @classmethod
    def _missing_(cls, value: object):
        print(f"missing: {cls = } - {value = }")

        return cls._FALLBACK
        # return None  # super()._missing_(value)


class Model(pydantic.BaseModel):
    v: E


x1 = Model.model_validate({"v": "A"})
print(f"{x1 = }")
# x1 = Model(v=<E.A: 'A'>)

x2 = Model.model_validate({"v": "C"})
print(f"{x2 = }")
# missing: cls = <enum 'E'> - value = 'C'
# x2 = Model(v=<E._FALLBACK: 'FALLBACK'>)

y1 = Model.model_validate_json('{"v": "A"}')
print(f"{y1 = }")
# y1 = Model(v=<E.A: 'A'>)

y2 = Model.model_validate_json('{"v": "C"}')
print(f"{y2 = }")
# missing: cls = <enum 'E'> - value = None
# y2 = Model(v=<E._FALLBACK: 'FALLBACK'>)
```

title: Multiple subsettings that share same base model
BaseModel attributes can overshadow builtin types in Python 3.14
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a subclass of `BaseModel` defines attributes (methods) with names already existing in the global namespace (such as `dict`), those attribute names can shadow the built-in classes such as `int`, `str`, `dict`, etc.

I understand that it is not a particularly good practice to name methods with built-in or existing names, but this may exist in production code since this is not namespace pollution (we would be redefining for example`BaseModel.str`, not `str`)

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Entity(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(default="")
    attributes: dict = Field(default_factory=dict)

    def str(self):
        return f"name={self.name},attributes={self.attributes}"

    def dict(self):
        return self.model_dump(mode="python")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    entity_obj = Entity(name="Trenza1ore", attributes=dict(age=-1))
    print(f"Serialize as a dict: {entity_obj.dict()}")
    print(f"Serialize as a str: {entity_obj.str()}")
```

title: Please consider adding `require-runtime-dependencies = true` to pyproject.toml
body:
This lets hatchling resolve and match constraints for `dependencies` and error if they don't match during build/install.

Since the 2.12.5 pyproject.toml is pinning pydantic-core

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/bd2d0dd0137dfa1a8fdff2529b9dfb1547980150/pyproject.toml#L51

this is especially needed as otherwise, pydantic will happily build and install a wheel with pydantic-core 2.42.0 for example

With the hook, it will error if constraints cannot be matched:

```
    + sh -c -e $'python -P -mbuild --no-isolation --wheel --outdir /buildstream/freedesktop-sdk-builder/pydantic.bst/dist  .\n'
    * Getting build dependencies for wheel...

    ERROR Missing dependencies:
    	pydantic-core==2.41.5
    	typing-inspection>=0.4.2
    	annotated-types>=0.6.0
    Command 'python -P -mbuild --no-isolation --wheel --outdir /buildstream/freedesktop-sdk-builder/pydantic.bst/dist  .' failed with exitcode 1
```

I'm not experienced in hatchling but something like this seems to work:

```patch
--- ./pyproject.toml	2020-02-02 05:30:00.000000000 +0530
+++ ./pyproject.toml	2026-03-22 20:03:22.445810040 +0530
@@ -300,3 +300,6 @@
 test-framework = "pytest"
 ignore-paths = []
 formatter-cmd = ["ruff check --exit-zero --fix $file", "ruff format $file"]
+
+[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
+require-runtime-dependencies = true
```


Context. https://gitlab.com/freedesktop-sdk/infrastructure/freedesktop-sdk-docker-images/-/merge_requests/704

title: docs: Clarify behavior of model_validator with mode='before' when input is not a dict
body:
## Documentation Gap

`@model_validator(mode='before')` receives the raw input, which may not be a dict (e.g., when constructing from another model instance, a list, or a primitive). The current documentation does not clearly explain how to handle non-dict inputs safely.

## Current Behavior

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    value: int

    @model_validator(mode='before')
    @classmethod
    def check_input(cls, data):
        # data may be a dict OR a MyModel instance OR something else
        # this is not clearly documented
        if isinstance(data, dict):
            data['value'] = abs(data.get('value', 0))
        return data
```

## Suggested Documentation Addition

A note explaining that `mode='before'` receives the raw value before coercion, and that validators should handle at minimum: `dict`, `model instance`, and the case where `data` is `None`.

This would prevent a common class of runtime errors where validators assume `data` is always a dict.

Is a PR to the validators documentation page the right approach here?

title: Generic model with bounded type fails validation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Already reported in #7308 with a slightly different use case. Feel free to close this one, i just didn't want to necro an old issue

When a model uses a bounded generic type, arguments in the model are allowed to use any of the types from the bound, unless explicitly overridden in the call.

Only a single type, among the ones defined in the bounds, should be allowed in the model

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class A[T: (int, str)](BaseModel):
    a: list[T]


A(a=[1, "a"])  # passes though it should fail
A[int](a=[1, "a"])  # workaround, validation fails
```

title: Extra fields not dumped by `model_dump`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

A new feature was added to Pydantic 2.12 in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12233 to change the behaviour of `extra` when calling `model_validate`. However, this is not reflected when calling `model_dump` on that validated data.

Here is a trivial example
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class A(BaseModel, extra="forbid"):
    a: int

# Normally forbid this class from having any extra fields,
# but in this one instance, allow extra fields
a = A.model_validate({"a": 1, "b": "test"}, extra="allow")
print(a)  # A(a=1, b='test')

# However, if we dump this object, the extra fields are not included
print(a.model_dump())  # {'a': 1}

# Even if we explicitly say to include the extra field
print(a.model_dump(include=["a", "b"]))  # {'a': 1}
```

This feels like a bug. We should honour the `extra` override from the `model_validate` for this particular instance of the class, but we are not.

There is a kind of dirty way to workaround this, which is to use a custom serializer
```python
class A(BaseModel, extra="forbid"):
    a: int = 1

    @model_serializer(mode="wrap")
    def serialize(self, handler):
        serialized = handler(self)
        if self.model_extra:
            serialized.update(TypeAdapter(dict).dump_python(self.model_extra))
        return serialized
```
But this is a less than ideal solution as now you have to add this serializer to every class for which you want this behaviour.

Ideally, we should be able to honour the `extra` override from `model_validate` when we call `model_dump` or `model_dump_json` on that object

### Example Code

```Python

```

title: field_validator in Pydantic_dataclass not inherited from parent
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When I make Base a @dataclass then the validators are not working in 3 children, but when I make Base a regular class, the validators work just fine. Also alias are not propagated to BaseEntity [issue](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12933)

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import  BaseModel, field_serializer, Field, ConfigDict, field_validator



model_con = ConfigDict(
        populate_by_name=True,
        arbitrary_types_allowed=True,
    )

@dataclass(config=model_con) #Remove this and check
class Base:
    id: Optional[str] = Field(alias="_id", default=None)
    
    @field_validator('id')
    def id_has_5(cls, v:str):
        print("In validator")
        if '5' not in v:
            raise ValueError('Error in id')
        return v

    @field_serializer("id")
    def serialize_objectid(self, value: str) -> str:
        print("In Serializer")
        return f'{str(value)} is the Id'


BaseDc = dataclass(Base, config=model_con)


class BaseEntity(BaseModel, Base): # _Base or _BaseDc, both not working
    model_config=model_con
    
    ...



@dataclass
class BaseDataClass(Base):
    ...

test_id = "34324nhjljk4"
try:
    b = BaseEntity(**{"id":test_id})
    print("1. BaseEntity: ok", b)
    b = BaseEntity(**{"_id":test_id})
    print("2. BaseEntity: ok", b)
except Exception as e:

try:
    b = BaseDataClass(**{"id":test_id})
    print("1. BaseDataClass: ok", b)
    b = BaseDataClass(**{"_id":test_id})
    print("2. BaseDataClass: ok", b)
    b = BaseDataClass(**{"_id":test_id})
    print("2. BaseDataClass: ok", b)

except Exception as e:
    print(f"7. BaseDataClass: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

    b = BaseDc(**{"id":test_id})
    print("1. BaseDc: ok", b)


except Exception as e:
    print(f"7. BaseDc: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

Im using Beanie hence in the end I need a BaseModel, but internally in the code I want to use dataclass for transfers etc. Im aware of TypeAdapter though. 
As most of the features are supported by both, I defined the common fields first and then created its Dataclass and BaseModel.

I see serializers, validators(in strange case [issue](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12934)) working fine . But facing issue with the alias.

title: Breaking change in `2.12.0` related to alias generator behavior
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The example provided works in Pydantic version `2.11.7` but breaks in Pydantic version `2.12.0`. It is a simplified version of code found in my codebase.

The class `AlwaysWorks` works in both versions, allowing the `to_pascal` alias generator to convert the name as needed between `MyTest` and `my_test`.

The class `BreaksInPydantic_2_12` works in version `2.11.7`, producing the same result as `AlwaysWorks`, but in `2.12.0`, it raises a `ValidationError`:

```
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for BreaksInPydantic_2_12
my_test
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'MyTest': 1}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/missing
```

I am looking for workarounds to produce the original functionality (namely a mixin that adds the alias generator functionality). I am also curious what changed to break this as I didn't expect breaking changes between these two minor versions.

Thank you!

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import AliasGenerator, BaseModel, ConfigDict
from pydantic.alias_generators import to_pascal


class PascalCaseMixin:
    def __init_subclass__(cls, **kwargs):
        super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)
        if cls.model_config:
            cls.model_config["alias_generator"] = AliasGenerator(
                validation_alias=to_pascal,
                serialization_alias=to_pascal,
            )
        else:
            cls.model_config = ConfigDict(
                alias_generator=AliasGenerator(
                    validation_alias=to_pascal,
                    serialization_alias=to_pascal,
                )
            )


class BreaksInPydantic_2_12(BaseModel, PascalCaseMixin):
    my_test: int


class AlwaysWorks(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(
        alias_generator=AliasGenerator(
            validation_alias=to_pascal,
            serialization_alias=to_pasc

title: API Breakage: model_rebuild() crashes with TypeError due to mismatched SchemaValidator.new() signature
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

This issue was detected by our testing tool and confirmed after discussion among multiple developers. 
A critical breaking change was introduced in PR #12689 ("Do not re-use prebuilt serializers/validators on rebuilds"), where a new _use_prebuilt keyword parameter was added to the Rust implementation of SchemaValidator.new(), but this parameter was not correctly exposed to the Python runtime. As a result, calls to BaseModel.model_rebuild() and PluggableSchemaValidator immediately raise a TypeError: SchemaValidator.__new__() got an unexpected keyword argument '_use_prebuilt', breaking all existing code that relies on these core APIs. The expected behavior is that model_rebuild() should execute without errors, and SchemaValidator should properly accept the _use_prebuilt parameter at runtime, avoiding such breaking changes to public interfaces.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

# Minimal reproduction example
class SimpleModel(BaseModel):
    name: str
    value: int

# This call directly triggers the crash
SimpleModel.model_rebuild()
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.9.0.dev (git commit from PR #12689)
python version: 3.10.13
platform: Ubuntu 22.04
```

title: Regression(?) in Annotated string type with BeforeValidator and PlainSerializer from 2.11.7 to 2.12.5
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

We observed a behavior change after upgrading from Pydantic 2.11.7 to 2.12.5 that affects a custom Annotated string type used in ORM-backed models.

Versions

Works: pydantic==2.11.7, pydantic-core==2.33.2
Fails/changes behavior: pydantic==2.12.5, pydantic-core==2.41.5
Python: 3.12
Related stack: SQLModel 0.0.24, SQLAlchemy 2.0.48, GeoAlchemy2 0.18.4
Pattern
We used a custom alias like:
```py
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BeforeValidator, PlainSerializer

def parse_wkb(wkb: WKBElement | str) -> str:
    """
    Parse WKBElement to WKT string, or validate and pass through if already a string.

    This function handles bidirectional conversion:
    - WKBElement (from database) -> WKT string (for serialization)
    - WKT string (from input) -> validated WKT string (with polygon validation)
    """
    if isinstance(wkb, str):
        # Validate WKT string before passing to database
        return validate_wkt_multipolygon(wkb)
    try:
        return to_shape(wkb).wkt
    except (TypeError, AttributeError) as e:
        raise ValueError(f"Invalid geometry data: {e}") from e

WKT = Annotated[
    str,
    BeforeValidator(parse_wkb),
    PlainSerializer(parse_wkb, return_type=str, when_used="always"),
]
```
and then:
```py
class L1B(SQLModel, table=True):
    geom: WKT = Field(sa_column=Column(Geometry("MULTIPOLYGON", srid=4326)))
```

Observed issue
After upgrading to 2.12.5, this path no longer behaved like 2.11.7 in our model flow. In our ORM context, the geometry-backed field effectively degraded to string behavior and caused downstream breakage. Under 2.11.7, same pattern behaved correctly.

Workaround
We replaced the Annotated alias approach with explicit field-level hooks:
```py
class L1B(SQLModel, table=True):
    geom: str = Field(sa_column=Column(Geometry("MULTIPOLYGON", srid=4326)))

    @field_validator("geom", mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def validate

title: `model_dump_json` of a model with a (discriminated) union is slow
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`MyModel.model_dump_json` does not seem to short-circuit correctly when `MyModel` contains a `Union` even in cases where determining the matching union member is trivial (such as in discriminated unions).

Other observations:

- Union serialization tries every variant's schema even when the concrete type is already known via `model_construct`.
- Discriminated unions show zero benefit over plain unions for serialization
- The cost scales linearly with the size of the data in the matching variant - `O(n * num_variants)` instead of `O(n)`.

Output of the reproducer below on my machine:

```bash
$ python pydantic_union_serialization.py
python 3.12.7 (main, Oct  1 2024, 02:05:46) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.3)]
pydantic 2.12.5  pydantic-core 2.41.5

--- 10,000 element list ---
  no union (baseline)            0.59 ms  (1.0x vs baseline)
  discriminated union            2.00 ms  (3.4x vs baseline)
  plain union                    1.99 ms  (3.4x vs baseline)

--- 100,000 element list ---
  no union (baseline)            6.01 ms  (1.0x vs baseline)
  discriminated union           20.02 ms  (3.3x vs baseline)
  plain union                   20.20 ms  (3.4x vs baseline)

--- 1,000,000 element list ---
  no union (baseline)           57.99 ms  (1.0x vs baseline)
  discriminated union          198.63 ms  (3.4x vs baseline)
  plain union                  200.45 ms  (3.5x vs baseline)
```

### Example Code

import time

    buffer: StrictStr
    encoding: Literal["a"]


class StrDataB(BaseModel):
    """Another str variant."""

title: Improve performance of string JSON serialization by skipping escaping
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`model_dump_json` is ~3x slower than `model_validate_json` for models containing large strings. We frequently encounter this bottleneck in [Tesseract Core](https://github.com/pasteurlabs/tesseract-core) where we serialize (potentially large) arrays as base64-encoded strings.

The root cause appears to be `serde_json`'s string serialization doing a full UTF-8 scan + JSON-escape check + buffer copy, whereas jiter's string parsing is significantly faster.

For payloads where the string is known to be ASCII-safe (e.g. base64), this overhead is pure waste.

Output of the reproducer below on my machine:

```bash
$ python pydantic_serde_asymmetry.py

title: [Bug]: Performance regression with nested models in Pydantic v2
body:
## Environment Information
- Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04 / macOS Sonoma / Windows 11
- Programming Language: Python 3.11
- Pydantic Version: 2.x

## Problem Description
Pydantic v2 shows significant performance regression when working with deeply nested model structures compared to v1. Model validation and serialization become extremely slow with more than 10 levels of nesting.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Create a deeply nested Pydantic model structure (15+ levels)
2. Validate data against the model
3. Observe significant performance degradation

## Expected Behavior
Validation time should scale linearly or near-linearly with nesting depth, similar to Pydantic v1 behavior.

## Actual Behavior
- Validation time increases exponentially with nesting depth
- 15-level nested model takes 5+ seconds to validate
- Memory usage spikes dramatically
- Same data validates in <100ms with Pydantic v1

## Error Log
```python
# Benchmark with 15-level nesting:
import time

start = time.time()
model = NestedModel(**data)
end = time.time()
print(f"Validation time: {end - start:.2f}s")  # 5.23s in v2 vs 0.08s in v1
```

## Minimal Reproduction Code
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

def create_nested_model(depth):
    if depth == 0:
        return type('Leaf', (BaseModel,), {'value': str})
    
    child = create_nested_model(depth - 1)
    return type(f'Level{15-depth}', (BaseModel,), {'child': child})

NestedModel = create_nested_model(15)
data = {'child': {'child': ... {'value': 'test'}}}
model = NestedModel(**data)
```

## Initial Analysis
1. The new v2 validation algorithm may have different complexity characteristics
2. Nested model caching may not be working correctly
3. Schema generation for deep structures may be inefficient

## Suggested Solutions
1. Investigate and optimize the nested model validation path
2. Add caching for repeated nested model schemas
3. Consider adding a warning for very deep nesting (>10 levels)
4. Profile and identify the specific bottleneck i

title: RootModel emmits int and breaks logic used by pyright
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi,

It seems that when using `RootModel` there is a type mismatch that makes pyright throw an error

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import RootModel

class MVAWp(RootModel):
    root: float | tuple[float, float]

    def __str__(self):
        if isinstance(self.root, float):
            return f'{self.root:.3f}'
        else:
            low, high = self.root  # Pyright error: "int" is not iterable
            return f'{low:.3f}-{high:.3f}'
```

title: Add `exclude_extra` option to `model_dump` and `model_dump_json`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

If a pydantic base model allows extra fields, they get included in `model_dump` by default, e.g.
```python
class A(BaseModel, extra="allow"):
    a: int

a = A(a=1, b="test")
print(a)  # A(a=1, b='test')
print(a.model_dump())  # {'a': 1, 'b': 'test'}
```
There are cases where we might want to exclude the extra fields from being dumped. The requested feature being something like
```python
print(a.model_dump(exclude_extra=True))  # {'a': 1}
```
`exclude_extra` would be `False` by default preserving the current behaviour.

Technically, this can be done today by doing something like
```python
a_dict = a.model_dump()
for k in a.model_extra:
    a_dict.pop(k)
```
But this is cumbersome, especially if doing `model_dump_json` since you have to deserialize, remove extra fields and then reserialize the dict, which defeats the point of calling `model_dump_json`. Having the option to exclude the extra fields to begin with would be nice.


### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [x] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)

title: ignored_types doesn't work with typing
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The ignored_types configuration option in Pydantic models is intended to prevent certain types of class attributes from being treated as fields. However, its effect is limited to attributes that do not have a type annotation. Attributes that are annotated (even if their value is an instance of a type listed in ignored_types) are still collected as fields.

### Example Code

```Python
class _ClassPropertyDescriptor:
    def __init__(self, getter):
        self.getter = getter

    def __get__(self, instance, owner):
        return self.getter(owner)

classproperty = _ClassPropertyDescriptor

class Model(BaseModel):
    # it doesn't work
    class_name_1: str = classproperty(lambda cls: str(cls.__name__))
    # it work
    class_name_2 = classproperty(lambda cls: cls.__name__)

    # it work
    @classproperty
    def class_name_3(cls) -> str:
        return cls.__name__

    model_config = ConfigDict(
        ignored_types=(classproperty,)
    )


a = Model()
a.model_dump()
# {"class_name_1": "Model"}
```

title: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) produces spurious warnings for Union members with unset defaults
body:
## Description

`model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` on a model containing a `Union` field produces spurious `PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue` warnings when a union member has unset fields with defaults.

## Reproduction

```python
from typing import Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel


class Cat(BaseModel):
    type: Literal['cat']
    color: str | None = None  # field with default


class Dog(BaseModel):
    type: Literal['dog']


class Zoo(BaseModel):
    animals: list[Cat | Dog]


cat = Cat(type='cat')
zoo = Zoo(animals=[cat])

# Sanity check: model_dump without exclude_unset works fine
assert zoo.model_dump() == {'animals': [{'type': 'cat', 'color': None}]}

# Sanity check: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) on the Cat alone works fine
assert cat.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) == {'type': 'cat'}

# BUG: model_dump(exclude_unset=True) on the parent Zoo produces warnings
assert zoo.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) == {'animals': [{'type': 'cat'}]}
```

The last line produces:

```
UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected 2 fields but got 1: Expected `Cat` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='animals', ...])
  PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `Dog` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='animals', ...])
```

The output is correct, but the warnings are spurious.

## Version info

- **Broken**: pydantic 2.13.0b2 / pydantic-core 2.42.0 (installed from latest on github)
- **Works**: pydantic 2.12.5 / pydantic-core 2.41.5
- Python 3.12

title: Cannot override __items__ with RootModel without typechecker error.
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am seeing an error from pyright when I do:

```python
class YieldsConf(RootModel):
    root: dict[str, YieldConf]

    def __iter__(self):  # type: ignore[override]
        return iter(self.root.items())

    def __contains__(self, item: object) -> bool:
        return item in self.root

    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> YieldConf:
        return self.root[key]
```

without the type ignore. This is the same issue as:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10954

So either

- There is a missing DictModel, in which case this is a feature request
- This is a bug

From:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10954#issuecomment-2495759230

I take that we are not supposed to implement dunders with pydantic. From:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10954#issuecomment-2499010672

I get that now it's the user's job to go into the source code of pydantic and find out if our dunder will break anything.

I do not think It should be the user's job to go into pydantics source code. 
Either:

- This should be allowed and pyright should not complain
- This should not be allowed and there should be a model, e.g. DictModel, ContainerModel, etc
- We would write our own model from BaseModel


### Example Code

```Python
class YieldsConf(RootModel):
    root: dict[str, YieldConf]

    def __iter__(self):  # type: ignore[override]
        return iter(self.root.items())

    def __contains__(self, item: object) -> bool:
        return item in self.root

    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> YieldConf:
        return self.root[key]
```

title: Support `memoryview` in `model_validate_json`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

[model_validate_json](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/base_model/#pydantic.BaseModel.model_validate_json) currently only accepts `str | bytes | bytearray` as the type of the input JSON data. For zero-copy-ish workloads it would be great to also support `memoryview`, so one doesn't need to copy the JSON data just to have it validated.

For reference, this is the error one currently gets when trying to pass a `memoryview` to `model_validate_json`:

```
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for X
  JSON input should be string, bytes or bytearray [type=json_type, input_value=<memory at 0x110d693c0>, input_type=memoryview]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.10/v/json_type
```

title: extra='allow' can let alias/name collisions overwrite validated field output during serialization
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a model uses extra='allow' and a field has an alias, providing both an alias key and its corresponding field-name key in the input leads to inconsistent serialization. The alias value is used as the declared field value, while the field-name value remains in model_extra. During serialization, the extra entry collides with the declared field's output key. As a result:

  - model_dump() overwrites the validated field value with the extra value
  - model_dump_json() emits both values under the field-name key

Expected behavior: serialization would not overwrite the resolved field value with extra data

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field


class Model(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra='allow')
    x: int = Field(alias='X')


payload = {'X': 1, 'x': 2}
model = Model.model_validate(payload)

print('field value:', model.x)
print('extra:', model.model_extra)
print('model_dump():', model.model_dump())
print('model_dump_json():', model.model_dump_json())
```

title: revalidate_instances='never' still reruns model_validator(mode='after') for existing model instances passed to nested model fields
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When revalidate_instances='never' is set on a model, passing an existing instance of that model to a nested model field still reruns model_validator(mode='after'). In my repro, that validator runs again during parent-model validation and mutates the original object in place. This seems inconsistent with the "Configuration for Pydantic models" documentation for revalidate_instances='never', which says models and dataclasses are not revalidated during validation.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import ClassVar

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator
from typing_extensions import Self


class Child(BaseModel, revalidate_instances='never'):
    x: int
    calls: ClassVar[int] = 0

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def bump(self) -> Self:
        Child.calls += 1
        self.x += 1
        return self


class Parent(BaseModel):
    child: Child


child = Child(x=1)
Child.calls = 0
parent = Parent(child=child)
print('after', Child.calls, parent.child is child, child.x)
```

title: Title: `AttributeError` in `__repr__` when instantiating unparametrized generic model with `BaseModel` bound
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a generic Pydantic model uses `BaseModel` as a type hint bound, instantiating the model without parameterization succeeds, but attempting to print or `repr()` the resulting object causes an `AttributeError`.
This appears to be because Pydantic defaults the type to the literal `pydantic.BaseModel` class, which does not have the necessary internal attributes (like `__private_attributes__`) initialized to support Pydantic's internal `__repr__` logic.
Minimal Reproducible Example:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo[T: BaseModel](BaseModel):
    x: T

# Validation succeeds, but the resulting object is "broken"
m = Foo(x={}) 

# This triggers the AttributeError
print(m)
```

Traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
  File "pydantic/main.py", line 1237, in __repr__
    return f'{self.__repr_name__()}({self.__repr_str__(", ")})'
  File "pydantic/_internal/_repr.py", line 62, in __repr_str__
    return join_str.join(repr(v) if a is None else f'{a}={v!r}' for a, v in self.__repr_args__())
  ...
  File "pydantic/main.py", line 1000, in __getattr__
    private_attributes = object.__getattribute__(self, '__private_attributes__')
AttributeError: 'BaseModel' object has no attribute '__private_attributes__'. Did you mean: '__static_attributes__'?
```

Expected Behavior:
Either:
Pydantic should prevent `BaseModel` from being instantiated as a field value during validation.
The `__repr__` logic should be guarded to handle raw `BaseModel` instances without crashing.
Environment:
Python Version: 3.13
Pydantic Version: 2.12.5

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo[T: BaseModel](BaseModel):
    x: T

# Validation succeeds, but the resulting object is "broken"
m = Foo(x={}) 

# This triggers the AttributeError
print(m)
```

        pydantic-core version: 2.41.5
          pyd

title: `validate_as` doesn't accept Pydantic's own `Annotated` types (`NewPath`, `FilePath`, etc.)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`validate_as` works at runtime with `NewPath`, `FilePath`, etc., but the type signature only accepts `type[T]` or `EllipsisType`. 

<details>
<summary>Error (collapsed)</summary>

No overloads for "validate_as" match the provided arguments (Pylance [reportCallIssue](https://github.com/microsoft/pylance-release/blob/main/docs/diagnostics/reportCallIssue.md))

pipeline.py(149, 9): Overload 2 is the closest match

Argument of type "NewPath" cannot be assigned to parameter "tp" of type "EllipsisType" in function "validate_as"

  "Annotated" is not assignable to "EllipsisType" (Pylance [reportArgumentType](https://github.com/microsoft/pylance-release/blob/main/docs/diagnostics/reportArgumentType.md))

</details>


Since Pydantic's own path types are `Annotated[Path, ...]` aliases, they don't satisfy `type[T]`, causing type checker errors. 

Maybe the overload signatures could be broadened to accept annotated types for use in validation chains?


### Example Code

```Python
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import FilePath, NewPath, TypeAdapter
from pydantic.experimental.pipeline import transform

type InputFile = Annotated[FilePath, transform(Path.resolve)]
input_file = TypeAdapter[InputFile](InputFile).validate_python

type OutputPath = Annotated[
    Path,
    transform(lambda p: p.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) or p) # make the parent(s)
    .validate_as(NewPath)  # "Annotated" is not assignable to "EllipsisType" (Pylance)
    .transform(Path.resolve),
]
output_path = TypeAdapter[OutputPath](OutputPath).validate_python
```

body:

### Description

New type aliases prefixed with the `type` keyword are not supported as the type annotation for a callable discriminator.

Related:
- #11930
- #11941


### Example Code

```Python
import random
from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Discriminator, Field, Tag, TypeAdapter

discriminator = Discriminator(lambda _: random.choice(["a", "b", None]))
OldUnion = Annotated[int, Tag("a")] | Annotated[str, Tag("b")]
type NewUnion = Annotated[int, Tag("a")] | Annotated[str, Tag("b")]

TypeAdapter[OldUnion](Annotated[OldUnion, discriminator]) # OK
TypeAdapter[NewUnion](Annotated[NewUnion, discriminator]) # TypeError: Discriminator must be used with a Union type, not NewUnion

class M(BaseModel):
    u1: OldUnion = Field(discriminator=discriminator) # OK
    u2: NewUnion = Field(discriminator=discriminator) # pydantic.errors.PydanticUserError: `Tag` not provided for choice {'type': 'definition-ref', 'schema_ref': '__main__.NewUnion:140737344689696'} used with `Discriminator`
```

title: Dynamic models docs example uses wrong variable for model name
body:
In the `make_field_optional` [example ](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/examples/dynamic_models/) in docs/examples/dynamic_models.md, the `create_model` call uses `type.__name__` to generate the new model's name, but the function parameter is `model_cls`.

This means it references Python's built-in type rather than the passed-in model class, and the generated model always will be named "typeOptional" instead of e.g. "UserOptional":

```python
    return create_model(
        f'{type.__name__}Optional',
        __base__=model_cls,  
        **new_fields,
    )
```

title: Spurious Serialization Warnings with List of Discriminated Union
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm seeing unexpected warnings when dumping a **validated** model using `model_dump` and `model_dump_json`. This occurs on a model with a field that is a `list[SomeDiscriminatedUnion]`. See the minimal example below. It is odd that these warnings occur only during serialization, but never during deserialization. 

Presumably this warning serves to warn of a developer error in defining the models or some kind of issue with the model content. Based on the second case below, it doesn't not seem to be an intrinsic issue with the models. However, in all cases, the content is validated. So I would expect all of the code blocks below to serialize without a warning.

Seems like a good candidate for this milestone https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/milestone/13 

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated, Literal, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class ModelA(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["A"] = "A"
    value_a: str


class ModelB(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["B"] = "B"
    value_b: int


model_classes = {ModelA, ModelB}

ModelUnion = Annotated[Union[tuple(model_classes)], Field(discriminator="type")]


class ContainerModel(BaseModel):
    model: list[ModelUnion] = Field(default_factory=list)


def validate_and_serialize(container: ContainerModel):
    validated = ContainerModel.model_validate(container)
    return validated.model_dump_json()


# This emits a warning (thus the issue)
container_instance = ContainerModel(model=[])
container_instance.model.append({"type": "A", "value_a": "Hello"})
validate_and_serialize(container_instance)

# This does not emit a warning, so editing the model isn't the problem
container_instance = ContainerModel(model=[])
a_instance = ModelA(value_a="Hello")  # This does not incur a warning
container_instance.model.append(a_instance)

# This does not emit a warning either. Perhaps there is some difference in validation of ContainerMo

title: Stdlib dataclass custom docstrings suppressed from JSON schema description
body:
source https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/pull/4138#discussion_r2818450381

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic suppresses `__doc__` from stdlib dataclasses in JSON schema generation to avoid auto-generated signature docstrings (e.g. `'Foo(x: int = 1)'`) leaking as `description`. However, the suppression doesn't distinguish between auto-generated and custom docstrings — explicit docstrings are also dropped.

This is inconsistent with Pydantic dataclasses, which DO get their custom docstrings included.

The suppression is in `GenerateJsonSchema.dataclass_schema` (`pydantic/json_schema.py`):

```python
if is_stdlib_dataclass(cls):
    # vanilla dataclass; don't use cls.__doc__ as it will contain the class signature by default
    description = None
```

And similarly in `_update_class_schema`:

```python
docstring = None if cls is BaseModel or dataclasses.is_dataclass(cls) else cls.__doc__
```

Python makes it straightforward to distinguish auto-generated from custom docstrings — the auto-generated ones start with `f'{cls.__name__}('`.

This became more visible after #11822 fixed `is_stdlib_dataclass` (previously `is_builtin_dataclass`), which had a bug where dataclasses with `KW_ONLY` sentinel annotations were not recognized as stdlib dataclasses and accidentally got their docstrings preserved.

### Example Code

```python
# /// script
# requires-python = '>=3.10'
# dependencies = [
#     'pydantic>=2.12',
# ]
# ///
import dataclasses

import pydantic
import pydantic.dataclasses
from pydantic import TypeAdapter


@dataclasses.dataclass
class StdlibNoDoc:
    x: int = 1


@dataclasses.dataclass
class StdlibWithDoc:
    """A custom docstring that should appear as 'description' in JSON schema."""

    x: int = 1


@pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass
class PydanticWithDoc:
    """A custom docstring that should appear as 'description' in JSON schema."""

    x: int = 1


print(f'pydantic=={pydantic.__version__}')
prin

title: `typed_dict_schema` JSON schema ignores `extras_schema` when `extra_fields_behavior` is set via config
body:
source: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/pull/4138#discussion_r2814292364

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

`GenerateJsonSchema.typed_dict_schema()` correctly uses `extras_schema` to produce typed `additionalProperties` when `extra_behavior` is set as a **top-level** field on the schema, but ignores it when `extra_fields_behavior` is set via the **config** dict — always producing `additionalProperties: true` instead of the typed schema.

This was partially addressed by #10785 / #12123, but only the top-level `extra_behavior` path was fixed. The `config` fallback path (the `elif 'additionalProperties' not in json_schema` branch at the bottom of `typed_dict_schema`) sets `True` without consulting `extras_schema`:

```python
# pydantic/json_schema.py — GenerateJsonSchema.typed_dict_schema()
elif 'additionalProperties' not in json_schema:
    extra = schema.get('config', {a}).get('extra_fields_behavior')
    if extra == 'forbid':
        json_schema['additionalProperties'] = False
    elif extra == 'allow':
        json_schema['additionalProperties'] = True  # <-- ignores extras_schema
```

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic_core import core_schema
from pydantic.json_schema import GenerateJsonSchema

# Top-level extra_behavior — CORRECT
schema_top = core_schema.typed_dict_schema(
    fields={'x': core_schema.typed_dict_field(core_schema.int_schema())},
    extra_behavior='allow',
    extras_schema=core_schema.int_schema(),
)
result_top = GenerateJsonSchema().generate(schema_top)
print(result_top['additionalProperties'])
# {'type': 'integer'} ✓

# Config extra_fields_behavior — BUG
schema_cfg = core_schema.typed_dict_schema(
    fields={'x': core_schema.typed_dict_field(core_schema.int_schema())},
    config=core_schema.CoreConfig(extra_fields_behavior='allow'),
    extras_schema=core_schema.int_schema(),
)
result_cfg = GenerateJsonSchema().generate(schema_cfg)
print(result_cfg['additionalProperties'])
# True

title: Use a distinct error type for `EmailStr` validation instead of generic `value_error`
body:
Currently, the built-in `EmailStr` validator emits errors with:

```json
{
  "type": "value_error",
  "msg": "value is not a valid email address: …",
  "ctx": { "reason": "…" }
}
```

Seems like `EmailStr` is the only validator that reuses the generic `value_error` type while putting the specific failure message under `ctx.reason`, and not `ctx.error`.

Other Pydantic built-ins define distinct error types (e.g., `color_error`, `byte_size_unit`) that map cleanly to translation keys and associated context variables.

#

I am mapping Pydantic validation errors on the frontend with i18next ICU templates for translations and more user-friendly messages. The ICU templates expect `ctx.error` for interpolation of `"value_error": "{error}"`.

Using the generic `value_error` for email validation means:

* You cannot differentiate email errors from other `value_error` cases based solely on `type`.

* i18next mappings keyed on `value_error` expect `ctx.error`, but email errors place the message in `ctx.reason`, forcing workaround logic in frontend parsers.

#

Viable workarounds are either:

* Create a subclass and change the `type`, like described in this comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/8914#issuecomment-1972453944

* Explicitly handle `value_error` on the frontend with a special case for `ctx.reason` or `ctx.error`.

#

Proposal:

* Change the default `value_error` to a distinct error type, like `email_error`.

### Description

Using the minimal example provided.

My goal is to get the methods of the class `DummyBaseModel`. In particular `dummy_method`.

I can find `dummy_method` in the list `methods` when I call `methods = getmembers(obj, ismethod)`. But a pydantic deprecation warning is raised.

So I try to do like asked by the warning : `methods = getmembers(type(obj), ismethod)`. In this case, the warning disappears, but I no longer have the `dummy_method` returned in the list.

How can I get the expected behavior (meaning I want `dummy_method` to be listed) without the deprecation warning ? 
Can you help ?  

NB : I use `getmembers` because it works with python classes and python dataclasses.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from inspect import ismethod, getmembers


class DummyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    name: str

    def dummy_method(self, input):
        return input


def test_minimal():
    obj = DummyBaseModel(name="my dummy base model")

    methods = getmembers(type(obj), ismethod) # <- "dummy_method" is not listed ...

title: Allow model-like types as fields to be serialized as instances/as-is
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am trying to serialize a model that contains a dataclass with `model_dump` in such a way that leaves the dataclass as it is. I have tried multiple approaches, but in the end the dataclass is always converted to its dictionary format.

Based on the [serialization documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization) I have made some attempts:

### 1) `field_serializer`
A `field_serializer` that returns the object itself
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import BaseModel, field_serializer

@dataclass
class MyData:
    x: int
    y: int

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    data: MyData

    @field_serializer("data", mode='plain', when_used="always")
    def serialize_data(self, value):
        print("inside serialize", value)
        return value

dc = MyData(1, 2)
m = MyModel(data=dc)

dump = m.model_dump()
print(dump)
print(type(dump["data"]))
```
The output is:
> inside serialize MyData(x=1, y=2)
{'data': {'x': 1, 'y': 2}}
<class 'dict'>

While I would like to have 
> {'data': MyData(x=1, y=2)}

### 2) `SerializeAsAny`

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny

@dataclass
class MyData:
    x: int
    y: int

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    data: SerializeAsAny[MyData]

dc = MyData(1, 2)
m = MyModel(data=dc)

dump = m.model_dump()
print(dump)
print(type(dump["data"]))
```

The output is again
> {'data': {'x': 1, 'y': 2}}
<class 'dict'>

### 3) `serialize_model`
Even serializing the whole model
```python

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_serializer

@dataclass
class MyData:
    x: int

class MyModel(BaseModel):

    @model_serializer(mode="wrap")

        result = handler(self) 
        result["data"] = self.data
        print("inside serialize:", result)

title: Tagged union type alias causes validators to run for all union members
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

It seems like using `type Pet = Cat | Dog`, and then using `Pet` instead of `Cat | Dog` directly, causes a change in behaviour of validation of discriminating unions.

### Example Code

A stripped-down version of the official [example](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/unions/#discriminated-unions-with-str-discriminators):

```python
from typing import Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, model_validator


class Cat(BaseModel):
    pet_type: Literal["cat"]

    @model_validator(mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def validate_cat(cls, data: Any) -> Any:
        print(f"Validating {cls} with {data}")
        return data


class Dog(BaseModel):
    pet_type: Literal["dog"]

    @model_validator(mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def validate_dog(cls, data: Any) -> Any:
        print(f"Validating {cls} with {data}")
        return data


class GoodModel(BaseModel):
    pet: Cat | Dog = Field(
        discriminator="pet_type",
    )


print("The good")
GoodModel.model_validate({"pet": {"pet_type": "cat"}})
GoodModel.model_validate({"pet": {"pet_type": "dog"}})
```

The output is as expected:

```
The good
Validating <class '__main__.Cat'> with {'pet_type': 'cat'}
Validating <class '__main__.Dog'> with {'pet_type': 'dog'}
```

But let's suppose I'm using `Cat | Dog` elsewhere, and I want to introduce a name to refer to pets in general:

```python
type Pet = Cat | Dog


class BadModel(BaseModel):
    pet: Pet = Field(
        discriminator="pet_type",
    )


print("The bad")
BadModel.model_validate({"pet": {"pet_type": "cat"}})
BadModel.model_validate({"pet": {"pet_type": "dog"}})
```

The introduction of this alias, which I expected to be a no-op, causes `validate_cat` to be called with an instance of dog data, and vice versa:

```
The bad
Validating <class '__main__.Cat'> with {'pet_type': 'cat'}
Validating <class '__main__.Dog'> with {'pet_type': 'cat'}
Validating

title: dataclass+specialised generic does not reject bad data
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Please see the example below:

- a pydantic dataclass that has been specialised to some type will still accept data of any type for the params that use the Generic type
- it is possible to get the correct behaviour by using TypeAdapter and the undocumented object `pydantic_core.ArgsKwargs`

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic
from typing import TypeVar, Generic

T = TypeVar("T")
    
@pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass
class GenericModel(Generic[T]):
    foo: T
    
# works, but should raise ValidationError
print(GenericModel[bool](3))  
    
# correctly raises ValidationError
pydantic.TypeAdapter(GenericModel[bool]).validate_python(pydantic_core.ArgsKwargs((3,)))
```

    class State(BaseModel):
        name: str
        type: str | MISSING = MISSING


    class City(BaseModel):
        name: str
        country: Country
        state: State | MISSING = MISSING

    state = State(name="state")
    country = Country(name="country")
    city = City(name="city", country=country, state=state)
    assert city.model_dump() == {
        'name': 'city',
        'country': {
            'name': 'country',
        },
        'state': {
            'name': 'state'
        },
    }
```
This test will pass and throw some warnings:

```
... python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/main.py:464: UserWarning: Pydantic serializer warnings:
    PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected 2 fields but got 1: Expected `State` - serialized value may not be as expected [field_name='state', input_value=State(name='state', type=<MISSING>), input_type=State])
    PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected 'MISSING' sentinel)
    return self.__pydantic_serializer__.to_python(
```

Only seems to happen if `state.type` is left as MISSING.

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
Python: 3.12.12
Pydantic: 2.12.4
Pydantic-core: 2.41.5
Debian 12
```

title: generated json schema is non-deterministic when field default is a non empty set
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The ordering of "default" in json schema is inconsistent across runs when the default is an unordered collection (eg: `set`, `frozenset`).

Running the provided code multiple times produces a different schema each time it runs.

My use case is generating a json schema as part of my [bazel](https://bazel.build/) build, but I'm having undesirable behavior due to the generated schema being inconsistent across runs.
In particular, a test that validates that a copy of the json schema that's checked in to source control matches the generated json schema passes locally, but fails in CI (because CI generated a different json schema than what was generated locally).

For example:

```sh
$ diff <(uv run main.py) <(uv run main.py)
20,22d19
<         "a",
<         "b",
<         "f",
24c21
<         "d",
---
>         "f",
26c23,26
<         "e"
---
>         "e",
>         "b",
>         "d",
>         "a"
```

As a workaround, I settled on using `SortedSet[MyEnum]` where `SortedSet` is from [`pydantic_sortedcontainers`](https://github.com/drivendataorg/sortedcontainers-pydantic).

I also considered using the following workaround, but the runtime lie about the type makes me uneasy because `type(MyModel().field) == list`, so code like `MyModel().field.union({MyEnum.A})` would pass the type checker, but would fail at runtime.

```py3
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    _DEFAULT = frozenset(MyEnum)
else:
    _DEFAULT = sorted(MyEnum)

class MyModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    field: frozenset[MyEnum] = _DEFAULT
```

Finally, I considered the following workaround, but mypy rejects it[^1].

```py3
    field: frozenset[MyEnum] = pydantic.Field(
        default=frozenset(MyEnum),
        json_schema_extra={"default": sorted(MyEnum)},
    )
```

```sh
$ uv run mypy --strict main.py            
main.py:23: error: Dict entry 0 has incompatible type "str": "list[str]"; expected "str": "int | float | str | list[JsonValue] |

title: Regression serializing base models with generics and inheritance (ABC error from cloudpickle)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When using Pydantic base models with generic type parameters and some inheritance, attempting to use CloudPickle to serialize these fails with complaints about abstract base class internals:
```python
TypeError: cannot pickle '_abc._abc_data' object
```

This is a regression from Pydantic 2.9 and first appeared in 2.11.0 so far as I can tell.  It is still present in 2.12 and on `main`, at least as of the latest commit at the time of writing (https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/commit/08b64f7a43f96f02bb0af8d46aba67b3a68d6e88).  While I'm aware that a related issue has been raised with CloudPickle (https://github.com/cloudpipe/cloudpickle/issues/572), the fact that this is a regression on the Pydantic side means I think it is relevant to report here (at least for visibility).

For context, I am testing using Python 3.12, while the aforementioned issue reported by you (@Viicos) against CloudPickle is with Python 3.14.

The reproduction code given below is intended be run as a uv script.

### Example Code

```Python
# /// script
# dependencies = [
#   "cloudpickle==3.1.2",
#   "pydantic @ git+https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic.git@08b64f7a43f96f02bb0af8d46aba67b3a68d6e88",
# ]
# ///

import cloudpickle
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo(BaseModel): ...
class FooWrapper[E: Foo](Foo): ...
class Bar(Foo):
    def get[E: Foo](self) -> FooWrapper[E]: ...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cloudpickle.dumps(Bar)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.13.0a0+dev
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.12.11 (main, Jul  1 2025, 18:16:32) [Clang 20.1.4 ]

body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Android and iOS became officially supported Python platforms with the release of Python 3.13. Support for building Android and iOS wheels was added in cibuildwheel [v3.1.0](https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/releases/tag/v3.1.0) and [v3.0.0](https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/releases/tag/v3.0.0) respectively. Up until this point there has not been a standard way to cross compile and package wheels for Android and iOS, which has led to segregation when each framework had their own type of build manifests and strategies for building native code for mobile platforms.

Refer to this page for more information and mobile compatibility status of the most popular Python wheels: https://beeware.org/mobile-wheels/

I'm creating this issue to track the progress of this matter and to show that there is interest for such a thing. Personally I'm mostly interested in seeing an official Android wheel. This issue is largely dependent on the CI and build tooling and that is something that the maintainers will know better than me. However, If I have some time over I may look into creating a PR for this.

title: Incorrect Pydantic dataclass constructor signature when using `kw_only=True` in decorator and `pydantic.Field(kw_only=False)`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Example Code

```Python
import dataclasses
import inspect

import pydantic


@dataclasses.dataclass(kw_only=True)
class NormalDataclass:
    a: int = dataclasses.field(kw_only=False)


@pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass(kw_only=True)
class PydanticDataclassWithDataclassField:
    a: int = dataclasses.field(kw_only=False)


@pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass(kw_only=True)
class PydanticDataclassWithPydanticField:
    a: int = pydantic.Field(kw_only=False) # This should show in the signature as not being keyword-only.


for cls in [
    NormalDataclass,
    PydanticDataclassWithDataclassField,
    PydanticDataclassWithPydanticField,
]:
    instance = cls(1)  # Runs fine, type-checks fine.
    assert instance.a == 1
    print(f"{cls.__name__}: {inspect.signature(cls)}")
```

### Description

The example code above prints:

```
NormalDataclass: (a: int) -> None
PydanticDataclassWithDataclassField: (a: int) -> None
PydanticDataclassWithPydanticField: (*, a: int = <factory>) -> None
```

When I would have expected all the signatures to be the same:

```
NormalDataclass: (a: int) -> None
PydanticDataclassWithDataclassField: (a: int) -> None
PydanticDataclassWithPydanticField: (a: int) -> None
```

All of these classes still accept passing a positional argument for `a` to the constructor, but the signature for `PydanticDataclassWithPydanticField` is wrong, incorrectly showing `a` as a keyword-only argument, as well as showing a default value when the argument is actually required.

This appears to be an issue here at `pydantic._internal._dataclasses`:

```python
def as_dataclass_field(pydantic_field: FieldInfo) -> dataclasses.Field[Any]:
    field_args: dict[str, Any] = {'default': pydantic_field}

    # Needed because if `doc` is set, the dataclass slots will be a dict (field name -> doc) instead of a tuple:
    if sys.version_info >= (3, 14) and pydantic_field.description is not None:
        field_args['doc']

title: TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'str' and 'type' with forward reference and python 3.14
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Assume the following code:
```python

import typing
import pydantic

T = typing.TypeVar('T')

class Relationships(pydantic.BaseModel, typing.Generic[T]):
    data: typing.Union[T, str] = None

class Model(pydantic.BaseModel):
    # works with python == 3.14, doesn't work with < 3.14
    # field: Relationships[Model]
    # works with python < 3.14, doesn't work with == 3.14
    field: Relationships['Model']
```

With python < 3.14, I can use annotation `Relationships['Model']` just fine. Since the update to 3.14, the annotation using string gives an error attached below. However, the new way of writing annotation `Relationships[Model]` works. So the way the annotation has to be written is mutually exclusive.

title: Issue when calling wrap validator handler twice and using model's `__init__()`
Optional[datetime.date] = None resolves to NoneType when field name is date
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In Pydantic v2, a model field named date annotated as Optional[datetime.date] = None is incorrectly treated as NoneType only. As a result, validating any non-null value (e.g. an ISO date string) fails with none_required.

Aliasing or qualifying the date type, or renaming the field, resolves the issue.


```
import datetime
date: Optional[datetime.date] = None

from datetime import date as Date
date: Optional[Date] = None

event_date: Optional[date] = None

```

This appears to be a name-resolution issue when the field name (date) matches the imported type name (date).

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError


class Model(BaseModel):
    date: Optional[date] = None


print("Annotation:", Model.model_fields["date"].annotation)
print("Core schema:", Model.__pydantic_core_schema__)

try:
    Model.model_validate({"date": "2026-01-01"})
except ValidationError as e:
    print(e)
```

title: Extract type arguments used to specialize generic models at run-time
body:
### Initial Checks

### Description

I've been working on porting a Pydantic V1 [application](https://salsa.debian.org/freexian-team/debusine) to V2, and in the process found myself needing to import from `pydantic._internal._generics`.  Neither my reviewer nor I can think of a better way to do things at the moment, but we agreed it would make sense to file an issue upstream.

A number of parts of our database schema are structured with a category field and some JSON data fields; the category field selects the schema used to parse the JSON data fields.  For example, we have several different kinds of "artifact", each of which has its own schema for "artifact data".  The code to handle each artifact of course wants to work with a validated Pydantic model instance rather than the raw JSON data, but we wanted to keep things reasonably DRY by not repeating the code needed to construct the model in lots of different places.

    A class may specialize a generic base class in various ways: for
    example, it might supply specific values for some of the relevant type

    by its own subclasses, or both.  To help us introspect such classes,
    this returns the speciali

title: `model_validate_strings()` does not respect custom `__init__()`
body:
Initially reported in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-settings/issues/731:

Because the `StringMapping` does not implement `Input.as_kwargs()`:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/c449cae821e758e8f8e584a50c63baec4f427c16/pydantic-core/src/input/input_string.rs#L65-L80

The custom `__init__()` of a model isn't called:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/c449cae821e758e8f8e584a50c63baec4f427c16/pydantic-core/src/validators/model.rs#L290-L302

As `BaseSettings` rely on its custom `__init__()` to be called to inject data from sources (not the best approach, see https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-settings/issues/673#issuecomment-3448221284), this causes issues.

title: `model_validate_strings()` for complex types
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

As reported in:

- https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10465
- https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12673

There is currently no way to parse "complex" types (arrays/mappings) in the "strings" mode:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class A(BaseModel):
    a: dict[str, str]

A.model_validate_strings({'a': '{"b": "c"}')
"""
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for A
a
  Input should be an object [type=dict_type, input_value='{"b": "c"}', input_type=str]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/dict_type
"""
```

The issue is the "strings" mode isn't JSON. While naively we could say "these complex types should be validated as JSON", this needs proper thinking before going this way (similarly, `None` can't be parsed -- as reported in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12377 --, and it's unclear if we should allow the string `"None"`, or the JSON `null`?).

Documentation is also unclear/incorrect and needs to be updated in the meanwhile.

title: Improve logic around `ImportString` validation when the `ImportError` originates from another source
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When something else (and not the `ImportString` import path) produces an `ImportError`, `ImportString`'s error handling catches the exception and assumes it has been produced by the user-provided import path. 

This results in a misleading and confusing exception message. 

### Example Code

#### repro.py

```py

from pydantic import TypeAdapter
from pydantic.types import ImportString

adapter: TypeAdapter[type] = TypeAdapter(ImportString)

# This import path points to a valid module, but that module has its own ImportError
import_path = "my_module.MyClass"

print("Attempting to validate import path:", import_path)
print()

# What Pydantic reports:
print("=== Pydantic ImportString error ===")
try:
    adapter.validate_python(import_path)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")

print()

# What the actual error is:
print("=== Actual ImportError ===")
try:
    import my_module
except ImportError as e:
    print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")
```

#### my_module.py

```py
import torch  # do not install torch!

class MyClass:
    pass
```

```
› python repro.py
Attempting to validate import path: my_module.MyClass

=== Pydantic ImportString error ===
ValidationError: 1 validation error for function-plain[import_string()]
  Invalid python path: No module named 'my_module.MyClass' [type=import_error, input_value='my_module.MyClass', input_type=str]

=== Actual ImportError ===
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'torch'
```

title: interaction between models that allow extra fields and setting computed fields is unexpected
Add `Port` as a network type
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I would like to propose adding a built in `Port` type, similar in spirit to existing network types.

When handling configuration, network ports can be very common. It would be ideal if, when parsing such configuration, ports could be represented in a type safe and semantically meaningful way.

Ports have a clear meaning, and while it can be argued that ports are simply integers with a defined range, this semantic information is currently lost in most model definitions.

Currently, a way to solve the issue with having a typesafe `Port` could be the following:

```python
Port = Annotated[int, Field(ge=0, le=65535)]

class SomeConfig(BaseModel):
    port: Port
```

While the above example works, it leads to each project needing to define its own type. I believe implementing `Port` can provide a more centralized and reusable definition. It could also raise the intuition that a port also needs to be validated and not simply be taken as is, and applications would be able to fail at an earlier stage.

title: PydanticOmit raised when model_json_schema is called on Model using multiple type aliases for MISSING type
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When the provided code is run, PydanticOmit is raised. Removing the type keyword in front of NoneToMissing fixes the crash.

It also needs both of those fields to be on the model in order for the crash to happen, I couldn't reproduce it with only one of these fields present.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated, Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, BeforeValidator
from pydantic.experimental.missing_sentinel import MISSING


def _none_to_missing(v: Any) -> MISSING:
    if v is MISSING or v is None:
        return MISSING
    raise ValueError("Value is not empty")


type NoneToMissing = Annotated[MISSING, BeforeValidator(_none_to_missing)]


class TestModel(BaseModel):
    field1: Literal["a", "b"] | NoneToMissing = MISSING
    field2: Literal["x", "y"] | NoneToMissing = MISSING


TestModel.model_json_schema()
```

title: `IsInstance` does not handle all possible schema generation errors
AssertionError: MockCoreSchema assertion fails in model_json_schema()
body:
### Initial Checks

- [X] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When calling `model_json_schema()` on a model, an `AssertionError` is raised with the message "this is a bug! please report it" at line 2547 in `pydantic/json_schema.py`. The assertion fails because `cls.__pydantic_core_schema__` is an instance of `MockCoreSchema`.

This error occurred in a production AWS Lambda environment during ATS scorecard synchronization.

title: `cloudpickle` issue with models with definitions
No psycopg_async scheme in PostgresDsn network class
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

SQLAlchemy has two dialects of psycopg: psycopg2 (psycopg package version version 2) and psycopg (psycopg version 3)
The asyncio version of the dialect psycopg (version 3) may also be specified explicitly using the psycopg_async suffix, as:

```
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine

asyncio_engine = create_async_engine(
    "postgresql+psycopg_async://scott:tiger@localhost/test"
)
```
https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/dialects/postgresql.html#module-sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql.psycopg

But pydantic settings field with type PostgresDSN do not allow this suffix in schemes. Because "postgresql+psycopg_async" not in allowed schemes in PostgresDSN class.
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/main/pydantic/networks.py#L755



### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import PostgresDsn
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class PostgresSettings(BaseSettings):
    DSN: PostgresDsn | None = "postgresql+psycopg_async://user:password@postgres:5432/postgres"

postgres_settings = PostgresSettings()

# pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for PostgresSettings
# DSN
#   Input should be a valid URL, relative URL without a base [type=url_parsing, input_value='postgresql+psycopg_async...@postgres:5432/postgres', input_type=str]
#     For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/url_parsing
```

title: Support `exclude_if` for computed fields
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The new `exclude_if` annotation in `Field` is ignored on `computed_fields`.

I am not sure if this is similar to or completely different from the other issue (https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12387).  It seems like it's different enough I decided to open a new issue.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Annotated

IntExcludeZero = Annotated[int, Field(exclude_if=lambda v: v == 0)]

class Model(BaseModel):
    @computed_field
    def a_computed_field(self) -> IntExcludeZero:
        return 0

obj = Model()
print(obj.model_dump())  # {"a_computed_field": 0 }
```

The expected behavior for the code above  is for `a_computed_field` to not be in the result due to the `exclude_if` directive.

title: `model_dump()` produces different results when run in empty `contextvars.Context()` - fields missing from generic union members
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The result of `model_dump()` depends on the `contextvars` context in which it's executed. When a model containing a union of a base class and a generic subclass is serialized in an empty `contextvars.Context()`, fields from the generic subclass are silently dropped.
This appears to be caused by schema/validator caching in contextvars. When running in a fresh context, the cached schema is unavailable and the regenerated schema incorrectly resolves the union to the base class instead of the generic subclass.

We discovered this issue after uvicorn 0.39.0 changed to [set up fresh contextvars for each request](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/commit/d94bf28743de545d2d4b150f022203a5178cb705). This causes FastAPI/Starlette applications to suddenly produce incomplete JSON responses after upgrading uvicorn, with fields silently missing from serialized models.

### Example Code

```Python
import contextvars
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Base(BaseModel):
    name: str


class Generic[T](Base):
    items: list[T]


class Parent(BaseModel):
    child: Base | Generic[int]


def test():
    return Parent(child=Generic[int](name="x", items=[1])).model_dump()


print("current context:", test())
print("empty context:  ", contextvars.Context().run(test))


**Output:**

current context: {'child': {'name': 'x', 'items': [1]}}
empty context:   {'child': {'name': 'x'}}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.5
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.5
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.13.9 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Oct 22 2025, 23:38:18) [Clang 19.1.7 ]
