title: Faster _fields.py collect_model_fields
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I am wondering if the logic in _fields.py collect_model_fields is actually optimal. I am pretty sure it isn't because it is calling _typing_extra.get_model_type_hints which then loops through obj.__mro__ to consolidate the typing info.

It is not an issue for classes that have a small inheritance tree, but for classes with large trees it looks suboptimal.

For large inheritance trees given pydantic is already saving model_fields, class_vars and private_attributes into each base class why is there even a need to look at __mro__?

Isn't it enough to collect the data just from the bases and add in the hinting data just from the cls?

We have a need to create a large number of unique pydantic classes 10k+ with 3-7 levels of inheritance and collect_model_fields with _typing_extra.get_model_type_hints was coming up hot in the profile.


### Example Code

```Python

```

title: Improve validation error messages
body:
We should provide general guidelines to follow when writing validation error messages, e.g. _should state what's wrong, what's the expected input_.

e.g. with dates:

```python
from datetime import date

from pydantic import TypeAdapter

TypeAdapter(date).validate_python('.')
# Input should be a valid date or datetime, input is too short
```

should state the expected format.

title: Better TypeError message on serializer
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

When using FastAPI returning pydantic objetcs, I was able to track the following problem
- Pydantic Model was able to instantiate an object with datetime field using NaT  value
- The TypeAdapter serializer inside fastapi code raised `TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer`

Code to replicate what happened (without FastAPI inbetweens):
```python
from datetime import datetime
from pydantic import BaseModel, TypeAdapter
from pandas import NaT

class Model(BaseModel):
    timestamp: datetime

model = Model(
    timestamp=NaT
)

# Ran OK until this line
TypeAdapter(Model).serializer.to_python(
    model,
    mode="json",
    by_alias=True,
    include=None,
    exclude=None,
    exclude_unset=False,
    exclude_defaults=False,
    exclude_none=False,
    round_trip=False,
    warnings=True,
    serialize_as_any=False,
) # throws TypeError
```

Not sure if this behavior is expected, but I'd like to **ask for better error messages** when having issues with these serialization functions, for example something like Pydantic modeling error which shows the exact location of where the serializer is having an error.

### Description

When defining fields via stdlib `dataclasses.Field`, resulting `model_validate` behaves differently to `model_validate` of a model defined with `pydantic.Field`.

I've checked the Core schemas on the model classes, and, interestingly enough, they seemed identical to me.

I'd like to be able to reliably use `dataclasses.Field` to define schemas that can be consumed without the Pydantic dependency, but used with Pydantic's TypeAdapters for serialization.

Apologies if a duplicate; I tried my best to sift through 198 issues from the `is:issue state:open dataclasses field` search result, but might've failed.

### Example Code

```Python
from dataclasses import field as StdlibField

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field as PydanticField


class PydanticFieldModel(BaseModel):
    required: str
    optional: str = PydanticField(default="world")

class StdlibFieldModel(BaseModel):
    required: str
    optional: str = StdlibField(default="world")


assert PydanticFieldModel.model_validate({"required": "hello"}) == PydanticFieldModel(required="hello", optional="world")  # passes

title: @model_validator can corrupt input data
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When I use @model_validator decorator for custom deserialization, the input data can be corrupted. See minimal code example below. After a call to model_validate() the content of test_data has changed.

I did not expect this behavior. I expected that @model_validator provides an isolated copy of input data. Otherwise I would need knowledge of how Pydantic works internally to understand what's going on. Probably nobody wants to permanently change input data when using a model validator. I guess fixing it by deep-copying the input data could be done with minimum effort.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator, Field


class TestCase(BaseModel):
    property: str = Field(default={}, init=False, exclude=False)

    @model_validator(mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def set_property(cls, values):
        values["property"] = "changed"
        return values


def test_deserialization():
    test_data = {
        "property": "original"
    }
    TestCase.model_validate(test_data)

title: TypeAdater deserializing generics
Premature warning about Python 3.14 in pydantic.v1
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a module is imported `from pydantic.v1` and a user is running Python 3.14, they'll get a warning: "Core Pydantic V1 functionality isn't compatible with Python 3.14 or greater."

On the surface, the logic here is sound: if the user is importing something from `pydantic.v1` they must be about to use that thing, so let's warn them that it's not going to work.

But there are packages that import classes from V1 just to _check_ if the end user is using V1. For example, [LangChain in this file](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/libs/core/langchain_core/utils/pydantic.py).

In these cases, the warning is confusing and sends developers down a rabbit hole of trying to work out "who's trying to use Pydantic V1?" and if they don't know Python intimately, they might think that different packages are using different versions of Pydantic at the same time and they're clashing, all rather unpleasant, considering that nothing's actually wrong!

I see three possible futures:
1. Python 3.14 users in this situation get warnings, they can ignore them (warning fatigue, not great)
2. All packages that do this "import from `pydantic.v1` to check usage" pattern fix their code to check in some way that doesn't involve importing from `pydantic.v1` (probably not going to happen)
3. Pydantic refines the logic to only show this warning when it looks like something is actually going to break (e.g. triggered in the constructors of classes)

The current logic is going to be helpful to a shrinking number of users, and rather annoying for a growing number of users.

Relevant PR: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/12263

```Python

```

title: Importing pydantic causes ~30% async iteration slowdown on Python 3.14
body:
**Environment:**
- pydantic 2.12.5
- Python 3.14.0 vs 3.13.9
- macOS, Apple M1 Max

**Reproduction:**

```python
import asyncio
import time
from collections.abc import AsyncIterator

# Uncomment to see slowdown:
# from pydantic import BaseModel

async def gen():
    for i in range(10000):
        yield i

async def bench():
    start = time.perf_counter()
    count = 0
    async for x in gen():
        count += 1
    end = time.perf_counter()
    print(f'{count} iterations in {(end-start)*1000:.2f}ms')

asyncio.run(bench())
```

**Results:**

| Python | Without pydantic import | With pydantic import |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 3.13.9 | ~2.0M iter/s | ~2.0M iter/s |
| 3.14.0 | ~1.3M iter/s | ~0.9M iter/s |

Simply importing pydantic (without using it) causes async iteration to slow down by ~30% on Python 3.14, while Python 3.13 is unaffected.

title: [Request] Built-in validation of lists and dicts of BaseModels (e.g. via RootModel children)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Suppose I have a simple model:

```python

from pydantic import BaseModel

Foo(BaseModel):
    bar: str
```
I might have an external data source that will sometimes return a `Foo`, but other times it might return a `list[Foo]` or `dict[something, Foo]`. How do you validate this? A naive approach might be `[Foo.model_validate(foo) for foo in response]`. This often works, but what if `response` is not a `list`? Or it's a `dict` instead (in which case you're inadvertently making a list of the keys).

It's very convenient to have pydantic handle all of these edge cases for you. One way is another `BaseModel` child with a `list[Foo]` attribute. Another way is by using `RootModel`. This is nice because it also lets you take advantage of `Protocol` to automatically call `model_validate` on arbitraily-typed responses from your external data sources. If for some reason, though, you need the post-validation object to be used directly (e.g. 3rd party library), then having to carry around the `.root` makes this break.

What we end up doing in our libraries is something like this:

```python
_V = TypeVar("_V", bound=BaseModel)

class ListModel(RootModel[list[_V]]):
    def append(self, value: _V):
        self.root.append(value)

    def extend(self, values: Iterable[_V]) -> None:
        self.root.extend(values)

    def insert(self, index: SupportsIndex, value: _V) -> None:
        self.root.insert(index, value)

    # And so on; whatever other methods we need.
```

You can do something similar with `dict`.

It would be great if something to handle these types of scenarios were built into pydantic to enable something like these:
```python
from pydantic import ListModel

class ListOfFoo(ListModel[Foo]):
    pass

ListOfFoo.model_validate(data)

Baz

title: Support for arbitrary metadata (like callable) in pydantic field declaration
body: ### Initial Checks - [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything - [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing ### Description I am developer of ODM, called butty based on pydantic, one of core feature is configuring some fields behaviour with callable in metadata (`extra` in v1 `FieldInfo`, `json_schema_extra` in v2), something like ```py class Model(BaseModel): id: Annotated[str, Field(json_schema_extra={"id_factory": lambda: str(uuid4())})] ``` So in pydantic v1 everything is ok, schema generated correctly even with callable in extra, but in v2 json schema generation for FastAPi is broken due to `json_schema.update(to_jsonable_python(js_extra))` with no fallback function. Is it possible to provide fallback to `to_jsonable_python` call to ignore non-jsonable metadata in `json_schema_extra` ? Or could you please advice approach to assign some arbitrary metadata into field declaration? v1 - OK ```py from typing import Annotated import uvicorn from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel, Field class Model(BaseModel): id: Annotated[int, Field(factory=lambda x: 1)] app = FastAPI() @app.get("") def get() -> Model: ... ``` v2 - schema broken ```py from typing import Annotated import uvicorn from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel, Field class Model(BaseModel): id: Annotated[int, Field(json_schema_extra={"factory": lambda x: 1})] app = FastAPI() @app.get("") def get() -> Model: ... uvicorn.run(app) ``` ``` File "/Users/avovsyannikov/PycharmProjects/GigaHub/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/pydantic/json_schema.py", line 541, in js_extra_handler_func json_schema.update(to_jsonable_python(js_extra)) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^ pydan

title: No ForwardRef in model_fields annotations with list['Model'] field type with legacy-style annotations (w/o from __future__ import annotations)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

There is no `ForwardRef` in model_fields annotations  with `list['Model']` field type with legacy-style annotations (w/o `from __future__ import annotations`) when forward model name is declared as string literal.

So `model_rebuild()` does not work correctly, and after rebuild there is no actual type in field annotation.

If we take a look into captured annotations, we'll see that event before rebuild annotation is not a `ForwardRef`.

```py
class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: 'Bar'
    bar2: list['Bar']


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???

Foo.model_rebuild()

print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'> OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???
```

with `from __future__ import annotations` PEP563 annotations everything works as expected

```py
class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: Bar
    bar2: list[Bar]


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('list[Bar]') OK

Foo.model_rebuild()

print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'> OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list[__main__.Bar] OK
```

### Example Code

```Python
# from __future__ import annotations
import sys

import pydantic
from pydantic import BaseModel

print(sys.version)  # 3.10.4 (tags/v3.10.4:9d38120, Mar 23 2022, 23:13:41) [MSC v.1929 64 bit (AMD64)]
print(pydantic.version.VERSION)  # 2.12.5


class Foo(BaseModel):
    bar1: 'Bar'
    bar2: list['Bar']


class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass


print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # ForwardRef('Bar') OK
print(Foo.model_fields['bar2'].annotation)  # list['Bar'] ???
print()
Foo.model_rebuild()
print(Foo.model_fields['bar1'].annotation)  # <class '__main__.Bar'>

title: Json fields are not transitive when copied across models
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

If I have a Json field in a model, it requires string-like input and converts it to something else (presumably a dict of whatever). But if I then need to create a new model with input from the first, it will fail because a Json field does not accept another Json field, only a string.

The reason why I might do this is because I could have a generic pipeline that expects to receive _some Python object_ and then automatically converts it to a Pydantic schema object of my choosing. It stands to reason that I should, if possible, be able to provide exactly the right schema object required, and it can be passed through as-is. This is the case for any other schema object I try, just not ones containing a Json field.

FastAPI appears to use exactly this mechanism - annotate a path with a Pydantic schema, and it'll attempt to create the object for you - which is why I have hit this problem. I can't return an object of the type I want because it's already got 'loaded' JSON in it and fails validation when creating the response.

It appears there might be special case code inside model_validate because the process works there, just not when creating new objects directly.

I expect this is related to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11154 .

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Json

class SimpleSchemaContainingNoJSON(BaseModel):
    name: str
    number: int

class SimpleSchemaContainingJSON(BaseModel):
    name: str
    document: Json


def test_schema_without_json():
    """ Check that a schema can usually be loaded into a new instance without trouble. """
    val_1 = SimpleSchemaContainingNoJSON(name="Name", number=123)
    val_2 = SimpleSchemaContainingNoJSON(name=val_1.name, number=val_1.number)
    assert val_2 is not None  # succeeds

def test_schema_with_json():
    """ Check whether a schema containing JSON can be loaded into a new instance. """
    val_1

title: repr when fields are aliased is misleading
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In Python, the [`repr`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#repr) of an object is most typically `eval`-able to reproduce the object. In the cases where that's not possible the usual convention is to surround the information with angle brackets rather than making it look like Python code.

pydantic's field aliases appear to break from this convention in ways that can easily pass unnoticed:

``` python
In [1]: import pydantic
   ...: 
   ...: class Foo(pydantic.BaseModel):
   ...:     aliased: int = pydantic.Field(42, alias="the_alias")
   ...: 

In [2]: Foo(the_alias=2)
Out[2]: Foo(aliased=2)  # huh, ok, I guess I can use that spelling too

In [3]: Foo(aliased=2)
Out[3]: Foo(aliased=42)  # doesn't match _and_ no error
```

I realise that if the field default is removed then an error is surfaced, however the error complains about a missing field rather than an extra/misspelled one. Additionally, field defaults are hardly uncommon.

I discovered this after attempting to use the non-aliased spelling which my type checker (`mypy`) encouraged me to use[^1], then finding that that didn't work as I expected it would. I haven't been able to find anything in the documentation to indicate that this is intentional.

[^1]: to me using the field names in `__init__` also feels more natural, even when there is an alias, since that's how e.g: dataclasses work

title: Allow raising custom model validation exceptions
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

It can sometimes be useful to treat validation errors coming the same model differently, based on the place in the code, where the validation was attempted.

One particular use case for this is a web application, which puts data fetched from a database into a model instance. `ValidationError` raised here should be considered a server-side error and treated differently from failing to validate a request body (which is a client-side error).

One way to differentiate validation failures is to allow setting an exception class or instance to be re-raised from a `ValidationError`, i.e.:

```python
class Model(BaseModel):
    # `model_config` can contain an optional exception class to be used by default for `Model`
    model_config = ConfigDict(custom_validation_exception=SomeException)
```

```python
def model_validate(
    ...
    # An override for the specific `model_validate` call, which has the priority
    # over `model_config` exception, if set, and could be provided with `ValidationError`
    # to disable re-raising
    custom_validation_exception: Exception | type[Exception] | None = None
):
    ...
    # `CustomExceptionContextManager` catches `ValidationError` and re-raises `exc` from it,
    # if `exc` is not resolved to None or `ValidationError`
    exc = custom_validation_exception or cls.model_config.get("custom_validation_exception")
    with CustomExceptionContextManager(exc):
        return cls.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(
        ...
        )
```

Another potential option is to restrict both `custom_validation_exception` values to `ValidationError` and its subclasses and use them directly in pydantic-core (although I can't properly assess the possibility & the amount of work required to do so).

It's certainly pos

title: String's case conversion using `LowerCase` and `UpperCase` from `annotated-types` not working
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

According to [documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/standard_library_types/#strings), `LowerCase` and `UpperCase` from `annotated-types` can be used for `str`. However, the string's case remain unchanged. `StringConstraints` works as expected.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated

from annotated_types import LowerCase, UpperCase
from pydantic import BaseModel, StringConstraints

test_string = "Test String"

# This works
class StrWithStringConstraints(BaseModel):
    upper_case: Annotated[str, StringConstraints(to_upper=True)]
    lower_case: Annotated[str, StringConstraints(to_lower=True)]

print(StrWithStringConstraints(upper_case=test_string, lower_case=test_string))
# output: upper_case='TEST STRING' lower_case='test string'

# This is not working
class StrWithAnnotatedTypes(BaseModel):
    upper_case: Annotated[str, UpperCase]
    lower_case: Annotated[str, LowerCase]

print(StrWithAnnotatedTypes(upper_case=test_string, lower_case=test_string))
# output: upper_case='Test String' lower_case='Test String'
```

title: Overridden BaseModel.model_validate is not invoked for a model property
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I want to override model_validate in a subclass of RootModel in order to implement a function to select the correct model for a union.  Unfortunately, pydantic fails to invoke the overriding function.

In the code below, I expect Foo.model_validate(obj) to raise an exception, but instead model_validate returns an object.

If I call Bar.model_validate({"name": "test"}) directly, then Bar.model_validate is invoked and exception raised. 

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Bar(BaseModel):
    name: str
    @classmethod
    def model_validate(
        cls,
        obj: Any,
        *,
        strict: bool | None = None,
        extra: ExtraValues | None = None,
        from_attributes: bool | None = None,
        context: Any | None = None,
        by_alias: bool | None = None,
        by_name: bool | None = None,
        ) -> Self:
        raise Exception('Bar.model_validate invoked.')

class Foo(BaseModel):

obj = {'bar': {"name": "test"}}
Foo.model_validate(obj)

title: Do not make `PydanticUserError` a `TypeError` subclass
body: For some reason, `PydanticUserError` is defined as a subclass of `TypError`. `TypeError` is for error related to invalid types (e.g. in function arguments), but `PydanticUserError` is used for a variety of other things as ell.

title: Deserialization of timestamp as a `datetime` fails
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a `JSON` payload that has a field containing an `int` value representing the number of seconds since the Unix epoch.

From https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/standard_library_types/?query=Datetimes#datetimes :

> * Strings and bytes are validated in two ways:
>    * [...]
>    * Unix timestamps, both as seconds or milliseconds sinch the [epoch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time). See the [val_temporal_unit](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.val_temporal_unit) configuration value for more details.
> * Integers and floats (or types that can be coerced as integers or floats) are validated as unix timestamps, following the same semantics as strings.

my understanding is that typing the field with `: datetime` should then work...

Except it triggers a `ValidationError`.

title: Support `nan` in combination with limits
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently `Field` information can define limits, defaults, and settings for things like allowing `inf` and `nan` values. However when combining them there can be compatibility issues, especially after serialisation and de-serialisation.  
While it makes sense that `+inf` should not be allowed with an upper limit, `nan` is often used to indicate that a value is not yet set. It would be useful if these `allow_x` values also applied when using limits. It may be needed to also split the `allow_inf_nan` into `allow_inf` and `allow_nan`.

```python

class Grade:
    name: str
    score: float = Field(default=float('nan'), ge=0.0, le=10.0, allow_inf_nan=True)

jd = grade(name="John Doe")  # currently make jd = {name='John Doe', score=nan}
jd2 = Grade.model_validate(jd.model_dump())  # Currently fails validation with: Input should be less than or equal to 10 [type=less_than_equal, input_value=nan, input_type=float]
```

### Affected Components

title: Allow a custom error message when a regex pattern isn't matched for a Field
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

In our code we have several places where we have code like the following in order to provide both a nice error message at runtime and a JSON schema compatible regex:

```
DEBIAN_PACKAGE_NAME_REGEX = r"^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9.+-]+$"


def _validate_debian_package_name(name: str) -> str:
    """Validate a Debian package name."""
    if not re.match(DEBIAN_PACKAGE_NAME_REGEX, name):
        raise ValueError("Nice error message goes here")
    return name


DebianPackageName = Annotated[
    str,
    pydantic.Field(pattern=DEBIAN_PACKAGE_NAME_REGEX),
    pydantic.BeforeValidator(_validate_debian_package_name),
]
```

This works fine, but is a bit redundant. It would be nice to have an optional parameter for `Field` that replaces the "string should match pattern" message with a custom error message.

### Affected Components

title: Increased memory usage with @model_validator
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

After upgrading pydantic from version 2.10.6 to 2.11, we noticed a large (4x) increase in startup memory usage in our fastapi app. I seems to be related to the presence of a @model_validator on our BaseModel. I haven't been able to really narrow in on what causes the memory increase but the example below shows some of that behavior. While most of the memory usage is due to the regex as addressed by #12540, in combination with @model_validator memory usage increases when upgrading pydantic version.

memray peak memory usage:
2.10.6 with @model_validator: 145 MB
2.12.0 with @model_validator: 174 MB
2.10.6 without @model_validator 144 MB
2.12.0 without @model_validator 30 MB

### Example Code

```Python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import (
    Annotated,
)

from pydantic import (
    BaseModel,
    Field,
    model_validator,
)

ConstraintType = Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]


class MyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def my_validator(self) -> MyBaseModel:
        return self


class R7(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    t1: ConstraintType
    t2: ConstraintType
    t3: ConstraintType
    t4: ConstraintType


class R6(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    t1: ConstraintType
    t2: ConstraintType
    t3: ConstraintType
    t4: ConstraintType
    t5: ConstraintType


class R4(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    f0: list[R6]
    f1: list[R7]
    f2: list[ConstraintType]
    t1: ConstraintType


class R10(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType


class R5(MyBaseModel):
    f0: R10
    f1: R1
    f2: R1


class R1(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    t1: ConstraintType


class R8(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    t1: ConstraintType
    f0: list[R6]
    f1: list[R7]
    t2: ConstraintType


class R3(MyBaseModel):
    t0: ConstraintType
    t1: ConstraintType
    f0: list[R6]
    f1: list

title: Altering Pydantic behavior/configuration globally
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently, Pydantic behavior can generally be controlled using [the configuration](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/). Pydantic is quite configurable: validation/serialization behavior, JSON Schema, behavior for specific types, etc. But this also means that we have to choose reasonable defaults for all these configuration values.

As an application/library author, you often want to set your own defaults, to be used in your entire code base. The canonical way to do so is to [define your own `BaseModel`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/#change-behaviour-globally):

```python
class MyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = {'validate_by_name': True}
```

However, there are limitations with this approach:

- [Configuration is _not_ propagated](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/#configuration-propagation) for Pydantic model and dataclasses.
- If you happen to use dataclasses, TypedDicts or `TypeAdapter`, you need specify this configuration every time.
- For some settings, it makes sense to have it applied globally (e.g. [`ignored_types`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.ignored_types)).

---

It may be beneficial for end users to have a way to set the configuration globally. I believe this is particularly useful for applications (e.g. a FastAPI backend app). Unlike libraries, you "own" all of your code base. For instance, the [Django REST Framework](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework) libraries (and Django in general) uses [settings](https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/settings/) to control the behavior globally, where you can provide your own defaults that can be overridden per viewset. In some way, Pydantic is no different when u

title: Empty dict serialization for dataclass subclasses in union types (Pydantic 2.12.4)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When a Pydantic `BaseModel` field uses a union type (`BaseModel | JSONMixin`) and contains a nested `BaseModel` with a dataclass subclass instance, `model_dump()` produces an empty dict `{}` in Pydantic 2.12.4. This works correctly in Pydantic 2.12.3.

### Version Information

- **Pydantic version**: 2.12.4 (bug occurs) / 2.12.3 (works correctly)
- **pydantic-core version**: 2.41.5 (bug occurs) / 2.41.4 (works correctly)
- **Python version**: 3.12+
- **OS**: Any

### Example Code

```Python
import dataclasses
import fastclasses_json
import pydantic


@fastclasses_json.dataclass_json
@dataclasses.dataclass
class BaseError(fastclasses_json.JSONMixin):
    code: str
    message: str


@fastclasses_json.dataclass_json
@dataclasses.dataclass
class NetworkError(BaseError):
    code: str = dataclasses.field(default='network_error', init=False)


class Result(pydantic.BaseModel):
    error: BaseError


class Container(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)  # Required for JSONMixin in union
    
    message: pydantic.BaseModel | fastclasses_json.JSONMixin


def main() -> None:
    print(f"Pydantic {pydantic.__version__}\n")

    base_error = BaseError(code='base', message='test')
    network_error = NetworkError(message='test')
    network_error_dict = NetworkError(message='test').to_dict()

    container_base = Container(message=Result(error=base_error))
    container_network = Container(message=Result(error=network_error))
    container_network_dict = Container(message=Result(error=network_error_dict))

    print(f"BaseError:        {'✓' if container_base.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗'}")
    print(f"NetworkError:     {'✓' if container_network.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗✗✗ BUG'}")
    print(f"Convert to dict:  {'✓' if container_network_dict.model_dump()['message'] != {} else '✗'}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

title: `validation_types` in ConfigDict to improve type-checking
Evolving Pydantic: `BaseStruct`
body:
This is a feature plan for the next step in the `pydantic` API, which we'd like to call `BaseStruct`.

The high-level goal is to take some of the lessons we've learned from `BaseModel`, and present a new type which should achieve higher performance, more features and better default semantics. `BaseModel` may eventually gain some of these changes itself, but we would prefer to be extremely conservative with changing `BaseModel` and introduce a new type so that users can opt-in to the changed semantics.

We will merge `BaseStruct` into the `pydantic.experimental.structs` namespace while this type is being developed, possibly as soon as Pydantic 2.13. We anticipate that Pydantic V3 may be the milestone where `BaseStruct` is stabilised, although this is not strictly required.

Rough outline of the planned semantics of `BaseStruct`:

- Performance
   - [ ] `BaseStruct` is planned to keep more data as (internal?) native state rather than Python objects
   - [ ] Field accessors will probably be cached so that once the Python value is accessed, it will have normal Python semantics
- Ergonomics
   - [ ] `BaseStruct` will have no methods (`model_validate`, `model_dump`). Instead we will expose free functions such as `validate`, `validate_json`, `to_python` and `to_json`
   - [ ] There will likely be a `StructMethodsMixin` type to add methods such as `struct_validate` where desired
   - [ ] We might try to do something different with generic structs so that we don't create new subclasses when doing generic parameterisation, and just make "regular" generic aliases.
- Features
  - [ ] We would like to eventually offer compatibilty between struct types and dataframes, particularly arrow "struct arrays". The exact form of this is yet to be explored.

title: Regression in v2.12.4 on Python 3.14: TypeError: _eval_type() got an unexpected keyword argument 'prefer_fwd_module'

title: data validation gets skipped when working with generic pipeline api
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

this might be a bit whacky, so if i am using pydantic in ways it was not intended for feel free to close this issue.

**problem i was trying to solve:**

i am working with an api that returns some deeply nested data and wanted to write a pydantic model that would automatically flatten said data after validation.

**expected behavior:**

the first validation example correctly validates & serializes the data, this does not apply to the second example even though the type should still resolve to the same value.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated, Any
from pydantic import BaseModel, TypeAdapter, AfterValidator, BeforeValidator
from pydantic.experimental.pipeline import validate_as
from rich import print

sample_json = """{
    "data": [
        { "id": 0, "ignored_extra_data": "12341234" },
        { "id": 1, "ignored_extra_data": "12341234" },
        { "id": 2, "ignored_extra_data": "12341234" },
        { "id": 3, "ignored_extra_data": "12341234" },
        { "id": 4, "ignored_extra_data": "12341234" }
    ]
}"""

class APIResponse[T](BaseModel):
    data: T

class Product(BaseModel):
    id: int





FlattenedAPIResponsePipes: TypeAdapter[list[Product]] = TypeAdapter(Annotated[
    list[Product],
    validate_as(APIResponse[list[Product]])
    .transform(lambda response: response.data)
    .validate_as(list[Product])
])

validated = FlattenedAPIResponsePipes.validate_json(sample_json)
print("TRANSFORMATION VIA PIPES")
print(validated)
print(FlattenedAPIResponsePipes.dump_json(validated))
print(FlattenedAPIResponsePipes.json_schema(mode="validation"))
print(FlattenedAPIResponsePipes.json_schema(mode="serialization"))

# - validates and serializes correctly, json schema for serialization is correct
# after adding final `.validate_as(list[Product])`, even though return type of
# `.transform` call is technically already known
# - `response` field in the transform call

title: Excessive memory usage for regex validators
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

After upgrading pydantic from version 2.10.6 to 2.11, we noticed a large (4x) increase in startup memory usage in our fastapi app. It seems to be related to regex validator creation. Example 1 demonstrates high memory usage. We were able to mitigate the issue by refactoring to example 2 which does not suffer from the issue.

It seems like the issue is specific to some aspects of this regex. Changing the regex makes it mostly go away. So it seems like there are two things going on here: This particular regex takes up a lot of memory and it is replicated in memory due to how it is used by pydantic.

Memray stats for example 1:
Total number of allocations: 4084096
Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 307.6 MB
Python allocator: pymalloc

memray stats for example 2:

Total number of frames seen: 0
Peak memory usage: 7.2 MB
Python allocator: pymalloc

### Example Code

```Python
Example 1:

from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

ConstraintType = Annotated[str, Field(pattern="^(\\p{L}|_)(\\p{L}|\\p{N}|[.\\-_])*$")]


class TestModel1(BaseModel):

    field_2: ConstraintType
    ...

==================================================================

from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, RootModel


class RootType(RootModel[str]):

title: Preserve `NamedTuple` class during validation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

This is what I'm observing:

```Python
import pydantic
from typing import NamedTuple


class A(NamedTuple):
    x: int


class B(NamedTuple):
    x: int


class C(pydantic.BaseModel):
    a_or_b: A | B


c = C(a_or_b=B(x=1))
print(c)

# expected: a_or_b=B(x=1)
# prints: a_or_b=A(x=1)
```

This short-circuits on type arguments in the union in order in the `smart` mode of unions (`left_to_right` I assume makes no difference, the problem lies in exactness).
If we reverse it, the output will show `B` because `B` was first that structurally matched.

It works this way in strict mode too.
This resembles `left_to_right` more than `smart` mode of the union schema.

Conceptually, Pydantic looks for ways to match namedtuple as a structural type, whereas the nominal bit was missed.

This applies to old-fashion named tuples as well:
```py
from collections import namedtuple

import pydantic

A = namedtuple('A', 'x')
B = namedtuple('B', 'x')

class C(pydantic.BaseModel):
    a_or_b: A | B

c = C(a_or_b=B(x=1))
print(c)  # a_or_b=A(x=1)
```

We would want to convert between uniform namedtuples in non-strict mode -- sure:

```py
import pydantic
from typing import NamedTuple


class A(NamedTuple):
    x: int


class B(NamedTuple):
    x: int


class C(pydantic.BaseModel):
    only_a: A


c = C(only_a=B(x=1))
print(c)  # only_a=A(x=1)
```

but I'm not so sure we want the union schema to miss the nominal type of the input. If we can preserve the original type, my instinct is that we'd want to keep it. Esp. since we don't observe this behavior with dataclasses (namedtuples are of quite different nature, though).

The current behavior can be interpreted as a feature, not a bug -- feel free to relabel this as a feature request if you do think that's true.

## In short

I'm proposing a feature to, in validation, validate every namedtuple input against its targeted namedtuple class (so, `return_schema` -- seem

title: deep-copying `MISSING` crashes in `BaseModel.model_construct()` and `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()` (TypeError: Cannot pickle 'Sentinel' object)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

## Problem
When a field’s default is the experimental `MISSING` sentinel (pydantic >=2.12), Pydantic ends up trying to deepcopy that sentinel in a few places. The sentinel isn’t deepcopy/pickle-friendly, so this raises: 

> TypeError: Cannot pickle 'Sentinel' object.

## Where it happens
- `BaseModel.model_construct()`: default resolution goes through `smart_deepcopy()`, which currently tries to deepcopy `MISSING`.
- `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()`: stdlib `deepcopy()` is called on `self.__dict__` (and others), which hits `MISSING` and fails.

## Proposed fix
1) In `smart_deepcopy()`, return early for the sentinel:

```python
# pydantic/_internal/_utils.py
from pydantic_core import MISSING

def smart_deepcopy(obj):
    if obj is MISSING:
        return obj
    # rest of current logic...
```

This keeps the singleton by identity and avoids deepcopy entirely.

2) In `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()`, use `smart_deepcopy()` (with memo) instead of raw `deepcopy()` for model internals.

- Today it does `deepcopy(self.__dict__, memo=...)`; that will crash if values contain `MISSING`.
- `smart_deepcopy()` doesn’t accept memo yet, it could be extended to do so and thread it into the fallback deepcopy call.

## Rationale
- `MISSING` is a singleton sentinel meant to be passed by identity. Copying it isn’t useful and currently fails.
- Using `smart_deepcopy()` in `BaseModel.__deepcopy__()` aligns behavior between construction and copying and avoids duplicating special cases.

## Minimal repro
```python
from copy import deepcopy
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from pydantic.experimental.missing_sentinel import MISSING  # 2.12.x

class M(BaseModel):
    a: int | MISSING = Field(default=MISSING)

# 1) model_construct path
M.model_construct()  # attempts to deepcopy MISSING -> TypeError

# 2) __deepcopy__ path
m = M()
deepcopy(m)          # deepcopy(self.__dict__) hits MISSING -> TypeError
```

Expected
- B

In https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/json_schema/#unenforced-field-constraints ,

the code example actually will not raise any error! It works that we set a `lt` argument for `PositiveInt` `Field`
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, PositiveInt

try:
    # this won't work since `PositiveInt` takes precedence over the
    # constraints defined in `Field`, meaning they're ignored
    class Model(BaseModel):
        foo: PositiveInt = Field(lt=10)

except ValueError as e:
    print(e)

...

title: Dataclass instances are missing attributes at runtime
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hi everyone,

I've encountered a strange bug that was quite hard to track down. The symptom was that accessing a certain attribute of an object (an instance of `pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass`) led to an exception `'MyObject' object has no attribute 'my_attr_1'`, even though that attribute is defined on the class. This occurred in an async context (FastAPI application) during concurrent requests.

I still don't completely understand the details. It seems to me that it's a race condition that leads to incomplete initialization, but I haven't been able to make more headway into the problem.

There's a minimal reproducer attached. As far as I can see, the following elements of it are necessary: 
- `from __future__ import annotations` and defining `MyNestedObject` after `MyObject`
- using `pandera.typing.DataFrame` as a type annotation for the second attribute

Disclaimer: I am unsure if it is something specific to `pandera` that is causing this, or if one might reproduce this with another "heavy" annotation.

Pandera version: 0.26.1

In the example below, only `a.x` passes Pylance without errors

This didn't work in Python 3.14.0 either.

Pylance version 2025.9.100

### Example Code

```Python
"""Test out type hints for Pydantic."""  

from typing import reveal_type

from pydantic import ConfigDict
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class Plain:
    """No special dataclass freezing."""

    x: int=1


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Frozen:
    """Frozen dataclass."""

    x: int=1


@dataclass(config=ConfigDict(frozen=True))
class ConfigFrozen:
    """Frozen dataclass via config."""

    x: int=1


@dataclass(frozen=False)
class FrozenFalse:
    """Explicitly not frozen dataclass."""

    x: int=1


a = Plain()
reveal_type(a.x)

b = Frozen()
reveal_type(b.x)

c = ConfigFrozen()
reveal_type(c.x)

d = FrozenFalse()
reveal_type(d.x)
```

title: spam
Duplicated models in JSON schema generation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When Pydantic models are "deeply" "nested" (one of the attribute is also a Pydantic model), the `GenerateJsonSchema.generate_definitions` function sometimes creates duplicated identical schema (e.g. Schema-Input and Schema-Output).

I initially created a [discussion in FastAPI repository]( https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/14288#discussioncomment-14883512), thinking it was a bug in fastapi itself.

Turns out it's a bug in pydantic. See code to reproduce the bug.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic.json_schema import GenerateJsonSchema
from pydantic import BaseModel

REF_TEMPLATE = "#/components/schemas/{model}"

schema_generator = GenerateJsonSchema(ref_template=REF_TEMPLATE)


class Level3(BaseModel):
    level_4: str


class Level2(BaseModel):
    # if level_3 is a str, the test passes. If it's Level3, it fails.
    level_3: Level3
    # level_3: str


class Level1(BaseModel):
    level_2: Level2


inputs = [
    (Level1, "validation", Level1.__pydantic_core_schema__),
    (Level1, "serialization", Level1.__pydantic_core_schema__),
]


field_mapping, definitions = schema_generator.generate_definitions(inputs=inputs)


assert "Level1" in definitions
assert "Level1-Input" not in definitions
assert "Level1-Output" not in definitions
```

title: `validate_assignment=True` with after model validator fails to protect integrity of a model object
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When `validate_assignment` is set to `True` in `model_config` of a Pydantic model, I think it is reasonable to expect that a validation failure in an assignment to a field of a model object prevents any effect of the assignment. However, this doesn't seem to be the case when the validation failure is raised by an after model validator.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing_extensions import Self

import typing

import pydantic


def _meaning_of_life(value: int) -> int:
    print("Validating the meaning of life...")
    if value != 42 and value != 43:
        raise ValueError("The meaning of life must be 42 or 43 (checked in after field validator).")
    return value


class Foo(pydantic.BaseModel):
    bar: typing.Annotated[int, pydantic.AfterValidator(_meaning_of_life)]

    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(validate_assignment=True)

    @pydantic.model_validator(mode="after")
    def _check_bar(self) -> Self:
        print("Checking life through `bar` ...")
        if self.bar != 42:
            raise ValueError("The meaning of life must be 42 (checked in after model validator).")
        print("`bar` knows the true meaning of life!")
        return self


foo = Foo(bar=42)
print(foo)

try:
    foo.bar = 40
except pydantic.ValidationError as e:
    # The field validator raises an error
    print(e)
"""
1 validation error for Foo
bar
  Value error, The meaning of life must be 42 or 43 [type=value_error, input_value=40, input_type=int]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/value_error
"""

# Field validation failed. The field value was not modified, as expected.
assert foo.bar == 42


print("=============")

try:
    foo.bar = 43
except pydantic.ValidationError as e:
    # The field validator raises no error. The model validator raises an error
    print(e)
"""
1 validation error for Foo
  Value error, The meaning of life must be 42 (checked in after

title: extra ignored when from_attributes=True
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When validating an object using `.model_validate` using the object's attributes, the `extra` param is ignored, both from `.model_validate` kwargs or in the models' `.model_config`.

### Example Code

```Python
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


@dataclass
class Foo:
    bar: str
    baz: int


class FooValid(BaseModel, ):
    model_config = ConfigDict(
        strict=True,
        extra='forbid',
        from_attributes=True,
    )
    bar: str

foo = Foo(bar='asdf', baz=123)  # contains extra baz from Foo which is not in FooValid 

FooValid.model_validate(foo) # this only considers strict, eg fails with Foo(bar=b'asdf'), but doesnt raise for the extra baz

FooValid(**asdict(foo)) # this fails as expected (or contains __pydantic_extra__ when extra='allow')
```

title: Incorrect schema interaction with SQLModel for validated datetimes with 2.12
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

As of the 2.12.0 update, validated datetimes are committed to a postgres table as `TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE` instead of the previous (and correct) `TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`

I am unsure whether this is an SQLModel issue or Pydantic issue, but I am raising here as changing the Pydantic version produces the error and I see some similar model schema with validator issues raised over the last few weeks. If it seems unlikely to be related to issues like https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12417, please let me know so I can close this an open in the SQLModel repository as one linked to https://github.com/fastapi/sqlmodel/issues/1623 :)

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import datetime as dt
from datetime import timezone as tz
from typing_extensions import Annotated

from sqlmodel import TIMESTAMP, Field, SQLModel
from pydantic import BeforeValidator

TIMESTAMP_WITH_TZ: type = TIMESTAMP(timezone=True)

IntDatetime = Annotated[
    dt,
    BeforeValidator(lambda x: x if isinstance(x, dt) else dt.fromtimestamp(int(x) / 1000, tz=tz.utc)),
]

# This model works with both 2.11.10 and 2.12.0
class Message(SQLModel, table=True):
    time: dt = Field(sa_type=TIMESTAMP_WITH_TZ)

# This model works with <=2.11.10, but 2.12.0 generates an error
# `can't subtract offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes`
# As the SQL generated is $1::TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE
class Message(SQLModel, table=True):
    time: IntDatetime = Field(sa_type=TIMESTAMP_WITH_TZ)
```

With the error being raised on the commit, as in:

```python
session.add(model)
await session.commit()
```

title: Pydantic does not fully support annotations behind the `type` keyword
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello,

Thanks for this great piece of software.

It looks like Pydantic does not fully support annotations behind type aliases using the `type` keyword.

For instance, the following pattern works:

```python
from typing import Annotated, Sized

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

type MaxLength[T: Sized] = Annotated[T, Field(max_length=1)]


class Model(BaseModel):
    field: MaxLength[list]


# Works, as expected.
Model(field=[])

try:
    # Fails, which is expected.
    Model(field=[1, 2])
except ValidationError:
    pass
```

However, this pattern does not:

```python
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

type Aliased[T] = Annotated[T, Field(alias="alias")]

# Also fails
# type AliasedFloat = Annotated[float, Field(alias="alias")]

# Works
# AliasedFloat = Annotated[float, Field(alias="alias")]


class Model(BaseModel):
    field: Aliased[float]


# Works, which is "unexpected".
Model(field=0)

try:
    # Fails, which is "unexpected".
    Model(alias=1)
except ValidationError:
    pass
```

Of course this example has its issues: what if you used the type alias on two fields? But since it would work with `AliasedFloat = Annotated[float, Field(alias="alias")]`, this result surprised me...

I initially thought the `type` keyword was not supported at all. Is `alias` special-cased?

### Example Code

```Python
# TL;DR
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

type Aliased[T] = Annotated[T, Field(alias="alias")]

# Also fails
# type AliasedFloat = Annotated[float, Field(alias="alias")]

# Works
# AliasedFloat = Annotated[float, Field(alias="alias")]


class Model(BaseModel):
    field: Aliased[float]


Model(alias=0)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.3
        pydantic-core version: 2.41.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false

title: 2.12: AnyUrl.build percent encoding breaks database connection string
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Upgrading from Pydantic 2.11.9 / Core 2.33.2 to Pydantic 2.12.3 / Core 2.41.4 has changed the way database  DSNs are built using `py.MySQLDsn.build`, which I've traced back to a change in the behaviour of `AnyUrl.build`.

In 2.11.9 underscores were not replaced in the username and password; in 2.12.3 they are.

Encoding the underscore to '%5F' breaks Alembic config parsing:

```
  File "/.../app/alembic/env.py", line 31, in <module>
    config.set_main_option("sqlalchemy.url", sqla_url)
  File "/.../venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/alembic/config.py", line 344, in set_main_option
    self.set_section_option(self.config_ini_section, name, value)
  File "/.../venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/alembic/config.py", line 377, in set_section_option
    self.file_config.set(section, name, value)
  File "/.../pyenv/versions/3.12.2/lib/python3.12/configparser.py", line 1182, in set
    super().set(section, option, value)
  File "/.../pyenv/versions/3.12.2/lib/python3.12/configparser.py", line 872, in set
    value = self._interpolation.before_set(self, section, option,
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/.../pyenv/versions/3.12.2/lib/python3.12/configparser.py", line 374, in before_set
    raise ValueError("invalid interpolation syntax in %r at "
ValueError: invalid interpolation syntax in 'mysql+pymysql://my%5Fuser:my%5Fpassword@my_host:3306/my_db' at position 18
```

The SQLAlchemy database connection appears to handle this correctly so I'm not sure if this should be fixed in Alembic, but I can't see any reason for URLEncoding underscores, or for that matter any of the special characters explicitly allowed in URLs by [RFC 1738](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1738.html#:~:text=the%20special%20characters%20%22%24%2D_.%2B!*%27()%2C%22).

Some other issues which may be relevant: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12434, https://github.com/pydantic

title: Memory leak in model_rebuild(force=True)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

There appears to be a memory leak when calling `model_rebuild(force=True)` on models that have recursive dependencies.

The attached code provides a simple example. When running the test, the memory of the process gradually increases.

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Annotated, List, Literal, Union

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


Block = Annotated[
    Union[
        "Collection",
        "BlockGroup",
        "PredictiveModel",
    ],
    Field(discriminator="type"),
]


class PredictiveModel(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["predictive_model"]
    result: str


class BlockGroup(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["block_group"]
    blocks: List[Block] = []


class Collection(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["collection"]
    before_run: List[Block] = []
    after_run: List[Block] = []


def test_rebuild():
    i = 0
    while True:
        Collection.model_rebuild(force=True)
        BlockGroup.model_rebuild(force=True)
        PredictiveModel.model_rebuild(force=True)

        print(i)
        i += 1
```

title: 2.12: `datetime`, `date`, `time`, and `timedelta` silently and incorrectly serializes types incorrectly when dumping in Python mode when used as part of a union type
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

pydantic/pydantic-core@37ec6e750f49af4ada2682c3bf72123cb7cb8d2d introduced a regression in which objects on fields annotated as `datetime`, `date`, and `time` will be silently handled as if the object was of the incorrect type (falling through to `infer_to_python` unconditionally). This is most evident when using a union type, where I would expect that if serializing as a `datetime` fails, Pydantic would try the next available type. The logic seems to have been borrowed from the existing `timedelta` serialization which was also buggy in a similar way (verified on 2.11.7).

The actual issue seems to be in `pydantic-core`. The `TypeSerializer` used to downcast to ensure that the values are the correct type, but it does not do so now, only doing so whenever `mode='json'` is passed in. Indeed, the code works correctly if I pass `mode="json"` to `BaseModel.model_dump()`.

This particular bug is insidious because it happens that most of the time, `infer_to_python` actually does the right thing. But with a specific user-defined type, one can see that we are silently doing the wrong thing.

I have a PR to fix this within `pydantic-core`.

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import date, datetime, time, timedelta

from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic_core import core_schema


class SampleType:
    @classmethod
    def __get_pydantic_core_schema__(cls, source, handler) -> core_schema.CoreSchema:
        serialization_schema = core_schema.plain_serializer_function_ser_schema(lambda v: None)
        json_validation_schema = core_schema.no_info_plain_validator_function(
            function=lambda v: v, serialization=serialization_schema
        )
        python_validation_schema = core_schema.any_schema()

        return core_schema.json_or_python_schema(
            json_schema=json_validation_schema,
            python_schema=python_validation_schema,
            serialization=seria

title: 2.12: AnyUrl.build and AnyUrl's constructor produce different URLs when including special characters
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Using AnyUrl.build on a URL that has special characters (that need url-encoding) produces different results compared to passing a string to AnyUrl's constructor.

The code below would produce these results:

- `AnyUrl(string)`: `http://your-username-with-sp%E2%82%ACc!@l-ch/#rs:$%E2%82%ACCURE@!***@localhost:8080/path
`- `AnyUrl.build`: `http://your%2Dusername%2Dwith%2Dsp%E2%82%ACc%21%40l%2Dch%23rs:%24%E2%82%ACCURE%40%21%2A%2A%2A@localhost:8080/path`

Basically, it looks like AnyUrl's constructor does not urlencode all special characters.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import AnyUrl

scheme = "http"
host = "localhost"
port = 8080
path = "path"
username = "your-username-with-sp€c!@l-ch#rs"
password = "$€CURE@!***"


url_1 = AnyUrl(f"{scheme}://{username}:{password}@{host}:{port}/{path}")
print(url_1)
url_2 = AnyUrl.build(
    scheme=scheme, username=username, password=password, host=host, port=port, path=path
)
print(url_2)

body:

When building a generic model, having the `TypeVar` be bound to a protocol (or any other arbitrary/unsupported type) raises a `PydanticSchemaGenerationError`.

It would be great to be able to use arbitrary types as `TypeVar` bounds/constraints, provided that a concrete/supported type will be provided by explicit parameterization.

title: Fields with `exclude_if` should not be required in serialization JSON schema
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I've been happily using the new `exclude_if` feature, but it's not quite behaving as expected.

Expectation: the JSON schema in serialization mode marks fields with `exclude_if` as optional. The JSON schema accurately describes the structure of the serialization output.

Reality: such fields are marked as required, even though they may be absent in the actual output.

For example, consider this model:

```python
class MyModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    field: Annotated[str, pydantic.Field(exclude_if=lambda x: x == "null")]
```

Its input and output JSON schema are as follows, with the `field` marked as `required`:

```json5
{"properties": {"field": {"title": "Field", "type": "string"}},
 "required": ["field"],  // <--
 "title": "MyModel",
 "type": "object"}
```

However, dumping values like `MyModel(field="null")` would results in an empty dict `{}` which violates the promised schema. Given the model, I'd have expected the `field` to be required in validation mode, but optional in serialization mode.

This matters when using a code-first design for a JSON schema, e.g. when writing FastAPI applications. A potential workaround is to set a default value, but that's not quite equivalent.

I used the below script to inspect schemas for for BaseModels and Dataclasses. In its output, I would expect the `'required': ['field']` item to be present in the input schemas but absent in the output schemas. It could perhaps make sense to include schema-level tests for the Exclude-If feature, in addition to the existing serialization tests ([models](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/3154a95fbd442b02612561d91347eebf48fba79a/tests/test_main.py#L1389-L1404), [dataclasses](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/3154a95fbd442b02612561d91347eebf48fba79a/tests/test_dataclasses.py#L3244-L3258)).

(Anecdotally, I've also seen warnings like `Expected 3 fields but got 2` as triggered by [this Pydantic-Cor

Pydantic 2.12 fails to resolve deferred imports in Python 3.14

The example code produces this output:

{'pattern': '^[\\da-fA-F]{32}$',

annotation=Union[str, NoneType] required=False default=None metadata=[_PydanticGeneralMetadata(pattern='^[\\da-fA-F]{32}$')]
{'anyOf': [{'pattern': '^[\\da-fA-F]{32}$', 'type': 'string'},

annotation=Union[Annotated[str, BeforeValidator], NoneType] required=False default=None metadata=[_PydanticGeneralMetadata(pattern='^[\\da-fA-F]{32}$')]
{'anyOf': [{'type': 'string'}, {'type': 'null'}],

title: Add FastDepends to third-party test suite
body:
[FastDepends](https://github.com/Lancetnik/FastDepends) is a dependency injection library that serves as the underlying core for both the [AG2](https://github.com/ag2ai/ag2) and [FastStream](https://github.com/ag2ai/faststream) frameworks.

It provides Pydantic-based dependency resolution and type validation, making it heavily dependent on Pydantic’s behavior and typing system.

Since both AG2 and FastStream rely deeply on Pydantic models and validation mechanics, including FastDepends in the third-party test suite would help ensure:

* Early detection of breaking changes in Pydantic that could affect dependency injection and validation in these frameworks.
* Greater confidence for framework maintainers when upgrading Pydantic versions.
* Improved feedback loop between the Pydantic team and the broader ecosystem of libraries built on it.

## Proposal

Add FastDepends to Pydantic’s thirdparty tests, similar to other ecosystem integrations (e.g., FastAPI, Django Ninja, Redis OM).

cc @Viicos

title: Serializers and Validators not run with serialize_as_any=True
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

We have upgraded to pydantic v2.12 and have run across some issues with serialize_as_any and field serialization/validation. We have a use case where we use serialize as any to dump a dictionary and save to mongo. 

We have a type on a model that is `List[MyAbstractType]` and if we put different types into that list and serialize it with out serialize as any we only get fields defined in the MyAbstractType class. To avoid this we use the "serialize as any" param and a custom baseclass that will find the correct implementation based off a discriminator field included in the model.  

In v2.11 this worked fine, but now when we try to do the same thing any custom serializers or validators are not run on fields that have them defined. This seems like a bug, and when serializing as any I would expect field validators/serializers to still be run if they are defined on known fields for the type. 

### Example Code

```Python
import json
from pydantic import BaseModel, BeforeValidator, PlainSerializer, field_serializer, field_validator
from typing import Annotated

##################
##Annotated examples
def serialize_as_json(value) -> str:
    return json.dumps(value)


def validate_json(value):
    if isinstance(value, str):
        return json.loads(value)
    return value


class JSONStringExample(BaseModel):
    field_1: Annotated[list[dict], BeforeValidator(validate_json), PlainSerializer(serialize_as_json)]

#Does not work
def test_annotated_serialize_as_any():
    example = JSONStringExample(field_1=[{"my_thing": "my_value"}])
    value = example.model_dump(serialize_as_any=True)
    assert isinstance(value["field_1"], str)

#Works fine
def test_annotated_serialize_normal():
    example = JSONStringExample(field_1=[{"my_thing": "my_value"}])
    value = example.model_dump()
    assert isinstance(value["field_1"], str)


###################
# Method based serializer/validator.

title: Breaking change: values changed to ValidationInfo type, breaking existing validation patterns and datamodel codegen
body: ### Initial Checks - [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description After upgrading to Pydantic 2.12+ (and pydantic-core 2.41+), I noticed a breaking change in the behavior of the @model_validator decorator. Previously, in [mode="after"], the second argument was either the model instance or a dictionary of field values. However, in v2.12+, the argument passed to the second argument is now a ValidationInfo object. This change breaks existing validation logic, which we built around the second argument. This also breaks datamodel_code_generator (0.30), since it is expecting values to be a dict and not a Validation Info ### Example Code ```Python @model_validator(mode="after") def random_function(cls, values) print("-----------------CLS-----------") print(type(cls)) print(cls) print("-----------------VALUES-----------") print(type(values)) print(values) prints the followwing in version 2.11.7 / pydantic-core 2.33.2: -----------------CLS----------- -----------------VALUES----------- in version 2.12.1 / pydantic-core 2.41.3: ------CLASS----- my_model_field="some-value" -------------VALUES-------- ValidationInfo(config={'title': 'ValidModel', 'extra_fields_behavior': 'forbid'}, context=None, data=None, field_name=None) ``` ### Python, Pydantic & OS Version ```Text pydantic version: 2.12.1 pydantic-core version: 2.41.3 pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false python version: 3.12.11 (main, Jun 4 2025, 08:56:18) [GCC 9.4.0] platform: Linux-5.15.153.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-x86_64-with-glibc2.31 ```

title: Cannot override a validation in a sub-class
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I cannot override a validation that uses subclass fields.

In the below example, the rule is: I am not allowed to change anything about how `Base` is declared. `Child` has to inherit from `Base`. It is assumed that each validation introduces irreversible changes to `val`. Otherwise I am open to any suggestions that do not involve hacking the model (i.e. `object.__setattr__`, etc).

The core of the issue is that `validate_1` always runs before anything related to child fields (`added_field_0`). I need to move this validation later in the subclass but I cannot.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator, ValidationInfo

class Base(BaseModel, frozen=True, validate_default=True):
    field_0: str = ""
    field_1: str = ""
    field_2: str = ""

    @field_validator("field_1")
    def validate_1(cls, val: str, info: ValidationInfo) -> str:
        info.data["field_0"]
        return val

    @field_validator("field_2")
    def validate_2(cls, val: str, info: ValidationInfo) -> str:
        info.data["field_1"]
        return val


class Child(Base):
    added_field_0: str = ""

    @field_validator("field_1")
    def validate_1(cls, val: str, info: ValidationInfo) -> str:
        info.data["field_0"]
        info.data["added_field_0"]
        return val


Child()
```

title: `model_rebuild()` Incorrectly Reports Discriminator Collision for Reused Discriminated Unions
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

  TLDR:
  - Error: "Value 'X' for discriminator 'Y' mapped to multiple choices" during model_rebuild()
  - Setup: Same discriminated union reused in multiple models
  - Evidence: Values ARE unique within their union
  - Version: Pydantic 2.12.1
  - Link to previous related fixes (#6340, #8904) that don't solve this case

# Bug Report: `model_rebuild()` Incorrectly Reports Discriminator Collision for Reused Discriminated Unions

## Environment

- **Pydantic Version**: 2.12.1
- **Python Version**: 3.12
- **OS**: Linux (WSL2)

## Summary

When the same discriminated union is referenced through multiple nested model paths, `model_rebuild()` incorrectly raises `TypeError: Value 'X' for discriminator 'Y' mapped to multiple choices`, even though:
- Each discriminator field name is unique across different unions
- Each discriminator value is unique within its union
- No actual value collision exists

## Minimal Reproduction

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import Union, Optional, Literal, TYPE_CHECKING
from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

# Define a discriminated union
class StatReference(BaseModel):
    attribute_reference_type: Literal["stat_reference"] = "stat_reference"
    base: str

class ResourceReference(BaseModel):
    attribute_reference_type: Literal["resource_reference"] = "resource_reference"
    base: str

AttributeReference = Annotated[
    Union[StatReference, ResourceReference],
    Field(discriminator="attribute_reference_type")
]

# Use this union in multiple nested paths
class ResourceCheck(BaseModel):
    attribute: AttributeReference  # Path 1

class ResourceCondition(BaseModel):
    condition_type: Literal["resource_condition"] = "resource_condition"
    check: ResourceCheck

class StatusCondition(BaseModel):
    condition_type: Literal["status_condition"] = "status_condition"
    has_status: str

Conditio

Follow up to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11748

Thanks so much for rolling out a fix so quickly for the original bug 🙏 

Unfortunately it seems that the bug is still there as soon as we change the original MRE to make the `Other` model class inherit from another model 🥲 

### Example Code

```Python
from typing import Generic, Optional, TypeVar
from pydantic import BaseModel


class ConfiguredBaseModel(BaseModel):
    """Base model to share configuration settings."""

    #     revalidate_instances="always",
    #     validate_assignment=True,
    # )


T = TypeVar("T")


class Base(ConfiguredBaseModel, Generic[T]):
    t: Optional[T]


class Other(ConfiguredBaseModel): # if we change this to inherit from BaseModel then the model validates as expected

a = Other.model_validate({"children": {"t": {"children": {"t": None}}}})

title: Serializer for extra fields not used
body:
From https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1835/files#r2425738743:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Model(BaseModel, extra='allow'):
    a: int
    __pydantic_extra__: dict[str, int]


m = Model(a=1, extra=1)
m.a = 'not_an_int'
m.model_dump_json()  # PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue warning raised

m.a = 1
m.extra = 'not_an_int'
m.model_dump_json()  # No warning raised
```

Also https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1835/files#r2425737760 should probably be supported.

title: 2.12: `serialize_as_any` issues
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic has a serialization concept called *serialize as any*, which can be enabled globally during serialization (using the `serialize_as_any` parameter) or per field (using the [`SerializeAsAny`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/functional_serializers/#pydantic.functional_serializers.SerializeAsAny) annotation). When used, Pydantic treats the type annotation of the field as if it were `Any`, and tries to infer the value using its actual type (see the [documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#serializing-with-duck-typing) for more details).

Up until now, the `serialize_as_any` flag and `SerializeAsAny` annotation were behaving quite differently, and they were [unified](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1478) in 2.12 (actually, the `serialize_as_any` flag behavior was aligned to match the `SerializeAsAny` annotation).

Unfortunately this also led to regressions where cases that previously serialized in 2.11 no longer serialize in 2.12, because dropping the type information forces `pydantic-core` to use type inference, and not all types have built-in inference in `pydantic-core`:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, IPvAnyAddress

class BugReproModel(BaseModel):
    ip_address: IPvAnyAddress

bug = BugReproModel(ip_address="192.168.1.1")

json_data = bug.model_dump_json(serialize_as_any=True)
# PydanticSerializationError: Unable to serialize unknown type: <class 'ipaddress.IPv4Address'>
```

**If you are using the `serialize_as_any` flag and encountering serialization errors in 2.12**:

1. Check if you can make use of the `SerializeAsAny` annotation instead, and apply it only on fields if this is necessary.
2. If you don't control the types you are serializing (e.g. a Pydantic model defined in a third party library), we'd like to know more about your `serialize_as_any` usage. If you use it to achieve [duck typing serialization](http

title: Docstrings leaking into JSON Schema descriptions impact agentic production systems
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In Pydantic v2, a model’s class docstring automatically becomes the "description" field in the generated JSON Schema. This is convenient for documentation, but it implicitly makes comments part of the runtime contract because many runtimes (especially LLM and agent frameworks) now consume those schemas directly to decide behavior.

A harmless docstring edit can change tool selection or behavior in production.

This coupling is amplified because LLM platforms explicitly encourage Pydantic for structured output/tool schemas (ie [OpenAI](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs) and I believe PydanticAI has same risk although I haven't had the time to experiment there yet).

Developers don’t expect docstrings to affect runtime behaviour.
The software folklore tale [The Inner JSON Effect](https://thedailywtf.com/articles/the-inner-json-effect) illustrates how comments bleeding into contracts can become a subtle foot-gun.


LLM agents often read schema "description"s to decide which tool/model to use; a teammate “polishing” a docstring can alter production outcomes.
On top of that, docstrings are frequently edited without the same review rigor as public contracts.


### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Foo(BaseModel):
    """An example base model"""
    pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(Foo.model_json_schema())

# > {'description': 'An example base model', 'properties': {}, 'title': 'Foo', 'type': 'object'}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

title: 💥 `pydantic>=2.12.0a1` Massive bug and breaking change: `FieldInfo` in `create_model`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In our code we dynamically create pydantic models. For this, we use `create_model` function. In all version before `2.12.0a1`, `FieldInfo` could be changed dynamically and it would be respected by `create_model`. But since `2.12.0a1`, new values (at least `default`) is not respected by `create_model`, if it's set dynamically.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import create_model
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo

field_info1 = FieldInfo(
    annotation=str,
)
field_info1.default = None  # The only difference: set dynamically

field_info2 = FieldInfo(
    annotation=str,
    default=None,
)

fields1 = {
    "foo": (str, field_info1),
}
fields2 = {
    "foo": (str, field_info2),
}

model1 = create_model(
    "MyModel1",
    **fields1,
)
model2 = create_model(
    "MyModel1",
    **fields2,
)

print(model1.model_fields)  # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=True)}
print(model2.model_fields)  # {'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=False, default=None)}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.12.0
pydantic-core version: 2.41.1
```

### All pydantic versions with their output:

```python

{'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=False)}
```

- `2.2.0` (Aug 17, 2023) ✅

```python
{'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=False)}
{'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=False)}
```

- `2.3.0` ✅
- `2.4.0` ✅
- `2.5.0` ✅
- `2.6.0` ✅

```python

```python

{'foo': FieldInfo(annotation=str, required=False, default=None)}
```

- `2.12.0b1` 💥

title: 2.12.0: field_serializer not called by model_dump(mode='json', serialize_as_any=True) in v2.12
body:
### Initial Checks

### Description

Field serializers seem to be ignored in version 2.12 when calling `model_dump` with `serialize_as_any=True`.  The example code fails with a `PydanticSerializationError` on v2.12 but runs fine on 2.11.9.  Expected behavior is no error.  Serializer *is* called correctly when `serialize_as_any=False`.

from pydantic import BaseModel, field_serializer, SerializationInfo
import numpy as np

class Test(BaseModel, arbitrary_types_allowed=True):

    @field_serializer("ar")
    def serialize_ar(self, v: np.ndarray, info: SerializationInfo):

test = Test(ar=np.identity(4))

title: No GC for Pydantic Objects
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

The msgspec library has an interesting feature where you can declare a class as gc=False if you are sure it has no cyclic dependencies, and want to avoid gc costs.  Is this something you would consider for Pydantic?

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [ ] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [ ] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

title: model_validate_strings cannot validate optional fields

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

class Model(BaseModel):
