- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In Pydantic v2.12.0, when validating a model with an empty defaultdict, Pydantic now extracts values from the defaultdict's default_factory instead of using the model's field defaults. This is a change in behavior versus v2.11.10 where empty defaultdicts were treated as missing values.

The example code below passes in v2.11.10 while in v2.12.0 it outputs `outer_key=InnerModel(inner_key=[])`

### Example Code

```Python
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class InnerModel(BaseModel):
    inner_key: Optional[list[str]] = Field(None)

class OuterModel(BaseModel):
    outer_key: Optional[InnerModel] = None

asset_to_field_candidates = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))

expected = OuterModel(outer_key=None)
actual = OuterModel.model_validate(asset_to_field_candidates)
assert actual == expected, actual
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

title: 2.12.0: `pydantic.ValidationInfo.field_name` reports incorrect field_name when validating default value
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The nightly test in our repo caught an unexpected change in behaviour going from 2.11.9 to 2.12.0. Our code sometimes rely on the `field_name` obtained from `pydantic.ValidatorInfo` in a `field_validator`, but in 2.12.0 it is either return None, or the field from the outer model.

Here's the minimal reproducible example:
``` python
import pydantic

class Foo(pydantic.BaseModel):

    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(validate_default=True)

    bar: float = 0.0

    @pydantic.field_validator("bar")
    @classmethod
    def my_validator(cls, value, info: pydantic.ValidationInfo):
        print(info.field_name)
        return value

# Expectation: both cases should print `bar`
Foo(bar=1.0)  # prints `bar`
Foo()         # prints `None` in v2.12.0a1. In v2.11.9 this prints `bar` as expected


class FooHandler(pydantic.BaseModel):

    foo: Foo


FooHandler(foo={"bar": 1.0})  # prints `bar`
FooHandler(foo={})            # prints `foo` in v2.12.0a1. In v2.11.9 this prints `bar` as expected
```
Bisecting further it seems like the change in behaviour was introduced in the alpha release of 2.12.0

title: `FieldInfo` 2.12 issues
body:
Some changes in Pydantic 2.12 related to the `FieldInfo` class resulted in issues for libraries relying on this class and its methods. Such changes impact behavior on:
- setting attributes on `FieldInfo` instances
- copying `FieldInfo` instances
- interactions with `FieldInfo` subclasses

While we feel some of these use cases are reasonable and could be better supported, many external libraries are setting too many expectations on the `FieldInfo` logic, and I don't know if we'll be able to fully preserve compatibility for every use case.

## Context

Before diving into the details, I'll give some background on the motivation for these changes. Starting in 2.11, we started refactoring the logic around the `FieldInfo` class,  as it was in a really bad state, and the source of multiple (~10!) separate bugs.

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11388 (in 2.11) and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11898 (in 2.12) were introduced to fix these bugs.

While doing so, we realized many third party libraries were heavily relying on undocumented behavior (e.g. retaining `FieldInfo` subclasses during merging) and some of its methods (e.g. `from_annotation()`, `merge_field_infos()`. This was a mistake from us, we shouldn't have made these public. Most of the bugs that we tried to fix were actually coming from them -- as a result, third party libraries were somewhat relying on bugous behavior).

For reference, here are some examples of inconsistent/bugous behavior, which as a consequence made user logic very brittle:

```python
class ChildFieldInfo(FieldInfo):
    ...

type(ChildFieldInfo.from_annotation(typing.Annotated[typing.Any, ChildFieldInfo()]))
#> ChildFieldInfo
type(ChildFieldInfo.from_annotation(typing.Annotated[typing.Any, ChildFieldInfo(), ChildFieldInfo()]))
#> FieldInfo, subclass not preserved

# ---

f: FieldInfo = ...

f_copy = copy.copy(f)

f_copy.metadata.append(...)  # Affects `f` as well

# ---

# In 2.11:

class Model(BaseModel):
    f: int

f =

title: Prevent mutations to `model_config` after model definition
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

It is currently perfectly fine at runtime to mutate the `model_config` class attribute for a model. Some users try to do so to e.g. temporarily unfreeze a model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Model(BaseModel):
    a: int = 1

m = Model()

Model.model_config['frozen'] = False
m.a = 2
Model.model_config['frozen'] = True
```

However, this is an anti-pattern because:
- It only "accidentally" works for some configuration values (e.g. `frozen`), but not all (the model config is inspected in `BaseModel__setattr__()` for `frozen`, but other settings such as [`validate_default`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.validate_default), [`val_temporal_unit`](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.val_temporal_unit) etc are affecting validation in `pydantic-core`, where configuration is _not_ read from the model, but rather derived at model build time).
- it is a class variable, and can result to unexpected behavior in multi-threaded environments for instance.
- Since we introduced [caching of `setattr()` handlers](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/10868) in 2.11, the example above doesn't work as expected anymore (see https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/12361, https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/11729).

We could use an immutable mapping structure to expose `model_config` (e.g. [`MappingProxyType`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/types.html#types.MappingProxyType)), but this will be breaking.

Before V3, we could set the items of the `ConfigDict` as [`ReadOnly`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.ReadOnly). We might need to differentiate the current `ConfigDict` class (may be used independently, mutated by users before

Hi there and thanks for your work maintaining pydantic :)
Today, our CI suite failed because it tried to use v2.12 of pydantic, which broke our filtering system. After some investigation, I found a workaround [here](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/11898/files#diff-bfaaac2ae0a71d9686a272982d29d0bed82bfaacf8b5b30e2b88ed2543e74a3bL216-R217), and opened [a PR](https://github.com/iiasa/ixmp4/pull/206) to incorporate the fix on our side.

title: Fields of frozen models remember if they were unfrozen if assigned to, breaking immutability guarantees
body:
# Facts

This test suite succeeds with the current version of pydantic (2.12.0) and pydantic-core (2.41.1):

```py
import pytest
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, ValidationError


class MyModel(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(frozen=True)
    x: int
    y: int


def test_before():
    obj = MyModel(x=1, y=0)

    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="frozen"):
        obj.x = 2

    assert obj.x == 1


def test_unfreeze_refreeze():
    obj = MyModel(x=3, y=0)

    MyModel.model_config["frozen"] = False
    obj.x = 4
    MyModel.model_config["frozen"] = True

    assert obj.x == 4


def test_after_x():
    obj = MyModel(x=5, y=0)

    # This assignment should NOT succeed, but it does!
    obj.x = 6

    assert obj.x == 6


def test_after_y():
    obj = MyModel(x=0, y=5)

    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="frozen"):
        obj.y = 6

    assert obj.y == 5
```

If you comment out the line `obj.x = 4`, then `test_after_x` will fail with a ValidationError, as you would expect.

I have confirmed this also in pydantic-core 2.33.2, but the issue is **not** present in pydantic-core 2.27.2! I have not yet bisected further than that.

# My assumptions

It seems that fields remember if they were every assigned to, and if so, they ignore whether the model is frozen or not. If an assignment was attempted while the model was frozen, the field does **not** remember that.

The reason I'm reporting this in pydantic-core and not pydantic is that I've traced the code path to `validate_assignment`, which is implemented here, not in pydantic.

# Why I think this is an issue

`ConfigDict` is not frozen, and `model_dict` is also not protected in any way. It is very easy to change the configuration of a model at runtime. In most cases, this works as expected, and it **did** work as expected in the past.

Being able to temporarily change "frozen" is actually a particularly useful feature when writing tests, because it is currently somewhat tricky to upd

title: 2.12.0: create_model() ignores modified FieldInfo.default in v2.12, causing fields to become required
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

## Bug Summary

When using `create_model()` with a modified `FieldInfo` object that has `default=None` set, Pydantic 2.12 ignores this default value and resets it to `PydanticUndefined`, making the field required even though it should be optional.

This is a regression from Pydantic 2.11.7, where modified `FieldInfo` defaults were correctly preserved.

## Impact

This breaks libraries like `instructor` (v1.9.2) that rely on dynamically creating partial models for streaming LLM responses. When streaming partial responses, empty initial chunks (`{}`) fail validation with "Field required" errors instead of treating fields as optional.

## Expected Behavior

When a `FieldInfo` object with `default=None` is passed to `create_model()`, the created model should preserve this default value, making the field optional.

## Actual Behavior (2.12.0)

The `default=None` is ignored and reset to `PydanticUndefined`, making the field required and causing validation errors on empty dicts.

## Behavior in 2.11.7

Works correctly - the `default=None` is preserved and the field is optional.

## Example Code

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, create_model
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo
from typing import Optional
from copy import deepcopy

# Create a FieldInfo with required field
original_field = Field(..., description='Required field')

# Modify it to be optional (what instructor does)
tmp_field = deepcopy(original_field)
tmp_field.annotation = Optional[str]
tmp_field.default = None

print(f'Before create_model:')
print(f'  default: {tmp_field.default}')
print(f'  is_required: {tmp_field.is_required()}')  # Returns False ✓

# Create model with modified FieldInfo
TestModel = create_model(
    'TestModel',
    response=(tmp_field.annotation, tmp_field)
)

print(f'\nAfter create_model:')
field_info = TestModel.model_fields['response']
print(f'  annotation: {field_info.annotation}')
print

title: Inconsistent logic of validation error names
body:
Validation errors (listed on https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/errors/validation_errors/) tend to be confusing, because sometimes they mean "value is wrong _because_", and sometimes they mean "value is wrong _unless_". Compare errors like `too_short` or `set_item_not_hashable` with `greater_than` or `date_future`. Following the logic of the first two examples, I'd expect the error `greater_than` to mean "number is wrong because it's greater than x", and the error `date_future` to mean "date is wrong because it's in the future" - but they actually mean the opposite. So they should probably be named `not_greater_than` and `date_not_future` instead.

There are more cases like this, and I guess fixing all the names would be a major change, but maybe it's worth considering for v3.

Edit: as for numbers, error names like `not_greater_than_equal` also become difficult to understand in practice. Something like `number_too_small`/`number_too_large`, with `min`/`max` context instead of `ge`/`le`, would probably be much better.

title: 2.12.0: field name is missing when there are multiple field validators
body:
### Initial Checks

### Description

I have a class that have multiple field validators with `*` field and wrap mode. Inside the validator, I need to access the field name for custom validation. In version 2.12, the field name become `None` in one of the validators. It is not validator specific, looks like first validator "consume" the field name. 

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    foo: str

    @field_validator('*', mode='wrap')
    @classmethod
    def first_validator(cls, v, handler, info):
        print("first validator", info.field_name)
        return handler(v)

    @field_validator('*', mode='wrap')
    @classmethod
    def second_validator(cls, v, handler, info):
        print("second validator", info.field_name)
        return handler(v)
    
MyModel.model_validate({"foo": "test"})

second validator foo
first validator None

title: FalsePositive: UnsupportedFieldAttributeWarning - Query Parameters with a Pydantic Model raises UnsupportedFieldAttributeWarning with pydantic 2.12
body: ### Initial Checks - [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description With the introduction of 2.12, pydantic now throws the `UnsupportedFieldAttributeWarning` warning when using [Query Parameter Models](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-param-models/#query-parameters-with-a-pydantic-model) with a global alias generator. I think this is a false positive, as the alias is applied (in contrast to what the warning says): I'm not 100% sure where the issue lies. I can see that there is already some handling for FastAPI stuff before issuing the warning. I created an issue here, because FastAPI works as expected, although the pydantic warnings claims that it would not. ### Example Code ```Python from typing import Annotated, Literal from fastapi import FastAPI, Query from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict from pydantic.alias_generators import to_camel app = FastAPI() class FilterParams(BaseModel): # ❌ Using an alias generator here will trigger the warning model_config = ConfigDict(alias_generator=to_camel) limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100) offset: int = Field(0, ge=0) order_by: Literal["created_at", "updated_at"] = "created_at" tags: list[str] = [] @app.get("/items/") async def read_items(filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]): return filter_query ``` ### Python, Pydantic & OS Version ```Text pydantic version: 2.12.0 pydantic-core version: 2.41.1 pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false python version: 3.13.7 (main, Aug 14 2025, 11:12:11) [Clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.0.13.3)] platform: macOS-15.6.1-arm64-arm-64bit-Mach-O related packages: pydantic-settings-2.11.0 mypy-1.18.2 email-validator-2.3.0 pydantic-extra-types-2.10.5 fastapi-0.118.0 typing_extensions-4.15.0 c

title: 2.12.0: `ValidationInfo.field_name` is `None` when validating default values with `validate_default=True`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In a model with `validate_default=True`, when `@field_validator` is validating the default value of a field, the `field_name` property of the `ValidationInfo` instance has a value of `None` instead of the actual field name. 

This appears to be a regression in Pydantic 2.12.0 - I wasn't able to reproduce it in 2.11.10.

### Example Code

```Python
# /// script
# dependencies = [
#     "pydantic==2.12.0",
# ]
# ///


from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationInfo, field_validator


class Model(BaseModel, validate_default=True):
    field: str = "default"

    @field_validator("field", mode="after")
    @classmethod
    def _print_field_name(cls, value: str, info: ValidationInfo):
        print(info.field_name)
        return value


# expected output: field
#   actual output: None
Model()

# output: field
Model(field="input value")
```

title: bug: Pydantic dumps an object according to the original field type schema, instead of the real one
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

If we have some nested DTO objects and want to use a parent class as a field annotation, Pydantic ignores the real object type during dumping. It uses the original field annotation for dumping, which leads to incorrect (I believe) behavior

Original issue: https://github.com/ag2ai/faststream/issues/2553

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class BaseClass(BaseModel):
    pass

class ChildClass(BaseClass):
    data: str

class ParentClass(BaseModel):
    nested: BaseClass

obj = ParentClass(nested=ChildClass(data="pydantic"))
print(obj.model_dump())  # {'nested': {}}
```

But, if we declare a parent class with a field, Pydantic uses its schema to create a child object

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class BaseClass(BaseModel):
    original: str

class ChildClass(BaseClass):
    data: str

class ParentClass(BaseModel):
    nested: BaseClass

obj = ParentClass(nested=ChildClass(data="pydantic", original="python"))
print(obj.model_dump())  # {'nested': {'original': 'python'}}
```

The problem the same for

```python
obj.model_dump()
obj.model_dump_json()
pydantic_core.to_json(obj)
pydantic_core.to_jsonable_python(obj)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic==2.12.0
pydantic-core==2.41.1
```

title: 2.12.0: Use case for `merge_field_infos()`: Generating a programmatically modified model
body:
## Why am I writing this?

While trying out Pydantic v2.12.0b1 on my codebase, I got a deprecation warning about `FieldInfo.merge_field_infos()`, instructing me to open an issue if I relied on this method. So here we go 😄 

## The use case

My codebase has a Pydantic model defined "the regular way", i.e. the module source contains classes derived from `BaseModel`. JSON files corresponding to this model are both validated and dumped. So far, so good.
Additionally, the application provides a way of specifying "overlays", JSON files containing basically arbitrary parts of the model. These must be valid if present, but any part of the model may be missing. the validation of overlays also happens through Pydantic, using a modified version of the regular models, where everything is the same but all fields are optional.

The relevant part of the code base looks like this:

```python3
def make_model_with_all_fields_optional(cls: type[BaseModel]) -> type[BaseModel]:
    """Returns a new Pydantic model based on `cls`, but with all fields optional."""
    computed_field_names = set(cls.model_computed_fields.keys())
    fields = {
        field_name: _get_field_tuple(field_info)
        for field_name, field_info in cls.model_fields.items()
        if field_name not in computed_field_names
    }

    model = create_model(
        f"{cls.__name__}_AllFieldsOptional",
        __doc__=cls.__doc__,
        **fields,
    )
    return model

def _get_field_tuple(field_info: FieldInfo) -> tuple[type[Any], FieldInfo]:
    """Returns a tuple as required by Pydantic's ``create_model()`` API."""
    annotation = field_info.annotation
    assert annotation is not None, (
        "At this point, the annotation must be set. This is either a bug in Pydantic or the application."
    )

    if issubclass(annotation, BaseModel):
        annotation = make_model_with_all_fields_optional(annotation)

    if field_info.default is PydanticUndefined and field_info.default_factory is None:
        # Th

title: Optimize validation for `Sequence`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Generally, we try to encourage users as much as possible to use built-in types (such as `list` or `tuple`) over the abstract collection ones. However, `Sequence` is a pretty common type being used and we can't do much about it (we can't emit warnings for this), and the validation function is currently not that great:

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/effbce64c51c5c7fbf6951b8960dd4e8957a810b/pydantic/_internal/_validators.py#L28-L65

title: Add utility function to convert Pydantic model fields to dictionary representation for API documentation
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description


## Summary
Add a utility function that converts Pydantic model fields to a human-readable dictionary representation, particularly useful for generating API documentation and Swagger/OpenAPI schemas.

## Motivation
When building APIs with Pydantic models, developers often need to programmatically generate documentation that shows the expected structure of request/response bodies. Currently, there's no built-in way to convert a Pydantic model field structure into a clean, readable dictionary format that can be easily used for:

- Auto-generating API documentation
- Creating Swagger/OpenAPI schema descriptions
- Providing clear examples in API responses
- Debugging and development tools

## Proposed Solution
A new utility function `pydantic_model_to_dict()` that would:

1. **Handle nested models**: Recursively process Pydantic models within models
2. **Support primitive types**: Convert basic Python types (int, str, float, etc.) to readable names
3. **Handle Optional types**: Properly format `Union[Type, None]` as `Optional[Type]`
4. **Support Union types**: Format multiple type unions as `[type1, type2]`
5. **Handle collections**: Format `List` and `Dict` types with their contained types
6. **Support Enums**: Display enum values as readable options

## Example Usage
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, List, Dict
from enum import Enum

class Status(Enum):
    ACTIVE = "active"
    INACTIVE = "inactive"

class User(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: Optional[int]
    status: Status
    tags: List[str]
    metadata: Dict[str, str]

# Would output something like:
# {
#   "name": "str",
#   "age": "Optional[int]", 
#   "status": "<active, inactive>",
#   "tags": "List[str]",
#   "metadata": "Dict[str, str]"
#

Allowing something similar to:

title: Change the default `extra` behavior for dataclasses and TypedDicts
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Following https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785, this is a feature request tracking the potential change of the default `extra` behavior for TypedDicts and dataclasses.

Samuel mentioned in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785 that it should be `'forbid'` by default for dataclasses and TypedDicts, but without any motivation.

For dataclasses, my best guess would be that it should mirror the standard library if possible (which doesn't allow extra values). On the other hand, an argument can be made about Pydantic dataclasses and models to behave as closely as possible.

For TypedDicts, things are a bit more unclear (see [PEP 728](https://peps.python.org/pep-0728/) and https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/10785#issuecomment-3355959592).

- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I was recently asked for a way to require ascii-only text, and couldn't see anything better than requiring a regex accepting the ascii charset range in `StringConstraints`.

I would think that an `ascii_only` option (name to be bikeshed) would be more user-friendly. It's potentially also possible to check for ascii-only characters a lot cheaper without using a regex.

title: Support objects defining `__complex__()` (and others) method for `complex`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

The [`complex()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#complex) constructor accepts objects defining `__complex__()`, `__float__()` or `__index__()`, but these are not accepted by Pydantic (Which feels inconsistent considering it allows it for floats). Probably we should just delegate directly to the `complex()` constructor? This will most likely be faster that what we do know (a bunch of `isinstance()` calls).

title: Support `tuple` input for decimals
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

The [`Decimal`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/decimal.html#decimal.Decimal) type constructor accepts a three-tuple:

> If value is a [`tuple`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#tuple), it should have three components, a sign (`0` for positive or `1` for negative), a [`tuple`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#tuple) of digits, and an integer exponent. For example, `Decimal((0, (1, 4, 1, 4), -3))` returns `Decimal('1.414')`.

But this isn't accepted by Pydantic, and should probably.

title: hide_input_in_errors flag per field
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Given some BaseModel with sensitive data, I know I can use `repr=False` and `ConfigDict(hide_input_in_errors=True)` to not show that data in representation and errors. However I'd like to show the `username` input on errors in this model:

```python
class User(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(hide_input_in_errors=True)
 
    username: str = Field(min_length=5)
    password: str = Field(min_length=8, repr=False)
```

What I'd like is the possibility to have `hide_input_in_errors` at the `Field` level instead of the model level:

```python
class User(BaseModel): 
    username: str = Field(min_length=5)
    password: str = Field(min_length=8, repr=False, hide_input_in_errors=True)
```

title: Partial function not working in predicate
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I ran into an issue when using the new pipelines API in combination with `functools.partial` as predicate.
This check can also be done by using a lambda, but since `partial` is pretty standard and pre-installed in `functools` I assume there are users that might use that (e.g. if a partial function is already used in the code and just re-used on the predicate).

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import TypeAdapter, ValidationError
from pydantic.experimental.pipeline import validate_as
from typing import Annotated
from functools import partial

incr1 = partial(lambda x, incr: x == incr, incr=1)
ta_str_to_int = TypeAdapter[int](Annotated[int, validate_as(int).predicate(incr1)])
assert ta_str_to_int.validate_python(1) == 1
try:
    ta_str_to_int.validate_python(2)
except ValidationError:
    print("This is expected since 1 != 2")
```

body:

It looks like OrderedDict fields are broken, as can be seen in the example below:

from collections import OrderedDict
import pydantic
class Model(pydantic.BaseModel):
    foo: OrderedDict
foo = OrderedDict({"a": 1, "b": 2})
foo.move_to_end("a")
model1 = Model(foo=foo) # this doesn't work
model2 = Model(foo=dict(foo)) # this works
print(foo.keys(), model1.foo.keys(), model2.foo.keys())
```

title: Memory Usage From Schema Validators Is Fairly High, Even With Simple Models
body: ### Initial Checks - [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description (Related: #9982, except my models aren't complicated! they are simple) (Related: #7263, but I care about memory, not import time) Memory usage on Pydantic models with a non-trivial number of fields seems unreasonably high. This manifested in a 50 MB memory increase from us adding a few new models from an OpenAPI-generated spec (each with ~300-~400 fields). While debugging this I saw that the `SchemaValidator` object was taking a good amount of memory. While it's a Rust-side object from `pydantic-core`, my reproduction is in `pydantic` in a what I believe to be a simple case. With the reproduction script (it requires `memray` to do the memory analysis), I generated some classes with ~125 fields (all `str | None`s). In that case I found that: - `SchemaValidator` objects seem to be ~250kb each - `SchemaSerializer` objects seem to be ~140kb each With ~325 fields a piece, the Models each seem to take ~1.5 megs a piece (according to `memray`), with `SchemaValidator` and `SchemaSerializer` each taking ~650kb a piece Now looking into the code for those on the Rust-side, the main thing that stood out to me was the cloning of the `schema` and `config` objects (for example [here](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/blob/0cd11fe519df75db7878a80f0eb2686adeff2081/src/validators/mod.rs#L132)). Now I'm _fairly_ certain that this cloning is a deep copy of the schema, at least `objgraph` seems to think so. And these schema objects are fairly giant. My impression is that these schema objects are huge, and the comment seems to imply that holding onto them is only for implementing `__reduce__` with `cloudpickle`. Philosophical t

title: (Typed) dictionaries and `serialize_as_any` behavior
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Related: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/3427.

It seems to me that using `SerializeAsAny`/`serialize_as_any` means "treat the field annotation as `Any` when serializing a field value". However, setting `extra='allow'` is necessary to make this work, but I'm not sure this would be necessary?

```python
from typing import TypedDict

from pydantic import BaseModel, SerializeAsAny, with_config

# @with_config(extra='allow')
class Base(TypedDict):
    foo: str


# Equivalent to subclassing Base, as TDs are structural types
class Sub(TypedDict):
    foo: str
    bar: int


class Model(BaseModel):
    x: SerializeAsAny[Base]

m = Model(x=Sub(foo="hello", bar=123))

m.model_dump()
#> {'x': {'foo': 'hello'}}
```

If we uncomment the `with_config` on `Base`, it properly serializes as `Any`, but looks like it shouldn't be required?

cc @DouweM

```Python

```

```Text
2.11
```

mock.seal changes behavior of isinstance
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When mocking a `BaseClass` subclass via `unittest.mock.Mock`, using `unittest.mock.seal` on that mock changes its behavior w.r.t. `isinstance`.

The example code prints

```
Foo
    unsealed: True
    sealed: True
    sealed after isinstance: True

Bar
    unsealed: True
    sealed: False
    sealed after isinstance: True
```

So calling `isinstance` before `seal` is a workaround.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from unittest.mock import Mock, seal

class Foo:
    pass

class Bar(BaseModel):
    pass

for cls in [Foo, Bar]:
    print(cls.__name__)
    print("    unsealed:", isinstance(Mock(cls), cls))

    m = Mock(cls)
    seal(m)
    print("    sealed:", isinstance(m, cls))

    m = Mock(cls)
    isinstance(m, cls)
    seal(m)
    print("    sealed after isinstance:", isinstance(m, cls))

    print()
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.11.9
        pydantic-core version: 2.33.2
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.12.7 (main, Oct 11 2024, 12:45:52) [GCC 13.2.0]
                     platform: Linux-6.14.0-29-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
             related packages: typing_extensions-4.12.2 mypy-1.11.2
                       commit: unknown
```

title: Apply equivalent simplifications on annotations/core schemas
pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation on model_rebuild() when inheriting from another module
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Here is a minimal example

`a.py`

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Parent(BaseModel):
    x: "Member"

class Member(BaseModel):
    pass

class Child(Parent):
    pass

Parent.model_rebuild()
Child.model_rebuild()
```

`b.py`

```python
from a import Child

class Problem(Child):
    pass


Problem.model_rebuild()
```

This raises 

```
pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'Member' is not defined
```

Whereas I would expect it to be able to figure out `Member` is from the same module as `Child`.

Note that if you import `Member` in `b.py`, the error disappears

### Example Code

```Python
########### a.py ##############
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Parent(BaseModel):
    x: "Member"

class Member(BaseModel):
    pass

class Child(Parent):
    pass

Parent.model_rebuild()
Child.model_rebuild()


##################### b.py #####################

from a import Child

class Problem(Child):
    pass

Problem.model_rebuild() # pydantic.errors.PydanticUndefinedAnnotation: name 'Member' is not defined
```

title: Support RFC 9557
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Split from #9571

For `datetime` validation & serialization we should consider allowing an IANA timezone when provided in the form specified by [RFC 9557](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9557.html#name-examples).

This probably means using `ZoneInfo` objects for the tz.

This might need to be opt-in for serialization in V2 to avoid it being a breaking change. Probably this would be a good thing to be a default in V3 (or even not possible to disable).

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

title: Environment variable to show full input value in errors
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

See validation error like this, which raises a `ValidationError`:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError
import json

class SampleModel(BaseModel):
    id: int
    name: str

# Create a large but invalid JSON input (e.g., missing required fields, wrong types)
invalid_json = json.dumps({
    "id": 123 * 1e10,
    "data": ["a"] * 10000  # Large array to increase size
})

try:
    obj = SampleModel.model_validate_json(invalid_json)
except ValidationError as e:
    print("Validation error:", e)
```

The `ValidationError` has truncated input value to avoid bloating exception messages:

```
Validation error: 1 validation error for SampleModel
name
  Field required [type=missing, input_value={'id': 1230000000000.0, '...a', 'a', 'a', 'a', 'a']}, input_type=dict]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.11/v/missing
```

I propose we add an environment variable, `PYDANTIC_SHOW_FULL_ERRORS=1` (to be bikeshed) which stops the truncation, so the user can more easily debug.

title: regex patterns parsed differently when compiled (e.g. as StringContraints `pattern`) leading to wrong jsonschema pattern
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

jsonschema interprets restricting `"pattern"` as Javascript regular expression (Reference: https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-core#name-regular-expressions)

The `pattern` attribute of pydantic 2.11 StringConstraints accepts both compiled regular expressions and strings. (Ref: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/fields/#string-constraints )

However the behaviour is different (I assume one is compiled by python, while the other compiled by pydantic's rust core). This leads to a different behaviour and to wrong jsonschema files.

The example code below will output
```json
{
  "properties": {
    "matching_name3": {
      "pattern": "\\Aa{,3}\\Z",
      "title": "Matching Name3",
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "matching_name3"
  ],
  "title": "FooBar",
  "type": "object"
}
```

where `\A`, `{,3}` and `\Z` are invalid in Javascript's regex variant used by jsonschema. If someone uses the strings without previous compile (as commented out), pydantic-core seems to catch those cases.

It would be a good improvment to make the behaviour more consistent. For example trying to extract the re.Pattern.pattern and parse that (at least when writing a JSON) schema. 

To be really consistent with jsonschema, the differences from rust towards Javascript regexp would need to be checked. Maybe rust has only a subset, then it would be save.

The documentation should mention which regex engine will interpret the `pattern` constraint.

### Example Code

```Python
import json
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Annotated
import re

compiled_pattern = re.compile(r'\Aa{,3}\Z')

class FooBar(BaseModel):
    # regular expressions that will be rejected (as they are python only)
    # when they are parsed as string
    #matching_name : str = Field(pattern=r'^a{,3}$')
    #matching_name2 : str  = Field(pattern=r'\Anon\Z')

    # but accepted when speci

title: pydantic-settings - env_prefix not respected (No errors when non-prefix var is present)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When `env_prefix` is defined (say 'MY_APP'), I expected that for a specific variable, say `x`, if it appears in the `.env` file, it would trigger a validation error. However, currently it passes validation.

Is this by design? If so, why?

```Python
# .env file has `x=2` variable only

from pathlib import Path
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict

pth_env = Path("./.env")

class Settings(BaseSettings):

        env_file=pth_env,
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",

title: `after` validator run twice
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The following code produces two calls of the `after` validator for A, this seems like a bug.

### Example Code

```Python
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Self

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator

COUNTER = defaultdict(lambda: 0)


class A(BaseModel):
    x: int

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def running_validation(self) -> Self:
        COUNTER['A'] += 1
        return self


class B(BaseModel):
    a: A

    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def running_validation(self) -> Self:
        COUNTER['B'] += 1
        return self


B(a=A(x=2))
print(COUNTER)  # prints {'A': 2, 'B': 1}
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
Pydantic 2.11
```

title: `TypeAdapter.dump_json` slower than orjson seemingly due to number of arguments passed to pydantic-core
body:
I ran the following code (tested with Pydantic 2.11):

```python
from timeit import timeit

from orjson import dumps as to_json_orjson
from pydantic import TypeAdapter
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Example:
    b: str

ta = TypeAdapter(Example)
to_json_pydantic = ta.dump_json

e = Example(b='1q23')

print(to_json_orjson(e))
print(to_json_pydantic(e))

N = 1000000
pydantic = timeit('to_json_pydantic(e)', globals=globals(), number=N)
orjson = timeit('to_json_orjson(e)', globals=globals(), number=N)

print(f'Pydantic: {pydantic:.3f}s')
print(f'orjson: {orjson:.3f}s')
```

On my M1 mac, I find that `orjson` is about 2x faster, and running a profile the main cost in the Pydantic code seems to be inside PyO3's argument parsing.

I think it's probably just the sheer number of keyword arguments passed to `SchemaSerializer.to_json`. I wonder if this should be optimized in PyO3, pydantic, or both.

Aside, I am sure that I previously tested this same sample on a Linux desktop and found orjson to be 10x faster, but I need to re-check that result and understand if that was really the case.

title: PydanticSerializationError when json_schema_extra contains lambda functions in dict
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

**Description:**

When `json_schema_extra` is a dict containing callable values (like lambda functions), `model_json_schema()` fails with `PydanticSerializationError: Unable to serialize unknown type: <class 'function'>`.

**Impact:**

This breaks FastAPI applications that use such models - the automatic docs page (`/docs`) fails to render because FastAPI relies on `model_json_schema()` (I think) for OpenAPI generation. Users get an error instead of documentation.

**Expected Behavior:**

- `model_json_schema()` should work for all three cases
- Callable values in dicts should be handled the same way as callable functions passed directly to `json_schema_extra`

**Actual Behavior:**

- Case 1 (dict only) ✓ works
- Case 2 (callable function) ✓ works
- Case 3 (callable in dict) ✗ crashes with `PydanticSerializationError`

This inconsistency breaks schema generation for models that mix static and dynamic values in `json_schema_extra`.


### Example Code

```Python
"""Minimal reproduction: json_schema_extra behaviors with different types"""
import json
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get_openapi

# 1. Dict only (works fine)
class DictModel(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={"example": "test"})

app1 = FastAPI()
@app1.post("/test/")
def test_dict(data: DictModel): return data

# 2. Callable lambda (works fine)
class CallableModel(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra=lambda schema: schema.update({"example": "test"}))

app2 = FastAPI()
@app2.post("/test/")
def test_callable(data: CallableModel): return data

# 3. Dict with callable value (breaks)
class CallableInDictModel(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={"example": lambda: "test"})

app3 = FastAPI()
@app3.post("/test/")
def test_callable_in_dict(data: CallableInDictModel): return data

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_case

title: Partial update of nested model via model_copy doesn't work
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have nested model and i want to partial update it.
I saw issue with almost exact same title https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/7387, but issue wasn't fully resolved.
I saw issue with exact same problem https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/4177, but it was reproduced for pydantic v1, so issue was closed.

Firstly, I tried to use just model_copy. I got nested model as a dict object instead of BaseModel object.
Then, I tried to set flag `deep=True`, but nothing changed at all.
And finally, I tried to use `model_validate`, but obviously nested model has been fully rewritten. That's why i think https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/7387 wasn't fully resolved

So my question is why flag `deep=True` does nothing in that case? And is there a way other than write my own recursive update function?

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Foo(BaseModel):
    param1: int = 1
    param2: int = 2


class Bar(BaseModel):
    param3: int = 3
    param4: Foo = Foo()


bar = Bar()
bar.param4.param2 = 4
print("Bar", bar) # Bar(param3=3, param4=Foo(param1=1, param2=4))

update_dict = {"param3": 5, "param4": {"param1": 6}}
# As a result i want Bar(param3=5, param4=Foo(param1=6, param2=4)).

bar2 = bar.model_copy(update=update_dict)
print("Bar2", bar2, type(bar2.param4)) # I get param4 as a dict, not a Foo.

bar3 = bar.model_copy(update=update_dict, deep=True)
print("Bar3", bar3, type(bar3.param4)) # I get param4 as a dict, not a Foo.

updated_model = bar.model_copy(update=update_dict).model_dump()
# I'll get same result if use just dict.
# updated_model = bar.model_dump()
# updated_model.update(update_dict)
bar4 = Bar.model_validate(updated_model)
print("Bar4", bar4, bar4.param4.param2) # I get param2 reset to default value 2, but i want 4.
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

Version              pydantic version: 2.11.7
        pydantic-core ve

title: model_validate_json won't do coercions in strict mode if there is a `@model_validator`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/json/#json-parsing demonstrates that `model_validate_json` will do type coercion (like str to date, list to tuple) even in strict mode. 
This stops working if there is a `@model_validator` on the class (of any mode).

Context: We are building a pydantic-integrated ORM for the Gel database (https://github.com/geldata/gel-python) and I was hoping to use `@model_validator(mode='wrap')` to discriminate on a type name field so we can dispatch to model_validate on subtypes (https://github.com/geldata/gel-python/issues/755#issuecomment-3268388865). The more fundamental problem I ran into, though, is that I couldn't find a good way to call a subtype's validate function in JSON mode, since `model_validate_json` only takes encoded JSON but we already have it decoded. While trying to test the (horrible) strategy of `json.dumps`ing `data` when in `json` mode and then calling `model_validate_json`, I ran into this issue where recursing via `handler` still breaks.

### Example Code

```Python
from datetime import date

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, ValidationError, model_validator
from typing import Any

class Event(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)

    when: date
    where: tuple[int, int]

    @model_validator(mode='wrap')
    @classmethod
    def noop_wrapper(cls, data, handler):
        return handler(data)

    # # Also breaks things!
    # @model_validator(mode='after')
    # @classmethod
    # def noop_after(cls, data: Any) -> Any:
    #     return data

    # # Also breaks things!
    # @model_validator(mode='before')
    # @classmethod
    # def noop_before(cls, data: Any) -> Any:
    #     return data


json_data = '{"when": "1987-01-28", "where": [51, -1]}'
print(Event.model_validate_json(json_data))
#> when=datetime.date(1987, 1, 28) where=(51, -1)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version

title: Tell Pydantic to not generate JSON schema with $ref $defs
Support for serialising to NDJson (Newline delimited JSON / Jsonlines)
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Newline delimited JSON (where the JSON is serialised with no actual newlines), and each record is separated by a newline is very handy for streaming data.

https://database.guide/what-is-ndjson/

It would be good if the json optionally output in NDJSon format.



### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [x] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

title: v2.12 no longer supports ABC Union
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The following code no longer works in `2.12.0a1`

For real world use case see https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/pull/6889#issuecomment-3261516283

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class ABC_1(BaseModel, ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def do_something(self):
        pass

class ABC_2(BaseModel, ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def do_something_else(self):
        pass

class A1(ABC_1):
    def do_something(self):
        print("Doing something in A1")

class A2(ABC_2):
    def do_something_else(self):
        print("Doing something else in A2")

class X(BaseModel):
    x: ABC_1 | ABC_2

a1 = A1()
a2 = A2()

x1 = X(x=a1)
x2 = X(x=a2)

title: Feature: Add Workflow for Manual Version Bumps
body:
You should add a GitHub Actions workflow that allows you to **manually trigger version bumps**. This will help you keep your version and changelog consistent in the project.  

**Proposed Workflow**  
```yaml
name: Version Bump

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      release_type:
        description: 'Select version bump type'
        required: true
        default: 'patch'
        type: choice
        options:
          - patch
          - minor
          - major

jobs:
  bump:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Universal Version Bump
        uses: taj54/universal-version-bump@v0.12.0
        with:
          release_type: ${{ inputs.release_type }}
          git_tag: false
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
```

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] You can see the workflow in the **Actions** tab.  
- [ ] You can choose bump type (`patch`, `minor`, `major`).  
- [ ] The workflow bumps the version .  
- [ ] (Optional) You can extend this workflow later to include tagging + release creation.

title: `Mypy` fails to recognize names without default while using reference to `ConfigDict`
body: ### Initial Checks - [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description In my project we have many Pydantic `BaseModel`s, and they use the same setup for a `ConfigDict`. Therefore, we created one default `ConfigDict` and we use references in our models. Also, they represent responses from API calls, and this why we use a lot of aliases. Our project is type checked using `mypy`. Here is the thing, `mypy` works fine when specifying the `ConfigDict` directly in a model, but fails to recognize names instead of aliases when using the reference. This only happens when a `Field` doesn't have a default value. As you can see in the screnshot below, `mypy` only complains about the names `age` and `first_name` in `Model2`. Versions: `pydantic`: 2.11.7 `mypy`: 1.17.1 `python`: 3.13.2 ### Example Code ```Python from datetime import datetime from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field class Model(BaseModel): model_config = ConfigDict(validate_by_name=True) age: int = Field(..., ge=0, alias="UserAge") first_name: str = Field(..., max_length=100, alias="UserFirstName") last_name: str | None = Field(None, max_length=100, alias="UserLastName") signup_ts: datetime | None = Field(None, alias="UserSignupTs") list_of_ints: list[int] = Field(default_factory=list, alias="UserListOfInts") m = Model( age=42, first_name="John", last_name="Doe", signup_ts=datetime.now(), list_of_ints=[1, 2, 3], ) MODEL_CONFIG_REFERENCE = ConfigDict(validate_by_name=True) class Model2(BaseModel): model_config = MODEL_CONFIG_REFERENCE age: int = Field(..., ge=0, alias="UserAge") first_name: str = Field(..., max_length=100, alias="UserFirstName") last_name: str | None = Field(None, max_length=100, alias="UserLastName") signup_ts: datetime | None = Field(None, alias="UserSignupTs") lis

title: `computed_field`+`cached_property` not working with `validate_assignment`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Triggering a validation (triggered by an assignement) while fetching a computed field defeats `cached_property` logic.

This is because the schema validator is creating a new instance of `__dict__`.

### Example Code

```Python
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = [
#     "pydantic",
# ]
# ///
"""
Test showing a bug when using pydantic with cached_property.

# 1. Pydantic is creating a new dict after a validation following an assignement.
# 2. functools cached_property uses the instance dict to store cached values. But between the time it assigns the cache and uses it, that dict may have changed (see point 1). https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.13/Lib/functools.py#L1010
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import random
from functools import cached_property

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, computed_field


class A(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(validate_assignment=True)  # Set to False to avoid the bug.

    b: int = 0

    @computed_field
    @cached_property
    def random_number(self) -> int:
        self.b = 123
        return random.randint(0, 1_000)


a = A()
print(a.random_number)
print(a.random_number)
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

pydantic version: 2.11.7
        pydantic-core version: 2.33.2

title: Missing ValidationError for list[tuple(A, B)] type

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I'm not sure what the correct behavior should be, but I found a case where `list[Union[...]]` was mistakingly changed to `list[tuple[...]]`  and accepted by pydantic validation. 
Then a json schema was generated out of this model and everything worked end to end producing unexpected results.

I know this could have been caught by mypy, but maybe pydantic should handle it?

### Example Code

```Python

from pydantic import BaseModel

class ActionA(BaseModel):
    action_a_param: str

class ActionB(BaseModel):
    action_b_param: int

ActionTypeTuple = (ActionA, ActionB)
ActionTypeUnion = Union[ActionA, ActionB]

class ResponseWithTuple(BaseModel):
    actions: list[ActionTypeTuple]

class ResponseWithUnion(BaseModel):
    actions: list[ActionTypeUnion]


data = {
    "actions": [
        {"action_a_param": "hello"},
        {"action_a_param": "world"}
    ]
}


validated_tuple = ResponseWithTuple.model_validate(data)
validated_union = ResponseWithUnion.model_validate(data)


schema_tuple = ResponseWithTuple.model_json_schema()
schema_union = ResponseWithUnion.model_json_schema()

print(schema_union)

title: Attribute is not properly carried over when using nested model
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When an instance of a Pydantic model is passed as a field to another Pydantic model, the `model_validator` is triggered again for the nested model, causing the attribute to be reset to its initial value as defined within the validator. It seems that Pydantic re-validates the input object instead of just accepting the already-initialized model instance. This behavior is unexpected, as I would anticipate the entire object, including its attributes, to be passed through without re-initialization.

[Similar discussion](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/7314#discussion-5582861)

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validator


class MyData(BaseModel):
    file: str

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def init_data(self):
        # init data here, for example, read from self.file
        # here I assume that the initial data is {"a": 1}
        self._data = {"a": 1}
        return self

    @property
    def data(self):
        return self._data

    @data.setter
    def data(self, value):
        self._data = value


class DataAnalyzer(BaseModel):
    mdata: MyData


if __name__ == "__main__":
    mdata = MyData(file="")  # file is a dummy var her, no actual reading
    mdata.data["b"] = 2  # update the data
    print(mdata.data)  # {'a': 1, 'b': 2}, data is changed
    analyzer = DataAnalyzer(mdata=mdata)
    print(analyzer.mdata.data)  # {'a': 1}, data isn't carried over
    print(mdata.data)  # {'a': 1}, data is changed back, weird
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.11.7
pydantic-core version: 2.33.2
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
python version: 3.13.5 (main, Jul 11 2025, 22:43:46) [Clang 20.1.4 ]
platform: Linux-6.1.0-37-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.35

### Description

If a child class uses the `@model_serializer` decorator, this decorator will also be called when siblings are serialized. 

In my example I have a base class `Report` that contain a list of `Step` objects. 
`Step` is itself a pydantic model, and it has two child classes: `SpecialStep` and `AnotherStep`.
`SpecialStep` is quirky and needs a custom serialization. 

When you run the example code, you get the output below. As you can see, `SpecialStep.serialize()` is called also when `AnotherStep` is being serialized. Even though `serialize()` is a function on `SpecialStep`.

I would expect this function to only run when `SpecialStep` is being serialized. Is this a bug, or am I missing something?

A workaround is to add a type check before the serializer is being run. 

```python
if not isinstance(self, SpecialStep):
            serializer(self)
```

}

title: Change `__rich_repr__` method to ignore defaults
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Pydantic [implements](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/ad395e9b18a51375f115f68ba8ac0e71c458d348/pydantic/_internal/_repr.py#L76) the `__rich_repr__` protocol to pretty print pydantic models using [Rich](https://rich.readthedocs.io/en/stable/pretty.html#rich-repr-protocol).

When a large pydantic object is pretty-printed this way, it can take a lot of time and space to be printed. From the pydantic source code, I can see that every single field is returned, but that might be too much information.

The `__rich_repr__` protocol supports printing values only if they are not the field default value:

 > Each tuple specifies an element in the output.
 > `yield value` will generate a positional argument.
 > `yield name, value` will generate a keyword argument.
> `yield name, value, default` will generate a keyword argument if value is not equal to default.

Could pydantic implement that behavior where only fields not set to their default value are printed?

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)

title: Overly generic serialization schema with PlainSerializer on Generic Alias
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Hello,

I am not sure if this is a bug, but it puzzled me for a bit, so I thought of raising it just in case.

I am trying to build something similar to the `Secret` generic, but with some custom logic behind (that will be omitted here, so it will look a lot like `Secret`), that I'll call `Hidden`. And I was hopping to be able to drop in that generic as a field annotation into existing BaseModels, without having to create dedicated types (eg, use `field: Hidden[str]` and not `HiddenStr = Hidden[str]` then `field: HiddenStr`)

Doing so works well during the `model_dump()`, so I assumed I had it right. However, when inspecting the serialization JSON Schema, I realized that all my hidden fields where overly generic. By that, I mean that every `Hidden[T]` was resolved as being anything allowed for `T`, not just the type provided.

I am guessing that the type var `T` in the PlainSerializer is being resolved when declaring `Hidden`, before getting to the model declaration, and thus cannot be specialized according to the field annotations. But I was surprised not to see anything similar in the doc, and I couldn't find a straightforward workaround.

I managed to get the expected behavior by annotating `Hidden` with a custom class, implementing `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` directly. But that's a lot of added complexity.

### Example Code

```Python
import json
from datetime import date
from typing import Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel, PlainSerializer


def _hidder[T: str | date | int](value: T) -> T:
    match value:
        case str():
            return "*" * len(value)
        case int():
            return "1" * len(str(value))
        case date():
            return date(1111, 11, 11)


type Hidden[T: str | date | int] = Annotated[T, PlainSerializer(_hidder, T)]


class TestModel(BaseModel):
    bar: Hidden[str]
    baz: Hidden[date]
    regular: str
    bar_dict: dict[Hidden[

title: Replace use of deprecated typing.Pattern
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

I suggest changing the type of pattern in `fields.py` generally to `str | re.Pattern[str] | None` from the current `str | typing.Pattern[str] | None`. `typing.Pattern` is deprecated with replacement `re.Pattern[str]` since Python 3.9, which is the minimum version Pydantic V2 supports, so `re.Pattern[str]` certainly is available. This change should not change the behaviour of the library.

Other occurrences of `typing.Pattern` in the code might also be considered for change, but some might relate to user uses of it (I am not familiar enough with the code to judge this) and thus change the behaviour of the library, therefore I do not suggest a general change.

### Affected Components

title: Order of types in Union type important with default_factory side effects and when from_attributes=True
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

The below results in an error. if you change `d: X | Y` to `d: Y | X` it works as expected. 

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict

class X(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
    a: int
    b: int
    c: int = Field(default_factory=lambda: int(1/0))


class Y(BaseModel):
    a: int


class Z(BaseModel):
    d: X | Y 


Z(d=Y(a=100))


```
My understanding is that pydantic is trying to populate/validate `Y(a=100)` as `X` using it's attributes, filling in the gaps by calling the default factory. In my head a concrete `Y` instance should just be interpreted as the acceptable `Y` type over the potential coercion to `X`

title: Union anyOf instead of oneOf
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

We are using pydantic (via FastAPI) to generate interface descriptions that adhere the openapi specification.
If we use Union for a type e.g. float | None pydantic produces _anyOf_ instead of _oneOf_.
Our customer is not happy with this as he says his automatic code generation can't handle this and expects a _oneOf_ in that case.
I had a similar problem with complex data types which was solved by using Annotated and a bugfix.
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/4125


### Example Code

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Model(BaseModel):

title: Custom and built-in `int` types generate different JSON schema.
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I have a custom `int` type loosely based on [Concepts: Handling third-party types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/types/#handling-third-party-types), which I'm combining with an int constraint. I'd expect this to generate the same JSON schema as a regular `int` with the same constraints, but it does not, as shown below.

Specifically, the `Field` constraints like `ge` and `le` are normally added as `minimum` and `maximum` respectively, except for the custom `int` where they are added as-is.

I've looked through various docs and posts, but it's still unclear to me whether this is an actual bug, or there are just more steps needed in e.g. `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` to invoke the missing transformations.
But my understanding is that all of that happens later on, leading me to suspect a bug.

I'm also not certain whether both forms are in fact valid schema, but the fact that it is inconsistent is currently causing issues in tooling on our end.

Model JSON schema:

```json
{
  "properties": {
    "regular_int": {
      "maximum": 13,
      "minimum": 1,
      "title": "Regular Int",
      "type": "integer"
    },
    "model_id": {
      "ge": 1,
      "le": 13,
      "title": "Model Id",
      "type": "integer"
    }

### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently, when both the serialization and validation JSON schemas for a Model is generated (and they differ), Pydantic automatically appends the "-Input" and "-Output" suffix to the model name to differentiate them. It would be great for us if we can change this suffix via model config or maybe as argument to the `model_json_schema` function.

I do understand that you can subclass the `GenerateJsonSchema` class and override this specific function:
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/f7a9b73517afecf25bf898e3b5f591dffe669778/pydantic/json_schema.py#L2132-L2178

...which is exactly what I tried:

```python
import re

from pydantic.json_schema import (
    CoreModeRef,
    DefsRef,
    GenerateJsonSchema,
    JsonSchemaMode,
)

# ========== START OF MODIFICATION ========== #
_MODE_TITLE_MAPPING: dict[JsonSchemaMode, str] = {
    "validation": "Input",
    "serialization": "",  # do not give any suffix for serialization
}
# ========== END OF MODIFICATION ========== #


class CustomGenerateJsonSchema(GenerateJsonSchema):
    def get_defs_ref(self, core_mode_ref: CoreModeRef) -> DefsRef:
        """Override this method to change the way that definitions keys are generated from a core reference.

        Args:
            core_mode_ref: The core reference.

        Returns:
            The definitions key.
        """
        # Split the core ref into "components"; generic origins and arguments are each separate components
        core_ref, mode = core_mode_ref
        components = re.split(r"([\][,])", core_ref)
        # Remove IDs from each component
        components = [x.rsplit(":", 1)[0] for x in components]
        core_ref_no_id = "".join(components)
        # Remove everything before the last period from each "component"

title: Option to raise raw exceptions in validators instead of always wrapping into `ValidationError`
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Currently, Pydantic intercepts some errors raised in validators and automatically wraps them into a `ValidationError`.
Sometimes in complex validators, it could be convenient to debug the inner context, and for instance open a debugger at the original exception and understand what is going on.
For this, it would be nice to have a dedicated option to pass in `model_validate`.

A concrete use case: I work on several libraries that implement the SCIM protocol defined in RFC7643 and RFC7644. [scim2-models](https://github.com/python-scim/scim2-models) provides Pydantic models, and validation according to the specs, that are used to build SCIM servers. [scim2-tester](https://github.com/python-scim/scim2-tester) provides compliance check scenarios for SCIM servers, so developers can test if the code they build to articulate scim2-models, their web framework, and their database system, is doing well.

The regular usage for scim2-models is to be used in servers, and here the regular Pydantic validation error messages are what is needed. However, when using scim2-tester to test my own server, I found that I could have used a real debugger context opened inside the Pydantic validators. This way I could have more details than provided by the ValidationError messages.

What do you think?

title: Unexpected behavior when parsing large numbers in JSON
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I've noticed some behavior that seems unexpected when dealing with representations of large `Number` values in JSON. This seems like it might be an issue with `pydantic_core`, so I'm happy to re-file this over there if that's preferable. When working with a schema for matching float values, a validation error gets raised that wouldn't expect for certain large inputs when validating JSON:

1. `100000000000000000` — this is fine
2. `1000000000000000000` — this causes a validation error
2. `1000000000000000000.0` — this is fine

There's nothing in the JSON spec that leads me to think that there should be a difference in parsing a 57-bit int vs a 60-bit one (or really in parsing a number of any size). The target environment determines the boundaries of what can be interpreted, so there are always practical limits in the size of numbers you can work with, but that doesn't seem to be what's going on here either, given that we can work with `1000000000000000000.0` just fine. 



### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic_core import SchemaValidator, core_schema

sv = SchemaValidator(core_schema.float_schema())

# this works fine and returns a Python float: 1e+17
sv.validate_json(str(int(1e17)))

# this does not work, and raises: ValidationError: 1 validation error for float
#   Input should be a valid number [type=float_type, input_value=1000000000000000000, input_type=int]
#     For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.11/v/float_type
sv.validate_json(str(int(1e18)))

# this works fine and returns a Python float: 1e+18
sv.validate_json(str(int(1e18)) + ".0")

# this seems to be JSON-specific, because this also works fine.
sv.validate_python(str(int(1e18)))
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.11.4
pydantic-core version: 2.33.2
pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
python version: 3.11.13 (main, Jun  4 2025, 19:34:23) [GCC 12.2.0]

title: Base64 model export
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

Hi,

Currently, pydantic supports exporting a dict with `model_dump` and a JSON-encoded string version of it with `model_dump_json`, as described in https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#modelmodel_dump

I think it would be nice to also cover the export of the model to base64 / base64 string, could be named `model_dump_base64` /  `model_dump_json_base64`? Idea being that pydantic would take care of the bytes encoding / decoding, and base64 encoding. Less code on user side, and better performance!

### Affected Components

title: MyPy read-only errors when mutating a model that has been "un-frozen"
body:
### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

When writing a simple pytest to test some of my model validation configurations I noticed that I was getting a mypy error that was somewhat unexpected.

My setup was as follows:

1. Create a frozen model where I have some fields with explicit constraints defined
2. Create a pytest module where I first construct the model with data honoring the constraints to ensure the happy path is OK
3. Unfreeze the model (`model_config["frozen"] = False`) and enable assignment validation (`model_config["validate_assignment"] = True`), then assert I get a `ValidationError` when I assign a value outside my expected range.

This seems to work well to actually perform the test I intended, but it results in mypy throwing an error of the form: error: `Property "constrained_int" defined in "TestModel" is read-only  [misc]`.

For now I have been working around this by using `# type: ignore[misc]` on the lines where I perform the assignment.

It is not entirely surprising to me that this is flagged by mypy but figured I'd run it by you all to see if this is something that should be fixed.

Reproduction code is provided.

### Example Code

```Python
import pytest
import pydantic


# Model defined this way for production use.
class TestModel(pydantic.BaseModel):
    model_config = pydantic.ConfigDict(frozen=True)
    constrainted_int: int = pydantic.Field(gt=1, lt=3)


# In some test code, do the following.

# Run happy path test.
test_model = TestModel(constrainted_int=2)

# Run sad path test.
test_model.model_config["frozen"] = False
test_model.model_config["validate_assignment"] = True

with pytest.raises(pydantic.ValidationError):
    # The following line results in a mypy error.
    # error: Property "constrained_int" defined in "TestModel" is read-only  [misc]
    test_model.constrainted_int = 5
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

```Text
pydantic version: 2.11.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.33.2

title: [BUG] Live outputs different amounts of newlines depending on if console is a terminal
body:
- [x] I've checked [docs](https://rich.readthedocs.io/en/latest/introduction.html) and [closed issues](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for possible solutions.
- [x] I can't find my issue in the [FAQ](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/blob/master/FAQ.md).

**Describe the bug**

The Live class of Rich emits a newline on stopping but only if its console is connected to a terminal.

I use greenbone-feed-sync that is built with Rich and it makes the output captured by journald (when run as a service) hard to read.

I prepared a branch that fixes this: https://github.com/qha/rich/tree/fix-live-termination

Provide a minimal code example that demonstrates the issue if you can.
```python
from time import sleep

from rich.console import Console
from rich.live import Live
from rich.spinner import Spinner as RichSpinner


class Spinner:
    def __init__(self, console: Console, status: str) -> None:
        self.console = console
        self._spinner = RichSpinner(
            "dots", text=status, style="status.spinner", speed=1.0
        )
        self._live = Live(
            self._spinner,
            console=console,
            refresh_per_second=12.5,
            transient=False,
        )

    def __enter__(self) -> "Spinner":
        self._live.start()
        return self

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self._live.stop()


def showprogress(console):
    with Spinner(console,
                 "Downloading content from url"
                 " to destination"):
        sleep(1)
    console.print("Moving on to other work")


def test(force_terminal):
    console = Console(force_terminal=force_terminal)
    showprogress(console)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test(None)
    test(False)
    test(True)
```

Please ensure that your example runs without modification (don't forget imports).

If the issue is visual in nature, consider posting a screenshot.

title: [BUG] attribute names `awehoi234_wdfjwljet234_234wdfoijsdfmmnxpi492` and `_fields` make unrelated modules hard to debug
body:
- [x] I've checked [docs](https://rich.readthedocs.io/en/latest/introduction.html) and [closed issues](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for possible solutions.
- [x] I can't find my issue in the [FAQ](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/blob/master/FAQ.md).

**Describe the bug**

I have some code that I was debugging and was confused by the appearance of attributes called `awehoi234_wdfjwljet234_234wdfoijsdfmmnxpi492` and `_fields`. This was pretty hard to debug. It turned out that these are both added by `rich`.

* `awehoi234_wdfjwljet234_234wdfoijsdfmmnxpi492` is used to test objects for [auto-vivifying attributes](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/blob/9d8f9a372cc5916fd4781fec207ced7ddac2f08f/rich/pretty.py#L649). If the test succeeds (e.g., the [`Box`](https://github.com/cdgriffith/Box) class), the new attribute is left on the object.

* `_fields` is used to test if an object [appears to be a `namedtuple`](https://github.com/Textualize/rich/blob/9d8f9a372cc5916fd4781fec207ced7ddac2f08f/rich/pretty.py#L573). The comment asserts that “[i]t is possible to craft an object that passes this check and isn't a namedtuple, but there is only a minuscule chance of this happening unintentionally.” I don’t think that’s true; any object that auto-vivifies attributes will pass the check.

In my view, a library designed for debugging output (which `rich` is quite good at) should do its best to not modify the objects it is displaying, and if it does modify them, to be as obvious as practical about where the modifications came from.

I would suggest:

1. Because the sentinel `awehoi234_wdfjwljet234_234wdfoijsdfmmnxpi492` is controlled by `rich`, change it to something that makes its provenance clear, say `_rich_fake_attribute_test`.

2. Remove the sentinel attribute if it’s successfully created, e.g. with `delattr()`.

3. Test for the presence of `_fields` in a way that avoids false positives on auto-vivifying objects, e.g. with `__getattribute_

title: (perf) Syntax word wrap without line numbers repeatedly renders each source line
body:
`Syntax._get_syntax()` currently calls `console.render_lines(...)` once per highlighted source line when `word_wrap=True`, even when `line_numbers=False`.

For syntax blocks without line numbers, continuation prefixes are not needed, so the selected highlighted lines can be joined and rendered/wrapped in one batch. This avoids repeatedly entering the generic console render pipeline.

title: [BUG] Broken image links in all README files
body:
## Broken README image links

Several README files use image URLs pointing to:

`https://github.com/textualize/rich/raw/master/imgs/...`

These images no longer render correctly in some contexts.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open one of the localized README files, for example `README.cn.md`.
2. Look at the screenshots/logo sections.
3. The images referenced through `github.com/textualize/rich/raw/master/imgs/...` appear broken.

## Expected behavior

README images should render reliably.

## Suggested fix

Use local relative paths instead:

![Logo](imgs/logo.svg)
![Features](imgs/features.png)

instead of:

![Logo](https://github.com/textualize/rich/raw/master/imgs/logo.svg)

This works because the README files and `imgs/` are both at the repository root, `imgs/...` resolves correctly everywhere the repo is viewed, including forks and local clones.

## Scope

This affects the root README files, including the translated READMEs.
